AI.tldr: VL-MemKnG is a hybrid memory framework for navigation-related video QA that combines a spatio-temporal knowledge graph with segment-level contextual memory, achieving significant improvements (Top-1 retrieval from 58% to 67%, Recall@1 from 34.50% to 40.55%) over strong baselines like Gemini 2.5 Pro on the new WalkieKnowledgeT+ benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing long-context VLMs are computationally expensive for long egocentric videos and inefficient for repeated querying, while pure graph-based approaches like VL-KnG underrepresent temporal continuity and contextual cues.
AI.method: VL-MemKnG extends VL-KnG by adding a spatio-temporal knowledge graph (for structured relations and long-range object associations) plus persistent segment-level contextual memory (for broader temporal context), with a hybrid retrieval-and-reasoning module that jointly operates over both memory types.
AI.result: On WalkieKnowledgeT+, VL-MemKnG improves Top-1 retrieval accuracy from 58% to 67% and Recall@1 from 34.50% to 40.55%, outperforming Gemini 2.5 Pro and Qwen 3.5+, with greatest gains on temporal-global and temporally scattered aggregation questions.
AI.conclusion: Combining structured relational memory with segment-level contextual memory yields better evidence grounding and temporal reasoning for long-horizon navigation video QA, while maintaining efficient query-time inference.

title: Landsat-Sentinel-2 Algal Bloom Mapping Using Vision Transformers: Model Description, Implementation, and Examples
summary: Coastal algal bloom monitoring requires frequent, spatially detailed, and globally consistent observations, provided by Landsat-8/9 and Sentinel-2 A/B/C. Together, these missions offer over a decade of medium-resolution multispectral imagery with near-global coverage every 2-3 days, enabling the detection of fragmented bloom structures not resolvable by coarse ocean-color sensors. However, their use in aquatic environments remains challenging due to limited spectral coverage and a lack of harmonized reflectance products. As an alternative to traditional bio-optical methods, deep learning-based image classification offers a data-driven approach that can overcome many of these limitations. This study presents the first successful implementation of vision transformer-based coastal algal bloom mapping using 30-m Landsat-Sentinel-2 images. A globally distributed bloom patch dataset was generated across bloom-prone coastal hotspots worldwide. Four transformer-based architectures were compared against a standard convolutional baseline for fine-scale bloom detection, and assessed under different optical water types and atmospheric and surface conditions. All deep learning models showed strong capabilities in detecting floating bloom areas, with omission and commission errors of 8-65%. Under cloud and glint stress in a time series, the Swin Transformer outperformed traditional spectral-index approaches, which produced widespread false positives, effectively avoiding cloud- and glint-affected pixels. Comparisons with MODIS-derived products further highlighted the benefits of higher spatial resolution in detecting fragmented and irregularly affected blooms. Our findings support deep learning as a reliable tool for medium-resolution, consistent monitoring of floating algal blooms in dynamic coastal environments.

AI.tldr: This study implements vision transformer-based deep learning for coastal algal bloom mapping using Landsat-8/9 and Sentinel-2 imagery. The Swin Transformer outperformed traditional spectral-index methods, effectively avoiding cloud and glint false positives, demonstrating deep learning as a reliable tool for medium-resolution bloom monitoring.
AI.motivation: Coastal algal bloom monitoring requires frequent, spatially detailed, and globally consistent observations. Traditional bio-optical methods face challenges due to limited spectral coverage and lack of harmonized reflectance products from Landsat-8/9 and Sentinel-2 missions.
AI.method: Created a globally distributed bloom patch dataset from bloom-prone coastal hotspots. Compared four transformer-based architectures against a standard convolutional baseline for fine-scale bloom detection, assessed under different optical water types, atmospheric, and surface conditions.
AI.result: All deep learning models showed strong bloom detection capabilities with omission/commission errors of 8-65%. Swin Transformer outperformed traditional spectral-index approaches under cloud and glint stress, avoiding false positives. Higher spatial resolution (30-m) proved beneficial for detecting fragmented bloom structures vs. MODIS.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning, particularly vision transformers, is a reliable tool for medium-resolution, consistent monitoring of floating algal blooms in dynamic coastal environments.

title: Models Take Notes at Prefill: KV Cache Can Be Editable and Composable
summary: Prefix caching reuses prefill only across an exactly shared prefix, so one changed field invalidates the entire downstream cache. Yet overwriting the field's own key/value vectors and reusing the rest leaves the model acting on the old value. The reason, established causally across four model families: at prefill the model has already written the field-conditioned conclusion onto downstream notes; the field's own key/value drives under 1% of the decision. Read as a notebook of memoized conclusions, two capabilities follow. (1) It is editable. A salient erratum amends the notes; and with chain-of-thought, editing the field alone recovers the decision (1.00 at 8B, ~1% compute), while without CoT it is ignored. (2) It is composable. The notes are position-portable, so a precompiled skill can be RoPE-repositioned and spliced into any context, indistinguishable from full recompute (logit cosine 0.90-0.999, twelve models) at O(L) rather than O(L^2) time-to-first-token. A unified edit+compose agent stays decision-identical to recompute at up to 14.9x lower latency. The approach applies to any per-token attention KV cache, validated across scale, quantization, Mixture-of-Experts, and multimodal caches, and extends to several attention variants through small adapters. Because the erratum is append-only, it composes with production prefix caching: in an online vLLM benchmark it keeps the prefix cache-aligned (98.5% hit-rate), cutting p90 time-to-first-token by 53-398x.

AI.tldr: Prefix caching is limited by exact-match requirements. This paper shows that a field's own KV vectors drive <1% of decisions; most reasoning is written onto "notebook" downstream tokens. This enables (1) editing a field by appending an erratum (amending downstream notes) and (2) composability via RoPE-repositioned skill splicing. A unified edit+compose agent matches recompute with up to 14.9x lower latency, maintains 98.5% prefix cache hit-rate, and cuts p90 TTFT by 53-398x.
AI.motivation: Prefix caching is brittle: changing one field invalidates the entire downstream cache. The paper aims to understand why this happens and find a more efficient way to handle field edits without full recompute.
AI.method: Causal analysis across four model families reveals that a field's own KV vectors drive <1% of the decision; the model writes field-conditioned conclusions onto downstream "notebook" tokens at prefill. Based on this, two capabilities are derived: (1) editing via appending an erratum that amends the notes (works with CoT), (2) composability via RoPE-repositioning precompiled skill vectors for splicing into any context.
AI.result: Editing the field alone with CoT recovers the decision (1.00 at 8B, ~1% compute). Composition yields logit cosine 0.90-0.999 across twelve models at O(L) vs O(L^2). Unified edit+compose agent achieves up to 14.9x lower latency. In vLLM benchmark, maintains 98.5% prefix cache hit-rate, cutting p90 TTFT by 53-398x.
AI.conclusion: The approach applies to any per-token attention KV cache, validated across scale, quantization, MoE, multimodal caches, and extends to several attention variants. Because the erratum is append-only, it composes with production prefix caching effectively.

title: Skill-Constrained Model Predictive Control for Resilient Manufacturing Supply Chains
summary: In skill-constrained production-inventory systems, the qualified human capacity available tomorrow depends on training decisions made today: production requires certified workers, certifications decay unless maintained, and training consumes the same scarce worker hours that production needs now. We study a closed-loop skill-constrained model predictive controller that, at every shift, solves a finite-horizon mixed-integer program over production, inventory, backlog, and training, with binary predicted certification, hard production eligibility, and an interpretable terminal value that prices certified-capacity gaps at the horizon boundary; only the first-period action is applied before replanning. On synthetic, seed-controlled SkillChain-Gym scenarios - announced and surprise new-skill shocks, demand shocks, absenteeism, forecast- and availability-quality modes, capacity-boundary and training-rate sweeps, and negative controls - we evaluate the controller against production-only and maintenance-only ablations, static cross-training insurance plans, and a strong reactive heuristic, under an ex-ante locked configuration and paired statistics. The result is regime dependence, not superiority: no policy class dominates. Predictive control helps when skill or labor bottlenecks are forecastable early enough for training to complete; lean static insurance remains hard to beat under surprise shocks, near the demand-capacity boundary, and wherever pre-shock slack makes insurance cheap. Attribution ablations separate certification maintenance, re-acquisition of lapsed certifications, and greenfield skill acquisition. Forecastability, not adaptivity per se, decides when predictive control pays.

AI.tldr: This paper studies a closed-loop model predictive controller for skill-constrained production-inventory systems, finding that no policy class universally dominates; predictive control helps when bottlenecks are forecastable, while static insurance is better under surprise shocks and near capacity boundaries.
AI.motivation: To understand when and why predictive control outperforms simpler heuristic or static policies in skill-constrained production-inventory systems, where training decisions today affect future certified workforce availability.
AI.method: A model predictive controller solving finite-horizon mixed-integer programs over production, inventory, backlog, and training decisions, evaluated on synthetic SkillChain-Gym scenarios against ablations and heuristics under various shock types and conditions.
AI.result: No policy class dominates; regime dependence observed. Predictive control excels when skill bottlenecks are forecastable early enough, but lean static insurance is hard to beat under surprise shocks, near demand-capacity boundaries, and when pre-shock slack makes insurance cheap.
AI.conclusion: Forecastability, not adaptivity per se, determines when predictive control provides value in skill-constrained systems.

title: Fine-UNETR for PSMA PET/CT Lesion Segmentation: Automated Tumor Quantification and Overall Survival Stratification in Prostate Cancer
summary: Introduction: To develop and evaluate Fine-UNETR, a Vision Transformer-based architecture for automated segmentation of PSMA-avid lesions on whole-body PET/CT, and to assess clinical utility of AI-derived tumor burden biomarkers for overall survival stratification in radioligand therapy. Methods: In this retrospective study, 373 PSMA PET/CT scans (mean age, 71+-8 years) from patients with prostate cancer were analyzed. Fine-UNETR, a modified UNETR with 8x8x8 voxel patch embedding and axial sliding window training, was trained on 299 scans and validated on 74 scans. Overall survival stratification was assessed in an independent cohort of 67 pre-radioligand therapy patients using Kaplan-Meier analysis and log-rank testing. External validation was performed on 192 cases from the AutoPET IV PSMA PET/CT dataset. Results: Fine-UNETR achieved a Dice similarity coefficient (DSC) of 66.63%, sensitivity of 70.27%, precision of 67.77%, and a lesion detection rate of 79.53% (96.05% for lesions with SUVmax >= 5). On the external validation dataset, the model achieved a DSC of 44.11% and a lesion detection rate of 87.18%, indicating that lesion detection performance was preserved despite reduced voxel-level overlap. AI-derived biomarkers showed excellent agreement with ground truth (total tumor volume: r=0.984; total lesion uptake: r=0.989; lesion count: r=0.960). In the clinical cohort, total tumor volume (p=0.0019), SUVmax (p=0.014), and SUVmean (p=0.016) significantly stratified overall survival. Conclusion: Fine-UNETR enables accurate automated whole-body PSMA lesion segmentation and tumor burden quantification. Performance on an external dataset demonstrates robustness despite evidence of domain shift. AI-derived biomarkers significantly stratified overall survival in a pre-radioligand therapy cohort, supporting the clinical utility of automated PSMA PET/CT quantification for prognostication.

AI.tldr: Fine-UNETR, a Vision Transformer-based architecture, achieves accurate automated segmentation of PSMA-avid lesions on whole-body PET/CT, and AI-derived tumor burden biomarkers (total tumor volume, SUVmax, SUVmean) significantly stratify overall survival in prostate cancer patients undergoing radioligand therapy.
AI.motivation: To develop and evaluate a Vision Transformer-based architecture (Fine-UNETR) for automated segmentation of PSMA-avid lesions on whole-body PET/CT, and to assess the clinical utility of AI-derived tumor burden biomarkers for overall survival stratification in radioligand therapy.
AI.method: Fine-UNETR (modified UNETR with 8x8x8 voxel patch embedding and axial sliding window training) was trained on 299 scans and validated on 74 scans from 373 PSMA PET/CT scans. Overall survival stratification assessed via Kaplan-Meier analysis and log-rank testing in an independent cohort of 67 pre-radioligand therapy patients. External validation on 192 cases from AutoPET IV PSMA PET/CT dataset.
AI.result: Fine-UNETR achieved DSC=66.63%, sensitivity=70.27%, precision=67.77%, lesion detection rate=79.53% (96.05% for SUVmax>=5). External validation: DSC=44.11%, lesion detection rate=87.18%. AI biomarkers showed high agreement with ground truth (TTV: r=0.984, TLU: r=0.989, lesion count: r=0.960). Total tumor volume (p=0.0019), SUVmax (p=0.014), and SUVmean (p=0.016) significantly stratified overall survival.
AI.conclusion: Fine-UNETR enables accurate automated whole-body PSMA lesion segmentation and tumor burden quantification with robustness despite domain shift. AI-derived biomarkers significantly stratify overall survival, supporting clinical utility for prognostication in pre-radioligand therapy patients.

title: Large-scale Tunable Liquid Lens-assisted VLC Systems under Random Receiver Orientation
comment: This paper has been submitted to IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications journal
summary: This paper investigates the performance of tunable liquid lens (TLL)-assisted receivers in large-scale visible light communication (VLC) systems under random receiver orientation. A simple electrowetting-based TLL architecture is proposed, capable of dynamically steering the incident optical signal toward the photodiode receiver by adjusting the orientation of the liquid interface. The proposed architecture enhances the desired signal reception while mitigating interference from neighboring access points (APs). The spatial distribution of APs is modeled using a Matérn hard-core point process, whereas receiver orientation is characterized by uniformly distributed azimuth angles and Gaussian-distributed polar angles. Furthermore, a tractable mathematical optical channel model is developed to capture the combined effects of AP/receiver locations, receiver orientation, and lens adjustment angles on the VLC channel gain. Based on this framework, three lens orientation strategies, namely best signal reception (BSR), closest LED selection, and vertical upward lens orientation, are proposed to improve system performance under dynamic receiver conditions. Using stochastic geometry tools, exact and approximate analytical expressions for the outage probability are derived for each scheme. Numerical results verify the accuracy of the developed analysis and demonstrate that the proposed TLL-assisted receiver architecture significantly improves the robustness of VLC systems under severe receiver orientation fluctuations and dense AP deployments. In particular, the BSR scheme reduces the outage probability by $57.1\%$ compared with conventional fixed-lens receivers at an AP height of $3.5$ m and AP density of $0.2~\text{m}^{-2}$. The presented analytical framework and numerical results provide useful design insights for the deployment of future TLL-assisted VLC networks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a tunable liquid lens (TLL)-assisted receiver architecture for VLC systems that dynamically steers incoming light to improve signal reception and reduce interference. Using stochastic geometry, the authors model AP distribution (Matérn hard-core) and random receiver orientation, derive outage probability expressions for three lens strategies (BSR, CLS, vertical upward), and show the BSR scheme reduces outage probability by 57.1% compared to fixed-lens receivers.
AI.motivation: To address performance degradation in VLC systems caused by random receiver orientation and interference from neighboring APs, by introducing a dynamically adjustable liquid lens that can steer incident light toward the photodiode.
AI.method: Proposed an electrowetting-based TLL architecture; modeled AP spatial distribution using Matérn hard-core point process and receiver orientation as uniformly distributed azimuth + Gaussian polar angles; developed a tractable optical channel model; proposed three lens orientation strategies (BSR, CLS, vertical upward); derived exact/approximate outage probability expressions via stochastic geometry.
AI.result: The BSR scheme reduces outage probability by 57.1% compared to fixed-lens receivers at AP height 3.5m and AP density 0.2 m⁻². Analytical expressions are verified by numerical results. TLL-assisted receivers significantly improve robustness under severe orientation fluctuations and dense AP deployments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed TLL-assisted receiver architecture and analytical framework offer useful design insights for future VLC network deployment, particularly in scenarios with dynamic receiver orientations and high AP density.

title: ACE-Ego-0: Unifying Egocentric Human and Robotic Data for VLA Pretraining
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models benefit from large-scale and diverse embodied data, yet scaling robot trajectory collection is costly and labor-intensive. Recent advances show that large-scale egocentric human videos provide complementary real-world supervision in pretraining. However, joint training on human and robot data remains challenging due to divergences in action spaces, embodiment structures, temporal dynamics, and supervision quality. We introduce ACE-EGO-0, a unified VLA pretraining framework jointly leveraging heterogeneous data sources. To extract large-scale pretraining supervision from egocentric human videos, we build a scalable egocentric video-to-action pipeline that converts raw human videos into robot-format pseudo-action trajectories. To make these labels comparable with robot demonstrations, ACE-EGO-0 uses a unified action representation based on camera-space actions, morphology conditioning, and time-aligned action chunking. To robustly leverage noisy pseudo-action supervision from egocentric human videos, we formulate a reliability-aware training objective with a human auxiliary loss that concentrates supervision on reliable signals. We instantiate ACE-EGO-0 on 4.53K hours of robot and simulation data, together with 1.48K hours of pseudo-action-labeled egocentric human data. Experiments show that incorporating large-scale human supervision under reliability-aware weighting consistently improves both unified joint pretraining and supervised fine-tuning. ACE-EGO-0 achieves state-of-the-art performance on RoboCasa GR1 TableTop and RoboTwin 2.0, while demonstrating strong transfer to real-world bimanual manipulation.

AI.tldr: ACE-EGO-0 is a unified VLA pretraining framework that leverages both robot data (4.53K hours) and egocentric human video data (1.48K hours) by converting human videos into robot-format pseudo-action trajectories and using reliability-aware training to handle noisy supervision, achieving SOTA on RoboCasa GR1 and RoboTwin 2.0 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Scaling robot trajectory data is costly, but large-scale egocentric human videos offer complementary supervision. However, jointly training on human and robot data is challenging due to divergences in action spaces, embodiment structures, temporal dynamics, and supervision quality.
AI.method: (1) Scalable egocentric video-to-action pipeline converting human videos into robot-format pseudo-action trajectories. (2) Unified action representation using camera-space actions, morphology conditioning, and time-aligned action chunking. (3) Reliability-aware training objective with human auxiliary loss focusing on reliable signals from noisy pseudo-action supervision.
AI.result: Outperforms prior methods on RoboCasa GR1 TableTop and RoboTwin 2.0 benchmarks. Demonstrates strong transfer to real-world bimanual manipulation tasks. Incorporating human supervision with reliability-aware weighting consistently improves both joint pretraining and supervised fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: ACE-EGO-0 shows that large-scale egocentric human video data can be effectively leveraged for VLA pretraining via a unified framework with reliability-aware learning, enabling better generalization to real-world robotic tasks.

title: GeoDisaster: Benchmarking Orchestrated Agents for Operational Disaster Geo-Intelligence
summary: Remote-sensing vision-language models (RS-VLMs) have advanced Earth-observation analysis toward visual interpretation and instruction-following, yet fall short of operational geo-intelligence, which demands tool-grounded spatial reasoning and structured, evidence-backed decisions. We introduce GeoDisaster, an operational geospatial disaster reasoning benchmark with 2,921 verified instances across 43 question types and five task families: deforestation monitoring, multi-hazard analysis, building-damage assessment, flood-safe routing, and Sentinel-1 SAR flood monitoring. Instances integrate heterogeneous EO/GIS evidence-optical and SAR imagery, raster masks, vector geometries, road networks, and exposure layers-spanning hazard detection, damage assessment, exposure estimation, and diagnostic report generation. Ground-truth answers are grounded in executable geospatial workflows and deterministic consistency checks, removing the need for language-model annotation. We further propose an orchestrated multi-agent framework with 18 disaster-oriented tools, where role-specialized agents coordinate through explicit execution contracts, aligned via Role-Contract Expectation Alignment (RCEA): failure-aware supervised fine-tuning combined with contract-grounded reinforcement learning over dense step-level signals. Experiments show that GeoDisaster challenges existing RS-VLMs and agentic systems, while RCEA improves tool use, evidence grounding, state consistency, and decision generation.

AI.tldr: GeoDisaster is a benchmark for operational geospatial disaster reasoning with 2,921 instances across 43 question types, supported by a multi-agent framework using Role-Contract Expectation Alignment (RCEA) that improves tool use, evidence grounding, and decision making over standard RS-VLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing RS-VLMs advance visual interpretation but lack operational geo-intelligence, particularly tool-grounded spatial reasoning and structured, evidence-backed decisions for real-world disaster analysis.
AI.method: The authors introduce GeoDisaster (2,921 instances, 43 question types, 5 task families) and a multi-agent framework with 18 disaster-oriented tools. Role-specialized agents coordinate via execution contracts aligned through RCEA: failure-aware supervised fine-tuning plus contract-grounded reinforcement learning with dense step-level signals.
AI.result: GeoDisaster challenges existing RS-VLMs and agentic systems. RCEA improves tool use, evidence grounding, state consistency, and decision generation.
AI.conclusion: GeoDisaster bridges the gap between RS-VLMs and operational geo-intelligence by providing a rigorous benchmark and alignment method that significantly enhances multi-agent disaster reasoning capabilities.

title: The Critical Role of Model Selection in Causal Inference: A Comparative Analysis of Classification Models within the InferBERT Framework for Pharmacovigilance
summary: Distinguishing causal adverse drug events (ADEs) from spurious correlations remains a central challenge in pharmacovigilance. The InferBERT framework integrates transformer models with Do-calculus, but its success hinges on the underlying classification model. This study evaluates the impact of model choice in InferBERT, assessing whether simpler models suffice, if domain-specific pre-training helps, whether scaling to LLMs improves causal detection, and the effect of post-hoc calibration. We performed a comparative study on two benchmarks: Analgesics-induced Acute Liver Failure (AILF) and Tramadol-related Mortalities (TRAM). Four models were evaluated-XGBoost (baseline), ALBERT (original InferBERT), BioBERT (biomedical transformer), and Med-LLaMA (medical LLM)-using 5-fold cross-validation repeated over 20 runs. We measured accuracy, Expected Calibration Error (ECE) pre- and post-isotonic regression, and Jaccard concordance of causal terms with PRR, ROR, and EBGM; significance was tested with paired t-tests. BioBERT achieved the highest accuracy on both datasets, while Med-LLaMA underperformed despite its size and parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Domain-specific pre-training was decisive. Calibration improved ECE but had mixed effects on accuracy and causal discovery. BioBERT's superiority also yielded the strongest concordance with traditional pharmacovigilance signals. These results show that domain-specific pre-training provides a clear advantage over simpler baselines and larger LLMs. Investing in manageable, domain-aware models is more effective for computational pharmacovigilance than simply scaling model size.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates how model choice affects the InferBERT framework for detecting causal adverse drug events, finding that domain-specific pre-trained BioBERT outperforms both simpler baselines and larger LLMs like Med-LLaMA.
AI.motivation: Distinguishing causal adverse drug events from spurious correlations is a key challenge in pharmacovigilance. InferBERT integrates transformers with Do-calculus, but its success depends heavily on the underlying classification model, motivating a systematic evaluation of model choices.
AI.method: Comparative study on two benchmarks (AILF and TRAM) evaluating four models: XGBoost (baseline), ALBERT (original InferBERT), BioBERT (biomedical transformer), and Med-LLaMA (medical LLM). Used 5-fold cross-validation over 20 runs, measuring accuracy, Expected Calibration Error (pre/post-isotonic regression), and Jaccard concordance with PRR, ROR, EBGM. Significance tested with paired t-tests.
AI.result: BioBERT achieved highest accuracy on both datasets. Med-LLaMA underperformed despite larger size and parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Domain-specific pre-training was decisive for performance. Calibration improved ECE but had mixed effects on accuracy and causal discovery. BioBERT also showed strongest concordance with traditional pharmacovigilance signals.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific pre-training provides clear advantages over simpler baselines and larger LLMs. Investing in manageable, domain-aware models is more effective for computational pharmacovigilance than scaling model size.

title: Nothing from Something: Can a Language Model Discover 0?
summary: AI systems based on artificial neural networks are being developed with aspirations of pushing the boundary of human mathematical knowledge. A key question for these systems is how much they can reach beyond their training data. Mathematical discovery requires a strong form of out of distribution generalization; the ability to hypothesize genuinely new - and potentially logically more powerful - mathematical structures. It has been hypothesized that language abilities support such generalizations in human cognition. In this work, we use simple arithmetic as a case study for examining how modern AI models could expand their mathematical horizons, evaluating whether these models can independently discover the concept of "zero". We show that We show that (1) language models of a GPT-2 size are unable to perform this generalization at test time regardless of language pretraining, but (2) models can improve substantially after training on tens or hundreds of examples of zero. Additionally, we find that language pretraining reduces the number of required examples by approximately $50\%$, showing that language abilities can scaffold mathematical discovery in neural models.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether AI neural networks can discover the mathematical concept of "zero" beyond their training data, finding that GPT-2 sized language models cannot generalize to zero at test time regardless of language pretraining, but can learn it with few examples (tens to hundreds), with language pretraining reducing required examples by ~50%.
AI.motivation: To understand whether AI systems can achieve strong out-of-distribution generalization—specifically, the ability to discover genuinely new mathematical concepts beyond their training data—using the discovery of "zero" as a case study.
AI.method: The authors use simple arithmetic tasks to test whether GPT-2 sized language models can independently discover the concept of "zero" through: (1) evaluating test-time generalization without explicit training on zero, and (2) providing varying numbers of training examples of zero to measure learning efficiency, with and without language pretraining.
AI.result: (1) GPT-2 sized language models cannot generalize to zero at test time regardless of language pretraining. (2) Models improve substantially after training on tens or hundreds of examples of zero. (3) Language pretraining reduces the number of required examples by approximately 50%.
AI.conclusion: Language abilities can scaffold mathematical discovery in neural models, but current AI systems of this scale cannot independently discover fundamentally new mathematical concepts without some exposure; the finding that language pretraining cuts required examples in half suggests linguistic capabilities support mathematical generalization in AI, analogous to human cognition.

title: Perceptually-Weighted Video Quality Metric for Asymmetric Encoded Sports Videos
comment: accepted for International Conference on Quality of Multimedia Experience 2025 (QoMEX'26)
summary: Objective video quality metrics commonly assume uniform spatial attention, an assumption that conflicts with the selective nature of human visual perception, particularly in sports videos. Here, allocating more bits for salient regions through semantic encoding can lead to significant bitrate savings. We present a Perceptually-Weighted Video Quality Metric (PW-VQM), a full-reference metric that accounts for the unequal perceptual importance of spatial regions and therefore targets quality evaluation for asymmetrically encoded content. SSIM maps computed in a multiscale wavelet domain are weighted by differentiating between foreground and background regions. Perceptually salient foreground regions are identified by combining open-vocabulary object detection with optical flow analysis, and are assigned higher weight during quality aggregation. Evaluated on sports video content, PW-VQM achieves a Spearman Rank Order Correlation Coefficient of 0.9511, outperforming established metrics including SSIM, VMAF, FUNQUE, and LPIPS. An ablation study confirms the individual contributions of the components of the perceptual weighting.

AI.tldr: PW-VQM is a full-reference video quality metric that weights spatial regions perceptually (foreground vs. background) using open-vocabulary object detection and optical flow, achieving SROC=0.9511 on sports videos, outperforming SSIM, VMAF, FUNQUE, and LPIPS.
AI.motivation: Standard video quality metrics assume uniform spatial attention, which is inconsistent with human visual perception — especially in sports videos where viewers focus on salient foreground regions.
AI.method: Multiscale wavelet-domain SSIM maps are weighted by perceptual saliency; foreground regions are identified via open-vocabulary object detection combined with optical flow, then assigned higher weights in quality aggregation.
AI.result: PW-VQM achieves Spearman Rank Order Correlation Coefficient of 0.9511 on sports video content, outperforming SSIM, VMAF, FUNQUE, and LPIPS.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for unequal perceptual importance of spatial regions (via perceptual weighting) improves quality metric accuracy for asymmetrically encoded sports video content.

title: Exploiting RIS Optimization Limits for Multi-User Beamforming and Signal Suppression
comment: 14 pages, 5 figures, submitted to IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
summary: This paper presents a unified framework for exploiting the boundaries of reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RIS) joint optimization in multi-user wireless systems, where a single RIS accommodates diverse objectives.We first propose an adaptive gradient-scaling mechanism that accelerates the convergence of the underlying optimization algorithm while maintaining stable performance across varying channel and system parameters. The proposed mechanism enables the solver to reach a reasonably good solution rapidly without requiring manual tuning of step sizes or algorithmic hyperparameters when system inputs change. We then propose a low-complexity beamformer recovery method tailored for single-user scenarios, which circumvents the full matrix decomposition required by traditional approaches, thereby significantly reducing computational overhead. Building on these foundations, we develop an element allocation strategy that enables user-specific prioritization through assignment of RIS subsets. This is further extended by a modular add-drop mechanism that supports partial-panel optimization in general multi-user settings. The framework is evaluated across three representative scenarios: (i) signal amplification for all users, (ii) signal suppression for all users, and (iii) selective amplification and suppression. To characterize performance limits, we derive power trade-off boundaries using scalarized joint optimization, which closely align with Monte Carlo simulations. Our unified joint optimization method consistently yield solutions near these boundaries, confirming its near-optimality. Extensive simulations under realistic channel models demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms conventional semidefinite relaxation techniques, offering a scalable and effective RIS control strategy for cooperative and competitive multi-user environments.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a unified joint optimization framework for RIS in multi-user wireless systems, featuring an adaptive gradient-scaling mechanism, low-complexity beamformer recovery, element allocation strategy, and modular add-drop mechanism, achieving near-optimal performance across various scenarios.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of jointly optimizing RIS configurations across diverse multi-user objectives without manual hyperparameter tuning, enabling scalable and effective RIS control in both cooperative and competitive environments.
AI.method: Proposes an adaptive gradient-scaling mechanism for faster convergence; a low-complexity beamformer recovery method avoiding full matrix decomposition; an element allocation strategy for user-specific RIS subset prioritization; and a modular add-drop mechanism for partial-panel optimization in multi-user settings.
AI.result: The framework outperforms conventional semidefinite relaxation techniques; derived power trade-off boundaries closely match Monte Carlo simulations; the method consistently yields near-optimal solutions across signal amplification, suppression, and mixed scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unified approach provides a scalable, near-optimal RIS control strategy suitable for cooperative and competitive multi-user wireless systems, eliminating the need for manual parameter tuning.

title: Robust Beamforming Design for Secure Uplink NOMA-ISAC
summary: Integrated sensing and communication is an important technology for sixth-generation (6G) mobile networks, enabling the joint use of communication and radar sensing within a unified system. While offering significant benefits in terms of spectral efficiency, ISAC introduces new security challenges. In particular, the joint use of resources for sensing and communication can increase vulnerability to eavesdropping and information leakage. In this paper, we study an uplink Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA) system where the base station (BS) simultaneously receives user data and senses a potential eavesdropper (Eve) with uncertain location. To enhance the physical-layer security, a robust sensing signal is designed to both sense and jam Eve. We formulate a joint optimization problem that aims to maximize the users' sum rate and the BS sensing performance while maintaining security against Eve. Since the resulting optimization problem is non-convex, we develop an iterative alternating optimization (AO) algorithm that decomposes it into two tractable subproblems. In the first subproblem, the receive combining vectors are optimized in closed form using generalized eigenvalue decomposition. In the second subproblem, the transmit beamforming matrices and sensing power are jointly optimized via semidefinite relaxation (SDR) and successive convex approximation (SCA). Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of our solution in terms of fast convergence and resource allocation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a robust sensing signal design for uplink NOMA-ISAC systems to jointly sense and jam an eavesdropper, using an alternating optimization algorithm that maximizes sum rate and sensing performance while ensuring security.
AI.motivation: ISAC systems, while improving spectral efficiency for 6G, introduce new security vulnerabilities due to shared resources, specifically increasing risks of eavesdropping and information leakage.
AI.method: The authors formulate a non-convex joint optimization problem and solve it via an iterative alternating optimization (AO) algorithm, decomposing into two subproblems: (1) optimizing receive combining vectors via generalized eigenvalue decomposition, and (2) jointly optimizing transmit beamforming and sensing power via semidefinite relaxation (SDR) and successive convex approximation (SCA).
AI.result: Simulation results validate the proposed solution's effectiveness, demonstrating fast convergence and efficient resource allocation.
AI.conclusion: The robust sensing signal design with AO-based optimization effectively enhances physical-layer security in uplink NOMA-ISAC systems against eavesdroppers with uncertain locations.

title: Contrastive Action-Image Pre-training for Visuomotor Control
summary: Existing vision encoders for robotics face a fundamental bottleneck: robotic datasets lack the scale necessary for large-scale pre-training. Prior work circumvents this data scarcity by turning to internet-scale image and language data or egocentric human video. While these models show promise, neither paradigm learns from paired vision and action data, which downstream visuomotor control policies require. However, robot trajectories, the most direct source of this paired signal, are not available at pre-training scale, motivating us to extract action signals from abundant human video instead. To this end, we introduce CAIP (Contrastive Action-Image Pre-training), a vision encoder that treats human hand poses from large-scale egocentric video as a proxy for end-effector actions. By extracting 3D hand keypoints, a representation that aligns naturally with downstream robot action spaces, CAIP learns a unified action-image representation through a contrastive objective. Leveraging 32,041 hours of egocentric human video and only 88 hours of robotic manipulation data, CAIP outperforms state-of-the-art vision encoders including DINOv2, SigLIP, MVP, and R3M. Evaluated on a challenging real-world dexterous manipulation setup using Dexmate Vega and Sharpa Wave hands, CAIP yields performance gains of more than 30% on tasks involving folding, pouring, and fine-grained manipulation. Our results show that our method of contrastive action-centric pre-training yields a scalable path to achieving robust visual representations better suited for physical interaction.

AI.tldr: CAIP is a vision encoder that pre-trains on 32K hours of egocentric human video using hand poses as action proxies, then fine-tunes on only 88 hours of robot data, achieving >30% improvement on dexterous manipulation tasks over SOTA encoders like DINOv2 and SigLIP.
AI.motivation: Robotic datasets lack the scale needed for large-scale vision pre-training; existing approaches use internet images or human video but fail to learn from paired vision-action data that downstream robot policies require.
AI.method: CAIP extracts 3D hand keypoints from egocentric video as a proxy for robot end-effector actions, then uses contrastive learning to align image representations with these action proxies, pre-training on 32,041 hours of human video and fine-tuning on 88 hours of robot data.
AI.result: Outperforms DINOv2, SigLIP, MVP, and R3M; achieves >30% improvement on real-world dexterous manipulation tasks (folding, pouring, fine-grained manipulation) using Dexmate Vega and Sharpa Wave hands.
AI.conclusion: Contrastive action-centric pre-training on human video provides a scalable path to robust visual representations for physical interaction, overcoming the data scarcity bottleneck in robotics.

title: Pulling The REINS: Training-Free Safety Alignment of Video Diffusion Models via Representation Steering
summary: Open-weight video diffusion models can generate photorealistic unsafe content, from violence to misinformation, yet existing defenses either require expensive safety fine-tuning that degrades general capability, or apply external filters that are trivially bypassed by adversarial prompts. We present REINS (REpresentation-space INference-time Safety steering), a training-free method that aligns video diffusion models at inference time by steering their internal representations toward safe generation. Our key finding is that safety-relevant structure is linearly encoded in the hidden-state activations of video diffusion transformers, and a single direction, discovered via Supervised PCA on binary safety labels, suffices to separate safe from unsafe generation trajectories. At inference, adding this direction to hidden states at an intermediate transformer layer redirects generation from harmful content to semantically related safe alternatives, with no weight updates, no concept enumeration, and negligible computational overhead. Through mechanistic analysis, we reveal that while safety information accumulates monotonically with transformer depth, steering effectiveness peaks at intermediate layers (~50% depth), exposing a fundamental tradeoff between information availability and downstream propagation capacity. We evaluate REINS across 9 video diffusion models, multiple parameter scales (1.3B-5B), and both text-to-video and image-to-video generation, to our knowledge, the broadest safety evaluation suite in the video generation literature.

AI.tldr: REINS is a training-free, inference-time safety steering method for video diffusion models that linearly manipulates hidden-state activations in transformer layers to redirect unsafe generation toward safe alternatives, requiring no weight updates or concept enumeration.
AI.motivation: Open-weight video diffusion models can generate photorealistic unsafe content (violence, misinformation, etc.), but existing defenses are either expensive (safety fine-tuning degrades general capability) or easily bypassed (external filters by adversarial prompts).
AI.method: REINS uses Supervised PCA on binary safety labels to find a single linear direction in hidden-state activations of video diffusion transformers. At inference, this direction is added to hidden states at an intermediate transformer layer (~50% depth) to steer generation from harmful to safe content without weight updates.
AI.result: Evaluated across 9 video diffusion models (1.3B-5B parameters), both text-to-video and image-to-video. Steering effectiveness peaks at intermediate layers (~50% depth), revealing a tradeoff between safety information availability and downstream propagation capacity.
AI.conclusion: Safety-relevant structure is linearly encoded in hidden states of video diffusion transformers, and a single PCA-derived direction can effectively steer unsafe content to safe alternatives at inference time with negligible computational overhead.

title: Probing, Fusion, and Trustworthiness: A Systematic Evaluation of Foundation Model Representations for Multimodal Cancer Analysis
summary: Foundation models (FMs) have emerged as powerful representation extractors for medical data, yet their generalizability to datasets under distribution shift remains underexplored. This work systematically evaluates FM-based representations on a suite of computational pathology tasks across two real-world commercial cohorts, IH-BC and IH-NSCLC, drawn from the licensed in-house (IH) oncology dataset. The analysis focuses on two modalities, whole-slide images and transcriptomic profiles, drawn from the IH multimodal data. We first benchmark unimodal probing performance across five FMs on eight downstream classification tasks, and find that image and omics representations carry complementary predictive signals. Then we investigate whether multimodal fusion can yield additional gains over unimodal baselines by comparing three image-omics fusion strategies built on paired representations. The trustworthiness of selected unimodal and multimodal pipelines is further assessed through conformal prediction. Our results show that FM representations achieve competitive performance on out-of-distribution data and that multimodal fusion helps mainly when no single modality dominates the signal. Conformal prediction reveals that in the majority of cases where a point prediction fails, the true diagnosis remains recoverable within the prediction set, reinforcing the value of uncertainty-aware inference for clinical support.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates foundation model representations for computational pathology under distribution shift, showing that unimodal FMs perform competitively on out-of-distribution data, multimodal fusion helps when no single modality dominates, and conformal prediction enhances trustworthiness by recovering true diagnoses even when point predictions fail.
AI.motivation: To systematically evaluate whether FM-based representations generalize to out-of-distribution medical datasets, and to assess the value of multimodal fusion and uncertainty-aware inference for clinical support.
AI.method: Benchmarked five foundation models on eight classification tasks using whole-slide images and transcriptomic profiles from two commercial cohorts. Compared three image-omics fusion strategies and applied conformal prediction for uncertainty quantification.
AI.result: FM representations achieve competitive OOD performance; multimodal fusion offers gains only when no single modality dominates; conformal prediction recovers true diagnoses in most point-prediction failure cases.
AI.conclusion: FM-based representations are robust to distribution shifts in pathology, and uncertainty-aware inference is valuable for trustworthy clinical decision support.

title: Quantifying Consistency in LLM Logical Reasoning via Structural Uncertainty
comment: Published at ICLR 2026 Workshop on Logical Reasoning of Large Language Models. Accepted as best paper
summary: Large language models can arrive at the same answer through reasoning paths that are unstable, contradictory, or difficult to rank consistently -- a failure mode especially prevalent in multi-step deductive reasoning. Existing methods assess reliability primarily through output dispersion -- measuring how much sampled answers differ -- but this discards a complementary signal: whether the model can consistently rank competing reasoning candidates. We propose structural uncertainty, a consistency-aware framework derived from the stability of self-preference-induced rankings over sampled reasoning solutions. Given a query, we generate multiple candidate solutions and ask the model to judge pairwise preferences among its own outputs. We aggregate self-preferences into ranking distributions via Bradley-Terry modeling with PageRank, and decompose the signal into two entropy-based components: across-trial ranking instability and within-trial candidate ambiguity. Across five LLMs and eight benchmarks, structural signals provide information complementary to answer dispersion: on logical and mathematical reasoning tasks, the combination improves identification of unreliable instances, while on factual retrieval the structural signal collapses toward uniformity, diagnosing a regime boundary where reasoning-level consistency evaluation is uninformative. The two components relate differently to accuracy: within-trial ambiguity correlates positively with correctness -- consistent with settings where multiple plausible solution paths remain competitive -- while across-trial instability correlates negatively, signaling unreliable reasoning. Structural uncertainty is best understood not as a universal confidence estimator, but as a regime-sensitive evaluator of logical reasoning consistency.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes "structural uncertainty" — a framework that evaluates LLM reasoning reliability by analyzing the stability of self-preference rankings among multiple candidate solutions, rather than just answer dispersion. It decomposes into two entropy components (across-trial instability and within-trial ambiguity) and shows this signal complements existing methods, especially for logical/mathematical reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing reliability assessment methods rely primarily on answer dispersion (measuring how much sampled answers differ), which discards the complementary signal of whether the model can consistently rank its own reasoning candidates. This is problematic because LLMs often produce unstable, contradictory, or difficult-to-rank reasoning paths, especially in multi-step deductive reasoning.
AI.method: Generate multiple candidate reasoning solutions per query, then ask the model to judge pairwise preferences among its own outputs. Aggregate self-preferences into ranking distributions using Bradley-Terry modeling with PageRank. Decompose the signal into two entropy-based components: across-trial ranking instability and within-trial candidate ambiguity.
AI.result: Across five LLMs and eight benchmarks, structural signals complement answer dispersion. On logical/mathematical reasoning, combining both improves unreliable instance identification. On factual retrieval, structural signal collapses to uniformity (uninformative). Within-trial ambiguity correlates positively with correctness, while across-trial instability correlates negatively.
AI.conclusion: Structural uncertainty is a regime-sensitive evaluator of logical reasoning consistency, not a universal confidence estimator. It is most informative for logical/mathematical reasoning and collapses on factual retrieval tasks, suggesting a boundary where reasoning-level consistency evaluation is uninformative.

title: Multiscale reconstruction of protein conformations from cryo-EM images
comment: 19 pages, 11 figures. Submitted to the Journal of Structural Biology
summary: We present a novel multiscale algorithm for directly recovering the atomic model structure of a protein from single-particle cryo-EM data. Our algorithm is able to estimate protein structures to state-of-the-art accuracy for high-noise and low-contrast data. It is also robust to misspecifications in the TEM image formation model. These desirable properties are primarily due to the use of an explicit representation of the protein backbone in terms of bonds, torsion angles and bond angles, which supplies rich prior information to the structure recovery process. We apply our method on three protein cryo-EM datasets, generated using an electron microscope digital twin, and show that using a multiscale approach yields an improvement of the root-mean-square deviation (RMSD) and template modelling (TM) scores with respect to the ground truth. Furthermore, there is evidence that larger-scale structures are being prioritised with the multiscale algorithm, which reduces the possibility of convergence to bad local minima.

AI.tldr: A multiscale algorithm using explicit protein backbone representation (bonds, torsion/bond angles) recovers atomic models from cryo-EM data with state-of-the-art accuracy under high noise/low contrast and is robust to imaging model misspecifications.
AI.motivation: To directly recover atomic-level protein structures from cryo-EM data that is robust to high noise, low contrast, and misspecifications in the TEM image formation model.
AI.method: A multiscale algorithm that uses an explicit representation of the protein backbone (bonds, torsion angles, bond angles) as rich prior information for structure recovery.
AI.result: Applied to three cryo-EM datasets (digital twin generated); improves RMSD and TM scores; multiscale approach prioritizes larger-scale structures and reduces convergence to bad local minima.
AI.conclusion: The explicit backbone representation and multiscale strategy yield state-of-the-art accuracy under challenging conditions and improve convergence behavior.

title: Deep Learning-Empowered Movable-Antenna Position Optimization with Partial CSI
summary: Movable antennas (MAs) are a promising technology to improve wireless data rates by dynamically adjusting their positions to avoid deep fading. However, finding the optimal MA positions requires full channel state information (CSI) for all possible locations within the movement region, creating massive channel estimation overhead. This paper proposes a deep neural network (DNN)-based learning framework to predict the optimal positions of multiple transmit MAs in a multi-user multiple-input single-output (MISO) system, entirely bypassing explicit channel estimation.First, we analyze a single-user MISO case, revealing a complex, highly nonlinear mapping between the optimal MA positions and the channel power gains from a specific subset of locations in the transmit region to the user. Because this mapping cannot be mathematically characterized for practical channel models, we train a DNN via supervised learning to capture it. The pre-trained DNN can then determine optimized MA positions in real-time relying only on partial power measurements from the transmit region.Extending this to multi-user scenarios is challenging due to complex rate expressions and the lack of globally optimal position solutions to use as training labels. To overcome this, we develop an unsupervised training framework that directly maximizes the multi-user sum-rate. This framework utilizes an attention-based architecture to extract latent features from the partial channel measurements and effectively manage inter-user interference. Simulation results show that our proposed approach achieves near-optimal performance in single-user systems and surpasses conventional CSI-based alternating optimization algorithms in multi-user environments.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a DNN-based framework to predict optimal movable antenna positions in multi-user MISO systems using only partial channel power measurements, bypassing full CSI estimation. Single-user uses supervised learning; multi-user uses unsupervised learning with attention-based architecture to maximize sum-rate.
AI.motivation: Movable antennas can improve data rates but require full CSI for all possible positions, creating massive estimation overhead. Finding optimal positions without explicit channel estimation is critical.
AI.method: A DNN-based learning framework: (1) Single-user: supervised learning to learn mapping from partial power measurements to optimal MA positions. (2) Multi-user: unsupervised training with attention-based architecture that directly maximizes sum-rate, using only partial channel measurements.
AI.result: Single-user: near-optimal performance. Multi-user: surpasses conventional CSI-based alternating optimization algorithms.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DNN framework effectively bypasses full CSI estimation for movable antenna positioning, achieving near-optimal performance in single-user and outperforming traditional methods in multi-user settings.

title: Pilot-Aided MIMO Channel Identification and Linear Deconvolution in Correlated Gaussian Noise
summary: This paper presents a pilot-aided study of multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) channel identification and linear deconvolution under spatially correlated Gaussian noise. A real-valued $4\times4$ baseband model is analyzed for both memoryless and finite-impulse-response channels. The noise process is generated from a Toeplitz covariance matrix, the channel is estimated from pilot symbols through maximum-likelihood/least-squares formulations, and the empirical mean-square error is compared with the Cramer--Rao bound. The estimated channel is then used for data-symbol recovery through maximum-likelihood zero-forcing and linear minimum-mean-square-error deconvolution. The results show that sufficiently long and well-conditioned pilot blocks allow the channel estimator to approach the theoretical lower bound, whereas short training intervals cause rank and conditioning limitations, especially for the four-tap model. The deconvolution experiments further show that MMSE regularization provides a more stable inverse than unregularized zero forcing at low signal-to-noise ratios and for inaccurate channel estimates.

AI.tldr: This paper studies MIMO channel estimation and linear deconvolution under spatially correlated Gaussian noise, using pilot symbols and comparing channel estimation MSE to the Cramer-Rao bound, with findings that long pilot blocks approach theoretical bounds while MMSE regularization outperforms zero-forcing at low SNR.
AI.motivation: To analyze MIMO channel identification and linear deconvolution under spatially correlated Gaussian noise, particularly understanding how pilot length and conditioning affect estimation accuracy and data recovery.
AI.method: Real-valued 4×4 baseband model analysis for memoryless and FIR channels; Toeplitz covariance noise; maximum-likelihood/least-squares channel estimation; zero-forcing and MMSE deconvolution for data recovery; comparison with Cramer-Rao bound.
AI.result: Long, well-conditioned pilot blocks allow channel estimation near the Cramer-Rao bound; short training causes rank/conditioning issues especially for the 4-tap model; MMSE regularization yields more stable inversion than unregularized zero-forcing at low SNR and with inaccurate estimates.
AI.conclusion: Sufficient pilot length is critical for approaching theoretical estimation bounds, and MMSE regularization is preferable for robust data recovery under challenging noise and estimation conditions.

title: VISTA: Scale-Aware Visual Navigation via Action History Conditioning
summary: Vision Navigation Foundation Models (VNMs) promise end-to-end learned navigation policies capable of zero-shot deployment across diverse embodiments and environments. To maintain generality, many vision-based navigation models predict normalized actions. However, this normalization introduces a critical deployment vulnerability: applying different scaling factors to the same normalized trajectory alters its physical geometry, which degrades navigation performance and increases collision risks. We address this vulnerability by conditioning the model on normalized action histories alongside image observations, providing explicit context on the relationship between the model's predictions and the robot's actual physical displacement. Furthermore, current VNMs often struggle in visually repetitive environments that lack distinct features. To resolve this issue, we integrate a DINOv3 encoder, whose richer representations enable our model to capture both spatial and geometric dimensions between observations. VISTA generalizes robustly to out-of-distribution environments, achieving 100% goal prediction accuracy in zero-shot, real-world deployment in Outdoor, Forest and Office settings, and an average of 95% checkpoints crossed, demonstrating consistent path following in unseen environments.

AI.tldr: VISTA is a Vision Navigation Foundation Model that addresses two key vulnerabilities: action normalization issues (by conditioning on normalized action histories) and struggles in visually repetitive environments (by integrating DINOv3 encoder). It achieves 100% goal prediction accuracy and 95% checkpoint crossing in zero-shot real-world deployment.
AI.motivation: Current VNMs suffer from deployment vulnerabilities due to normalized action scaling that alters physical geometry, and struggle in visually repetitive environments lacking distinct features.
AI.method: Conditioning the model on normalized action histories alongside image observations to provide context on prediction-to-physical displacement relationships, and integrating a DINOv3 encoder for richer spatial and geometric representations.
AI.result: 100% goal prediction accuracy in zero-shot real-world deployment across Outdoor, Forest, and Office settings; average 95% checkpoints crossed; robust generalization to out-of-distribution environments.
AI.conclusion: VISTA demonstrates that addressing action scaling vulnerabilities and leveraging richer visual encoders (DINOv3) enables robust zero-shot navigation across diverse unseen environments.

title: Training LLMs with Reinforcement Learning over Digital Twin Representations for Reasoning-Intensive Surgical VideoQA
summary: Surgical video question answering requires multi-step reasoning across semantic, spatial, and temporal dimensions. Existing methods architecturally compress videos into discrete token representations and couple visual perception with reasoning. This approach fragments continuous spatial-temporal relationships and has been shown to restrict multi-step reasoning capabilities. We introduce a reinforcement learning (RL) framework that trains large language models (LLMs) to decouple perception from reasoning by operating over digital twin representations constructed from surgical foundation models. Additionally, we introduce hierarchical representations across frame, temporal window, and procedure levels with probabilistic uncertainty estimates. Finally, we propose a novel reward that combines format validation with accuracy assessment through clinical plausibility evaluation and uncertainty-aware calibration for training. To demonstrate the capabilities of this approach, we introduce REAL-Colon-Reason, a colonoscopic benchmark with 2000 question-answer pairs across three complexity levels. We achieve state-of-the-art performance on REAL-Colon-Reason and two existing surgical VideoQA benchmarks REAL-Colon-VQA and EndoVis18-VQA.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an RL framework that trains LLMs to decouple perception from reasoning using digital twin representations from surgical foundation models, achieving SOTA on surgical VideoQA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing surgical VideoQA methods compress videos into discrete tokens, coupling perception with reasoning, which fragments spatial-temporal relationships and limits multi-step reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: (1) An RL framework that trains LLMs to decouple perception from reasoning using digital twin representations from surgical foundation models; (2) Hierarchical representations across frame, temporal window, and procedure levels with probabilistic uncertainty estimates; (3) A novel reward combining format validation, clinical plausibility evaluation, and uncertainty-aware calibration.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on REAL-Colon-Reason (new benchmark with 2000 QA pairs across three complexity levels), REAL-Colon-VQA, and EndoVis18-VQA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling perception from reasoning via RL over digital twin representations significantly improves multi-step surgical video question answering, as demonstrated by SOTA results across multiple benchmarks.

title: MODE: Modality-Decomposed Expert-Level Mixed-Precision Quantization for MoE Multimodal LLMs
summary: Mixture-of-Experts Multimodal Large Language Models (MoE-MLLMs) offer remarkable performance but incur prohibitive GPU memory costs, making compression essential. Among PTQ methods, expert-level mixed-precision quantization has proven effective for MoE-LLMs, yet suffers notable degradation on MoE-MLLMs due to two overlooked biases in expert importance estimation. (1) At the cross-modal level, the numerical dominance of vision tokens causes expert selection frequency to be dominated by vision tokens, masking experts that are critical to the text modality; (2) at the intra-vision level, the large proportion of redundant vision tokens further skew frequency statistics, obscuring experts critical for informative visual content. To bridge gaps, we propose MODE, a modality-decomposed expert-level mixed-precision quantization framework for MoE-MLLMs that decomposes expert selection frequency by modality, filters redundant vision tokens to obtain denoised visual frequency, and further evaluates quantization sensitivity per modality as a complementary signal to frequency-based estimation. These signals are integrated into an Integer Linear Programming formulation to assign per-expert bit-widths under a given budget. Extensive experiments show that MODE is particularly well-suited for MoE-MLLMs, limiting average performance loss to within 2.9% at W3A16, with larger gains at the extreme 2-bit setting.

AI.tldr: MODE is a modality-decomposed mixed-precision quantization framework for MoE-MLLMs that corrects cross-modal and intra-vision biases in expert importance estimation, achieving under 2.9% performance loss at W3A16.
AI.motivation: Existing expert-level mixed-precision PTQ methods work for MoE-LLMs but degrade on MoE-MLLMs due to two biases: (1) vision tokens numerically dominate expert selection frequency, masking text-critical experts; (2) redundant vision tokens skew frequency statistics, obscuring experts critical for informative visual content.
AI.method: MODE decomposes expert selection frequency by modality, filters redundant vision tokens for denoised visual frequency, evaluates quantization sensitivity per modality, and integrates these signals into Integer Linear Programming to assign per-expert bit-widths under budget constraints.
AI.result: MODE limits average performance loss to within 2.9% at W3A16, with larger gains at extreme 2-bit settings, demonstrating strong suitability for MoE-MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: MODE effectively bridges the modality biases in expert importance estimation for MoE-MLLM quantization, enabling efficient compression with minimal performance degradation.

title: MemTrace: Probing What Final Accuracy Misses in Long-Term Memory
summary: LLM agents increasingly maintain long-term memory of user facts across sessions. Yet such memory is usually evaluated by aggregating accuracy over question rows or episodes. Because this approach scores question rows independently, even when several questions probe the same fact, it cannot show how that fact behaves as conditions change. We introduce MemTrace, a benchmark whose unit of measurement is the knowledge point: a single typed fact about the user, rather than an individual question. MemTrace probes each fact along three controlled dimensions: memory age, defined by how many sessions ago the fact appeared in the history; question type, covering current state, earlier state, and trajectory of change; and evidence condition, covering present, missing, and contradicted-by-false-premise settings. Evaluating 13 memory-system configurations across four paradigms, we find that similar pooled accuracy hides different failures: recovering a fact's current and earlier states does not imply tracking how it changed, and safe abstention does not imply correcting a false premise. The dominant bottleneck is evidence use, not retrieval: when systems fail, the evidence was retrievable 10 times more often than it was missing. These results suggest that improving long-term memory requires better use of reachable evidence, not simply more storage or retrieval.

AI.tldr: MemTrace is a benchmark that evaluates LLM memory by tracking knowledge points across three dimensions (age, question type, evidence condition), revealing that pooled accuracy hides specific failure modes and that the main bottleneck is evidence use rather than retrieval.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation methods aggregate accuracy over individual question rows, which cannot reveal how facts behave as conditions change (e.g., age, question type, evidence condition).
AI.method: MemTrace introduces knowledge points as the unit of measurement and probes facts along three controlled dimensions: memory age, question type (current/earlier state, trajectory of change), and evidence condition (present/missing/contradicted-by-false-premise).
AI.result: Evaluating 13 memory-system configurations across four paradigms shows that similar pooled accuracy hides different failures; recovering current/earlier states doesn't imply tracking change; safe abstention doesn't imply correcting false premises; and evidence was retrievable 10x more often than missing.
AI.conclusion: Improving long-term memory requires better use of reachable evidence rather than simply more storage or retrieval capacity.

title: Feedforward and Iterative Phase Noise Compensation for Channels with Chromatic Dispersion
comment: Accepted at European Conference on Optical Communications (ECOC) 2026
summary: Equalization-enhanced phase noise is avoided by applying phase noise compensation (PNC) before chromatic dispersion compensation. Feedforward and iterative PNC algorithms based on expectation propagation are proposed. Both achieve information rates close to channels without phase noise for 100 GBaud 64-QAM and 10,000 km of fiber.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes feedforward and iterative phase noise compensation (PNC) algorithms based on expectation propagation, applied before chromatic dispersion compensation, to avoid equalization-enhanced phase noise. Both methods achieve near-ideal information rates for 100 GBaud 64-QAM systems over 10,000 km fiber.
AI.motivation: To address equalization-enhanced phase noise (EEPN) in long-haul fiber-optic communication systems by applying phase noise compensation before chromatic dispersion compensation.
AI.method: Feedforward and iterative phase noise compensation (PNC) algorithms based on expectation propagation, placed before chromatic dispersion compensation to avoid EEPN.
AI.result: Both feedforward and iterative PNC algorithms achieve information rates close to channels without phase noise for 100 GBaud 64-QAM over 10,000 km of fiber.
AI.conclusion: The proposed expectation propagation-based PNC algorithms effectively mitigate EEPN, enabling high-performance long-haul transmission.

title: Backscatter Assisted Indoor NLOS Positioning
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures, accepted by IEEE International Symposium on Personal, Indoor and Mobile Radio Communications (PIMRC) 2026
summary: Passive backscatter devices (BDs) can enable indoor non-line-of-sight (NLOS) positioning by serving as virtual anchors whose Doppler-separated signatures are observable in standard channel estimates. This paper studies continuous user-equipment (UE) tracking in corridor environments using a noncoherent power-domain formulation that avoids BD phase synchronization and remains robust to residual carrier offsets and strong multipath. The BD-dependent measurements are modeled by a log-distance law with unknown BD-specific offsets, which allows passive asynchronous devices to be used as anchors without transmit-power calibration. Based on this model, we develop a corridor-constrained maximum a posteriori (MAP) tracker with motion regularization and Huber-robust estimation. In ray-tracing-inspired simulations, the method achieves median positioning errors of 0.23--0.27 m with 90th-percentile errors below 0.45 m. In office-corridor measurements with four passive BDs at 866 MHz, it attains an aggregated median error of 0.505 m and outperforms a simple weighted-average baseline. The results show that passive asynchronous BDs can provide practical sub-meter indoor NLOS tracking while remaining compatible with existing channel-estimation pipelines and energy-autonomous BD deployments.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a corridor-constrained MAP tracker using passive backscatter devices as asynchronous anchors for indoor NLOS UE tracking, achieving sub-meter accuracy (0.23–0.27 m median in simulation, 0.505 m in real measurements) without requiring BD phase synchronization or power calibration.
AI.motivation: To enable continuous indoor NLOS user-equipment tracking using passive backscatter devices as virtual anchors, while avoiding the challenges of BD phase synchronization, residual carrier offsets, and strong multipath in corridor environments.
AI.method: A noncoherent power-domain formulation with log-distance path loss model and unknown BD-specific offsets, combined with a corridor-constrained MAP tracker using motion regularization and Huber-robust estimation.
AI.result: Simulation: median errors 0.23–0.27 m, 90th-percentile < 0.45 m. Real office-corridor measurements (866 MHz, 4 passive BDs): aggregated median error 0.505 m, outperforming weighted-average baseline.
AI.conclusion: Passive asynchronous BDs can provide practical sub-meter indoor NLOS tracking compatible with existing channel-estimation pipelines and energy-autonomous BD deployments.

title: Abstention-Aware Personalized Object Rearrangement via Uncertainty-Guided LLM Assistance
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE 35th International Conference on Robot and Human Interactive Communication (RO-MAN 2026)
summary: Robotic assistance in household environments requires not only predicting where objects should be placed, but also reasoning about when objects should not be placed at all. Existing approaches to personalized object rearrangement primarily focus on placement decisions under the assumption of clean observations and complete actionability, limiting their applicability in realistic, cluttered, and partially erroneous settings. In this paper, we introduce APOLLO, a hybrid framework for abstention-aware personalized object rearrangement that combines a lightweight, personalized embedding model (PEM) with selective large language model (LLM) assistance. PEM is trained for each user-environment pair using a small number of demonstrations, operates entirely on CPU, and produces uncertainty estimates, which are used to selectively invoke LLM-based reasoning only for ambiguous decisions, balancing efficiency, privacy, and reasoning capability. To evaluate this formulation beyond existing benchmarks, we introduce APOR, a synthetic, LLM-generated dataset that captures room-level, multi-furniture environments, diverse organizational profiles, explicit abstention behavior, and noisy partial scene context. Extensive experiments on both PARSEC and APOR provide initial evidence that APOLLO improves over prior LLM-based baselines in controlled benchmark settings while substantially reducing LLM usage. Code is available at https://github.com/PaInt-Lab/APOLLO.

AI.tldr: APOLLO is a hybrid framework for abstention-aware object rearrangement that combines a lightweight CPU-based personalized embedding model (PEM) with selective LLM assistance, using uncertainty estimates to invoke LLM reasoning only for ambiguous decisions, achieving improved performance over LLM baselines with reduced LLM usage.
AI.motivation: Robotic assistance in household environments requires reasoning about when objects should not be placed at all. Existing approaches assume clean observations and complete actionability, limiting applicability in realistic, cluttered settings.
AI.method: APOLLO combines a lightweight, CPU-based personalized embedding model (PEM) trained per user-environment with selective LLM invocation based on uncertainty estimates, balancing efficiency, privacy, and reasoning.
AI.result: APOLLO improves over prior LLM-based baselines in controlled benchmark settings while substantially reducing LLM usage. Also introduces the APOR synthetic dataset.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid approach with abstention-aware reasoning is effective for personalized object rearrangement in realistic, cluttered environments.

title: Pareto LoRA: Mitigating Modality Imbalance in Unified Multimodal Models via Pareto-Optimal Gradient Integration
summary: Unified multimodal models (UMMs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for integrating multimodal understanding and generation within a single autoregressive transformer. However, during multimodal instruction tuning, these models often exhibit pronounced modality imbalance: language gradients dominate optimization, thus leading to lower image generation quality, especially under parameter-efficient fine-tuning such as LoRA. In this work, we systematically analyze modality imbalance in LoRA-based fine-tuning of UMMs for interleaved text-image generation. We show that vision modality performance degrades substantially more than text modality performance when compared to unimodal counterparts, and that modality-specific gradients can differ by orders of magnitude across various tasks and layers. Motivated by this observation, we reformulate the multimodal instruction tuning as a bi-objective optimization problem and propose Pareto LoRA, a Pareto-optimal gradient integration strategy that balances the text and image objectives by modulating the gradient direction and strength. Experiments on the CoMM benchmark with Emu2 demonstrate that Pareto LoRA consistently improves multimodal generation balance, achieving up to 44.9% gains in perceptual image quality over vanilla LoRA while maintaining comparable text performance.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Pareto LoRA, a method that addresses modality imbalance in multimodal instruction tuning by reformulating it as a bi-objective optimization problem, achieving up to 44.9% improvement in image generation quality while maintaining text performance.
