Surprisingly, latent damage representations plus a simple yet robust ensemble method is sufficient for adapting to unseen damage in real-time. Moreover, we identify conditions under which exponential sample complexity collapses to linear sample complexity for learned representations of architected materials, a concrete advantage over rigid components or continuum soft mechanisms. We demonstrate LEAP, our method for adaptive proprioception, via a tracing task for a 6DoF soft wrist based on Handed Shearing Auxetic (HSA) actuators. Our algorithm is able to adapt to cuts, burns, and actuator repairs, enabling simulation-free real-time adaptation that is critical for realizing the promise of soft robots outside the lab. Videos and more information are available at https://murpheylab.github.io/leap.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces LEAP, a method for real-time proprioceptive adaptation to catastrophic damage in soft-actuated robotic systems (specifically a 6DoF soft wrist with HSA actuators) that achieves adaptation in under one minute by leveraging latent damage representations and ensemble learning, with theoretical sample complexity advantages over rigid systems.
AI.motivation: Long-term robot autonomy requires adaptation to physical damage, but current approaches are limited to well-anticipated failure modes. Soft-actuated systems need real-time, simulation-free adaptation methods that work outside controlled lab environments.
AI.method: The proposed LEAP method uses latent damage representations combined with a robust ensemble method for proprioceptive adaptation. It leverages architected materials' property of gradual (rather than acute) actuator failure, and exploits low-dimensional discrete damage representations. The approach enables adaptation to cuts, burns, and actuator repairs.
AI.result: LEAP achieves adaptation to catastrophic damage in under one minute on a 6DoF soft wrist. The method adapts to unseen damage types (cuts, burns, repairs) in real-time without simulation. The authors also identify conditions where exponential sample complexity collapses to linear sample complexity for learned representations of architected materials.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates that simulation-free real-time adaptation to physical damage is achievable for soft robotic systems, crucial for deploying soft robots outside the lab. The sample complexity advantage of architected materials over rigid/continuum soft systems is a key theoretical insight.

title: Learning a Maximum Entropy Model for Visual Textures using Diffusion
summary: Visual textures -- spatially homogeneous image regions containing repeated elements (e.g. a field of grass, the bark of a tree) -- are ubiquitous in visual scenes and provide important cues for recognizing and analyzing materials and objects. A number of existing texture models extract essential statistics from a single texture image, and can then generate high-quality samples that are visually similar to the original by matching these statistics. However, their statistics are either hand-designed or based on a network pretrained for another purpose (e.g., object recognition). Here, we develop the first principled method for unsupervised learning of a set of statistics that are used to constrain a maximum entropy probability model. We leverage methods developed for generative diffusion models to derive training and sampling procedures, and compare these to the traditional method of sampling via matching the statistics. Despite the compactness of our trained model (512 statistics), it generates texture images whose quality is as good as or better than the current state-of-the-art model (~177k statistics). A more direct comparison of the two models, obtained by synthesizing images that are indistinguishable for one model but maximally different for the other, reveals their relative strengths and weaknesses. Finally, we show that unlike previous statistical texture models, a straight trajectory in the representation space of our model generates homogeneous texture samples that interpolate smoothly between the features of the two end points.

AI.tldr: This paper develops the first principled method for unsupervised learning of image statistics for texture modeling using maximum entropy probability models and diffusion-based techniques, achieving state-of-the-art quality with only 512 statistics compared to ~177k in prior work.
AI.motivation: Existing texture models use either hand-designed statistics or statistics from pretrained networks (e.g., for object recognition), lacking a principled unsupervised learning approach for deriving optimal texture statistics.
AI.method: The authors leverage generative diffusion model methods to train and sample from a maximum entropy probability model, learning a compact set of 512 statistics in an unsupervised manner rather than relying on hand-crafted or borrowed features.
AI.result: The model generates texture images with quality matching or exceeding the state-of-the-art model (which uses ~177k statistics), despite being dramatically more compact (512 statistics). The paper also provides comparative analysis through synthesized images that reveal model differences.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method not only achieves superior or comparable quality with far fewer statistics, but also enables smooth interpolation between texture features in representation space, a capability not available in previous statistical texture models.

title: PowerOPD: Stabilizing On-Policy Distillation with Bounded Power Transformation
summary: Standard on-policy distillation (OPD) for large language models estimates the reverse-KL objective using student-sampled tokens, yielding an unbiased single-sample Monte Carlo estimator that avoids vocabulary-wide computation. However, we show that this estimator suffers from severe training pathologies in practice: sample inefficiency, unstable generation dynamics, and a substantial performance gap compared to exact full-vocabulary OPD. Reward-level diagnosis traces these pathologies to the log-ratio reward, which is unbounded by construction, producing extremely high-variance gradients concentrated at early positions and persisting throughout training; standard post-hoc scaling fail as they operate only after this distortion occurs. To solve this problem, we propose PowerOPD: a family of natively bounded, sign-consistent rewards from the Box-Cox power transformation, parameterized by alpha > 0, of which the log-ratio is the degenerate alpha -> 0 limit. Across six mathematical reasoning benchmarks and four Qwen3 teacher-student pairs, PowerOPD achieves benchmark-averaged Avg@8/Pass@8 gains of up to +6.37/+5.71 over vanilla OPD, +3.01/+3.54 over post-hoc stabilization, and +2.59/+8.90 over full-vocabulary OPD, while reducing wall-clock time by 59.2% and peak GPU memory by 23.1%. Larger alpha generally improves accuracy, consistently shortens responses, and keeps gradient norms more than 3,000x smaller than vanilla OPD.

AI.tldr: Standard on-policy distillation (OPD) for LLMs suffers from training pathologies due to unbounded log-ratio rewards causing high-variance gradients. The authors propose PowerOPD, a family of bounded rewards using Box-Cox transformation, which achieves significant gains across math reasoning benchmarks while reducing compute cost.
AI.motivation: On-policy distillation (OPD) estimators using student-sampled tokens for reverse-KL suffer from three practical pathologies: sample inefficiency, unstable generation dynamics, and a significant performance gap versus full-vocabulary OPD. These are traced to the unbounded log-ratio reward that produces extremely high-variance gradients.
AI.method: Proposes PowerOPD, which applies Box-Cox power transformation (parameterized by α > 0) to create natively bounded, sign-consistent rewards. The log-ratio reward is a degenerate limit (α → 0). Standard post-hoc scaling fails because it acts after distortion occurs; PowerOPD addresses the root cause.
AI.result: Across 6 math reasoning benchmarks and 4 Qwen3 teacher-student pairs: Avg@8/Pass@8 gains of +6.37/+5.71 over vanilla OPD, +3.01/+3.54 over post-hoc stabilization, +2.59/+8.90 over full-vocabulary OPD. Reduces wall-clock time by 59.2%, peak GPU memory by 23.1%. Larger α improves accuracy, shortens responses, and keeps gradient norms >3,000x smaller.
AI.conclusion: PowerOPD effectively solves the training pathologies of vanilla on-policy distillation by fundamentally bounding the reward function, outperforming both vanilla OPD and full-vocabulary OPD while being more computationally efficient.

title: MapSatisfyBench: Benchmarking Satisfaction-Aware Map Agents through Behavior-Grounded Implicit Decision Factors
summary: Large language model agents are increasingly integrated into map services. Since map services are embedded in everyday-life scenarios rather than professional task settings, users often express their needs informally, resulting in underspecified queries with many unspoken needs, namely, implicit decision factors that are critical for user satisfaction. Although clarification is an effective way to mitigate this issue, it increases user burden in daily interaction, and a capable agent should first proactively recover such factors from available information sources. However, evaluating this ability is challenging. The first challenge is to determine which implicit decision factors are suitable for evaluation. A factor is evaluable only if it affects user acceptance and can be recovered from information available to the agent before it responds. Second, user satisfaction cannot be reliably represented by a single reference answer, requiring a benchmark that converts satisfaction-relevant factors into objective and quantifiable evaluation targets. To address these challenges, we propose a restore-identify-filter framework that reconstructs complete user needs from behavior-chain evidence, identifies implicit decision factors, and retains only those supported by pre-query evidence. Building on this methodology, we construct MapSatisfyBench from large-scale, real-world anonymized user data and annotate ground truth from five dimensions and enables full-chain evaluation of satisfaction-aware map agents. Experiments show that current agents generally perform well on explicit task completion, but remain limited in satisfying implicit decision factors and proactively acquiring the evidence needed for satisfaction-aware decisions. These findings establish MapSatisfyBench as a benchmark for shifting map-agent evaluation from task completion toward satisfaction-aware spatial decision making.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes MapSatisfyBench, a benchmark for evaluating LLM-based map agents on their ability to recover implicit user needs (decision factors) from available information, rather than requiring users to clarify underspecified queries. The authors build this benchmark using a restore-identify-filter framework on real-world user data, and find current agents excel at explicit tasks but struggle with satisfying implicit needs.
AI.motivation: Map services are used in everyday scenarios where users give informal, underspecified queries with unspoken needs. While clarification questions could help, they increase user burden. Ideally, agents should proactively recover implicit decision factors from available information. However, evaluating this ability is hard because (1) it's unclear which implicit factors are evaluable, and (2) user satisfaction can't be captured by a single reference answer.
AI.method: The authors propose a restore-identify-filter framework: (1) Restore complete user needs from behavior-chain evidence, (2) Identify implicit decision factors, (3) Filter to retain only factors supported by pre-query evidence. Using this, they construct MapSatisfyBench from large-scale real-world anonymized user data with annotations across five dimensions for full-chain evaluation.
AI.result: Current LLM agents perform well on explicit task completion but remain limited in satisfying implicit decision factors and proactively acquiring evidence needed for satisfaction-aware decisions.
AI.conclusion: MapSatisfyBench establishes a benchmark that shifts map-agent evaluation from task completion toward satisfaction-aware spatial decision making, highlighting the gap in current agents' ability to handle implicit user needs.

title: Where Should Action Generation Begin? A Learnable Source Prior for Generative Robot Policies
summary: Generative robot policies typically begin action generation from an observation-independent standard Gaussian distribution, leaving the choice of source distribution underexplored. This work asks a simple question: where should action generation begin? We propose LeaP, a Learnable source Prior that replaces the standard Gaussian with a proprioception-conditioned diagonal Gaussian over action chunks. Parameterized by a lightweight MLP, LeaP jointly predicts the mean and state-adaptive variance of the source distribution, while keeping the downstream generator architecture and inference solver unchanged. This design provides an observation-informed yet stochastic initialization, allowing the generator to focus on precise action refinement rather than transporting samples from an uninformed noise source. On 15 RoboTwin manipulation tasks, LeaP achieves an average success rate of 81.6%, outperforming four representative baselines -- including deterministic-source methods, a no-prior counterpart, and a diffusion-bridge policy -- by 6.5 to 25.5 percentage points. The same prior consistently improves both flow-matching and diffusion-bridge generators, while using fewer parameters and converging faster. The advantage carries over to real-world deployment, where LeaP attains the best performance. These results suggest that the source distribution is an independent and reusable design axis for generative robot policies, complementary to the choice of generative dynamics.

AI.tldr: LeaP replaces the standard Gaussian noise source in generative robot policies with a learnable, proprioception-conditioned prior, achieving 81.6% average success rate on 15 tasks and outperforming baselines by 6.5-25.5 percentage points.
AI.motivation: The standard Gaussian initialization for action generation is observation-independent and uninformed, leaving room for improvement by providing a more informative starting point.
AI.method: LeaP uses a lightweight MLP to predict a proprioception-conditioned diagonal Gaussian (mean and state-adaptive variance) as the source distribution, keeping the downstream generator and solver unchanged.
AI.result: 81.6% average success rate on 15 RoboTwin tasks; outperforms four baselines by 6.5-25.5 percentage points; fewer parameters and faster convergence; consistent improvements across flow-matching and diffusion-bridge generators; best real-world performance.
AI.conclusion: Source distribution is an independent and reusable design axis for generative robot policies, complementary to the choice of generative dynamics.

title: Bayesian Magnetic Resonance Joint Image Reconstruction and Uncertainty Quantification using Sparsity Prior Models and Markov Chain Monte Carlo Sampling
summary: We propose a novel framework for uncertainty quantification using compressed sensing magnetic resonance image reconstruction. The problem is formulated within a Bayesian framework as a linear inverse problem, with prior distributions assigned to the unknown model parameters. Specifically, the image to be reconstructed is assumed to be sparse in a given basis. We develop a general framework applicable to any basis and as examples, we test the sparsity of the image in its (1) spatial gradients using a total variation prior model, and in its (2) wavelet transform. A Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method, based on a split-and-augmented Gibbs sampler, is then employed to sample from the posterior distribution of the unknown parameters. The non-differentiable conditional distributions are efficiently sampled using a proximal MCMC method. The proposed algorithms are validated on both single-coil and multi-coil datasets using various k-space sub-sampling patterns and ratios. The results demonstrate the superior performance of each proposed approach in reconstructing images compared to its counterpart optimisation-based method. Moreover, our framework effectively quantifies uncertainty, showing a notable correlation between estimated uncertainty maps and error maps computed using ground truth and reconstructed images, compared with existing deep learning-based methods.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian framework for uncertainty quantification in compressed sensing MRI reconstruction using MCMC sampling with sparsity priors (TV and wavelet), demonstrating superior reconstruction and uncertainty estimation compared to optimization and deep learning methods.
AI.motivation: Existing compressed sensing MRI reconstruction methods (optimization-based and deep learning-based) lack reliable uncertainty quantification. The authors aim to provide both accurate reconstruction and principled uncertainty estimation for clinical reliability.
AI.method: Formulates MRI reconstruction as a Bayesian linear inverse problem with sparsity-inducing priors (total variation and wavelet). Uses a split-and-augmented Gibbs sampler (proximal MCMC) to sample from the posterior, handling non-differentiable conditional distributions efficiently.
AI.result: Outperforms optimization-based methods in reconstruction quality on both single-coil and multi-coil datasets across various k-space patterns. Uncertainty maps show strong correlation with error maps, outperforming existing deep learning-based uncertainty methods.
AI.conclusion: The Bayesian MCMC framework effectively combines high-quality reconstruction with reliable uncertainty quantification, offering a principled alternative to deterministic or deep learning approaches for compressed sensing MRI.

title: Sum-of-Squares Degree Barriers for the Reweighted-Hinge Method in Robust Halfspace Learning: A Christoffel-Function Characterization
summary: A certificate that removes outliers sees the data only through its low-degree moments, and an adversary exploits exactly this, hiding corruption where the clean data already looks typical, in the blind spot no bounded-degree test resolves. That blind spot turns out to have an exact size: the Christoffel function of the clean marginal, the very quantity modern data analysis thresholds to detect outliers, here read from the adversary's side as the corruption a bounded-degree certificate cannot remove. We turn this inversion into the organizing principle of the reweighted-hinge approach to robustly learning $γ$-margin halfspaces under malicious noise (Shen, 2025; Zeng and Shen, 2025): the governing resource is the Sum-of-Squares degree of the outlier-removal certificate, and the resolution principle states that the maximal corruption mass which can hide at a center $c$ from a degree-$2t$ certificate is exactly the Christoffel function $λ_{t+1}(c)$ of the clean marginal. Three consequences follow, all against the certificate method (not information-theoretic). A margin-degree tradeoff: certifying the dense pancake to error $ε$ costs SoS degree $Ω(\log(1/ε))$ or margin $Ω(\sqrt{\log(1/ε)}/\sqrt{d})$, explaining why the $\log(1/ε)$ margin Shen (2025) records is forced, with a weighted-Chebyshev reduction making the threshold $2t=Θ((|c|/s)^2)$ tight modulo one classical weighted-extremal estimate. A degree-$2$ outlier barrier: the resolution principle realized as an explicit instance on which degree $2$ is stuck at $η^{1/2}$ while degree $4$ escapes, locating the method's small breakdown rate in the degree, not the analysis. And a degree-$2t$ algorithm tracing the frontier $η^{1-1/2t}$ (recovering Shen (2025) at $t=1$), whose gain is an explicit constant, capped by the pancake density and shown unimprovable by the degree-$2$ barrier.

AI.tldr: This paper characterizes the exact blind spot of bounded-degree outlier-removal certificates via the Christoffel function, and uses this inversion to organize the reweighted-hinge approach for robustly learning halfspaces, proving tight tradeoffs between margin, degree, and corruption tolerance.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental limits of bounded-degree certificates for outlier removal, specifically why certain corruption cannot be detected, and to develop tight theoretical guarantees for robust learning of γ-margin halfspaces under malicious noise.
AI.method: The paper inverts the Christoffel function (normally used for outlier detection) to measure the maximal corruption mass that can hide from a degree-2t certificate. It uses Sum-of-Squares (SoS) degree analysis, weighted-Chebyshev reductions, and constructs explicit adversarial instances to prove lower bounds.
AI.result: (1) A margin-degree tradeoff: certifying to error ε costs SoS degree Ω(log(1/ε)) or margin Ω(√log(1/ε)/√d). (2) A degree-2 barrier: degree 2 is stuck at η^{1/2} while degree 4 escapes it. (3) A degree-2t algorithm achieving frontier η^{1-1/2t} with explicit constants.
AI.conclusion: The Christoffel function precisely quantifies the blind spot of bounded-degree certificates. The reweighted-hinge approach's resource is SoS degree, and its resolution frontier (η^{1-1/2t}) is tight against a degree-2 barrier, explaining why the log(1/ε) margin in prior work is necessary.

title: Dissecting model behavior through agent trajectories
summary: AI agent performance is not just a modeling problem, it is fundamentally a systems problem. The advanced capabilities of models are realized through agent harnesses. Therefore, a gap between model assumptions and harness behavior can easily prevent the model's full capabilities from translating into agent performance. We formalize this as the `intent-execution' gap: the mismatch between what the model intends and what the harness executes, and vice versa. We argue that minimizing this intent-execution gap is as important as other aspects of harness design such as tools and execution loops. To illustrate the impact of this harness-model alignment, we develop a simple and customizable harness called `Simple Strands Agent' (SSA). SSA aims to find the bulk of common patterns which generalize across different model families (such as Claude, Gemini, GPT, Grok, Qwen), as well as a small number of model-specific preferences. We make two contributions: (i) we $\textbf{reproduce or improve on the pass@1}$ performance reported by diverse model-provider families on popular agentic benchmarks (SWE-Pro, SWE-Verified and Terminal-Bench-2), and (ii) building on an $\textbf{analysis of 138k trajectories generated by SSA}$, we look beyond the $\texttt{pass@1}$ numbers which tend to be relatively even across frontier models. By representing agent trajectories in code state-spaces, we observe model-level differences in problem-solving behavior. Finer-grained metrics such as edit frequency, testing activity, and phase-transitions reveal how individual models allocate effort across different stages of autonomous problem solving.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that AI agent performance is fundamentally a systems problem, introducing the "intent-execution gap" between model intent and harness behavior. They develop the Simple Strands Agent (SSA) harness to minimize this gap, reproduce/improve pass@1 on agentic benchmarks across model families, and analyze 138k trajectories to reveal model-level differences in problem-solving behavior beyond aggregate metrics.
AI.motivation: Current AI agent research focuses heavily on model capabilities while neglecting the harness (system) layer that mediates between models and their execution environment. The authors identify that a mismatch between what a model intends and what the harness actually executes creates an "intent-execution gap" that degrades agent performance, making this a critical but underexplored problem.
AI.method: The authors design a simple, customizable harness called Simple Strands Agent (SSA) that finds common patterns across model families (Claude, Gemini, GPT, Grok, Qwen) plus model-specific preferences. They benchmark pass@1 on SWE-Pro, SWE-Verified, and Terminal-Bench-2, and analyze 138k trajectories by representing agent trajectories in code state-spaces to measure finer-grained metrics like edit frequency, testing activity, and phase-transitions.
AI.result: (i) SSA reproduces or improves upon pass@1 performance reported by diverse model providers on popular agentic benchmarks. (ii) Analysis of 138k trajectories reveals meaningful model-level differences in problem-solving behavior through finer-grained metrics, whereas pass@1 scores tend to be relatively even across frontier models.
AI.conclusion: Minimizing the intent-execution gap through harness-model alignment is as important as other harness design aspects. Beyond aggregate pass@1 metrics, finer-grained behavioral analysis reveals how different models allocate effort across autonomous problem-solving stages, suggesting deeper evaluation methods are needed to understand agent capabilities.

title: DexLink Hand: A Compact, Affordable, 16-DOF Linkage-Driven Hand with Human-Like Dexterity
summary: Dexterous robotic hands face a longstanding trade-off among dexterity, compactness, and affordability. Particularly, high-degree-of-freedom designs typically demand complex actuation and transmission, hindering integration into human-scale forms. To address these challenges, this work presents a compact, low-cost linkage-driven anthropomorphic hand that achieves high dexterity, structural integration, and human-hand-like functionality. The hand integrates 20 joints driven by 16 independent actuators, with all actuation, sensing, and transmission components compactly embedded within a human-hand-sized structure. The resulting prototype weighs only 320g at a total cost below USD 400. To meet these objectives, a hybrid mechanical architecture combining planar and spatial linkage mechanisms is proposed, enabling decoupled multidirectional motion, biomimetic joint synergies, and high passive load-bearing capability. The thumb further incorporates biomimetic features supporting human-like reconfiguration and opposition movements. Through the coordinated integration of these mechanisms and structural layout, the prototype achieves a highly integrated design with anthropomorphic dexterity. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that the hand achieves the maximum Kapandji score, reproduces all 33 Feix grasp types, and performs stable grasping and dexterous manipulation across a wide variety of daily objects and tools. These results validate the proposed hand as an affordable, compact, and mechanically efficient platform for dexterous manipulation, teleoperation, and robot learning in human-centered environments.

AI.tldr: A compact, low-cost ($400, 320g) robotic hand with 20 joints and 16 actuators achieves high dexterity (max Kapandji score, all 33 Feix grasps) using a hybrid planar-spatial linkage mechanism embedded in a human-hand-sized structure.
AI.motivation: To overcome the trade-off among dexterity, compactness, and affordability in dexterous robotic hands, where high-DoF designs typically require complex actuation and transmission that hinders human-scale integration.
AI.method: A hybrid mechanical architecture combining planar and spatial linkage mechanisms enabling decoupled multidirectional motion, biomimetic joint synergies, and high passive load-bearing; biomimetic thumb for opposition movements; all components embedded within a human-hand-sized structure.
AI.result: 320g weight, under $400 cost, maximum Kapandji score, reproduction of all 33 Feix grasp types, stable grasping and dexterous manipulation across diverse daily objects and tools.
AI.conclusion: The proposed linkage-driven hand provides an affordable, compact, and mechanically efficient platform for dexterous manipulation, teleoperation, and robot learning in human-centered environments.

title: Complex Layout Classification in the Wild: A Low-Resource Approach with Layout-Preserving Augmentations
summary: Many digitized corpora suffer from low resources because annotations may be scarce, page scans are noisy and of poor resolution, or layouts are structurally complex in ways that negatively affect the quality of automatic transcription. Developing robust classification models for low-resource languages is inhibited by the lack of large-scale annotated data and by the frequent semantic complexity of page layouts. To this end, we have curated a complex-layout dataset, manually classified into eight distinct layout types based on their separator regions. To overcome data scarcity, we propose a novel training strategy in the form of a CNN-based classifier that employs strong, domain-aware augmentations to improve generalization. We utilize narrow anisotropic Gaussian masking to suppress incidental textual details while preserving essential separations, compelling the model to learn global geometric arrangements. Additionally, we implement reflection-induced label transformations to enrich the training distribution while maintaining label consistency across asymmetric categories. The results demonstrate that layout-specific augmentations can substantially improve page-level layout classification under severe annotation scarcity.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a CNN-based classifier with domain-aware augmentations (narrow anisotropic Gaussian masking and reflection-induced label transformations) for classifying complex page layouts in low-resource digitized corpora, achieving substantial improvements under severe annotation scarcity.
AI.motivation: Low-resource digitized corpora suffer from scarce annotations, noisy scans, poor resolution, and complex layouts, which inhibit robust classification model development, especially for low-resource languages.
AI.method: A CNN-based classifier using: (1) strong domain-aware augmentations, (2) narrow anisotropic Gaussian masking to suppress incidental text while preserving separations, and (3) reflection-induced label transformations to enrich training distribution while maintaining label consistency across asymmetric categories.
AI.result: Layout-specific augmentations substantially improve page-level layout classification under severe annotation scarcity.
AI.conclusion: Domain-aware augmentations are effective for overcoming data scarcity in complex-layout classification tasks for low-resource digitized corpora.

title: Rift: A Conflict Signature for Deception in Language Models
comment: 13 pages, 4 figures. Code and experiment logs: https://github.com/Omibranch/Rift
summary: A model that lies while knowing the truth is the central case ELK cannot handle with behavioral evaluation alone. We ask whether such deception leaves an internal signature distinguishing it from honest error. Our key move is a control for wrongness: we contrast a sleeper agent (knows the truth, lies on trigger) against a naive liar (fine-tuned to emit the same wrong answers with no honest training). Both produce identical wrong outputs; any difference is about knowledge conflict, not incorrectness. We find deceptive forward passes carry a conflict signature - 2.1-2.3x higher residual rank than naive-liar passes on the same wrong answer - strong enough to identify which of two responses is the lie with 100% accuracy and no labels, across GPT-2 small/medium (three seeds) and three instruct models. Across Qwen2.5-1.5B/7B and Phi-3-mini, instructed deception raises residual rank on every tested fact (18/18, 40/40, 34/34); on Phi-3, lies separate perfectly from both honest answers and hallucinations (AUC 1.0, Wilcoxon p~6e-11). The signature survives strategic self-constructed deception (model invents its own lie, AUC 1.0), active concealment attempts (AUC 1.0), and length-controlled replication (20/20, AUC 1.0, p~1e-6). Using basis-free relative representations, a probe trained on one model family detects deception in two other families zero-shot (mean AUC 0.933), surviving simultaneous architecture and format change (AUC 0.821), and transfers across five languages (AUC 1.000, length-controlled). The signature is read-only: detectable but not injectable (0/8 both directions). Honest limitations and six negative experiments are documented in full.

AI.tldr: Deceptive language models that know the truth but lie leave a detectable internal signature (higher residual rank) that can be used to identify lies with perfect accuracy, transfers across models, languages, and cannot be artificially injected.
AI.motivation: To address the "ELK" problem where a model might knowingly produce false outputs while behavioral evaluation alone cannot distinguish deception from honest error.
AI.method: Contrasts sleeper agents (know truth, lie on trigger) vs naive liars (fine-tuned to output same wrong answers without honest training), measuring residual rank differences in forward passes across multiple model families and settings.
AI.result: Deceptive passes show 2.1-2.3x higher residual rank. Achieves 100% accuracy identifying lies across GPT-2, Qwen2.5, Phi-3-mini; survives self-constructed lies, concealment attempts, length control; transfers zero-shot across model families (mean AUC 0.933) and languages (AUC 1.0); signature is read-only (not injectable).
AI.conclusion: Knowledge conflict in deceptive models leaves a robust, transferable, and non-injectable internal signature that can reliably identify lies without behavioral labels, offering a promising direction for AI safety.

title: Can LLMs Be CEOs? Benchmarking Strategic Resource Reallocation with Multi-Role Agent Simulation
summary: Evaluating the decision-making capabilities of large language models (LLMs) is a growing research priority, yet existing benchmarks focus on isolated cognitive tasks such as reasoning, knowledge retrieval, and economic rationality in stylized settings. These evaluations overlook the defining challenge of real executive decision-making: integrating conflicting recommendations from specialized stakeholders under information asymmetry, organizational constraints, and temporal dependencies. We introduce \textsc{CEO-Bench}, a multi-agent benchmark that evaluates LLMs on CEO-level strategic resource reallocation -- the process of redirecting capital across business units in a multi-round, constraint-rich organizational environment. In \textsc{CEO-Bench}, LLM agents receive conflicting advice from four role-conditioned C-suite advisors (CFO, CTO, COO, CMO), each with private signals and distinct priorities, and must synthesize these into a concrete allocation plan evaluated along four dimensions: role integration, conditional boldness, history-sensitive judgment, and plan validity. Experiments across five frontier models on 13 scenarios reveal that all models achieve high structural validity but diverge sharply on strategic calibration -- the hardest capability layer. We identify systematic failure modes including single-advisor capture, conservative default under ambiguity, and historical amnesia, and uncover a structural integration-boldness tradeoff: models that engage more deeply with conflicting perspectives tend to produce less decisive action. These findings delineate the current capability boundary of LLMs as organizational decision-makers and inform the design of future AI-assisted executive systems.

AI.tldr: CEO-Bench is a multi-agent benchmark testing LLMs on CEO-level strategic resource reallocation with conflicting C-suite advisors, revealing that while models achieve structural validity, they fail at strategic calibration due to single-advisor capture, conservative default, and historical amnesia.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for LLM decision-making focus on isolated cognitive tasks (reasoning, knowledge retrieval, economic rationality) and overlook the real challenge of executive decision-making: integrating conflicting recommendations from specialized stakeholders under information asymmetry, organizational constraints, and temporal dependencies.
AI.method: The authors propose CEO-Bench, a multi-agent benchmark where LLMs act as CEOs receiving conflicting advice from four role-conditioned C-suite advisors (CFO, CTO, COO, CMO) with private signals and distinct priorities. Models must synthesize these into a concrete allocation plan, evaluated along four dimensions: role integration, conditional boldness, history-sensitive judgment, and plan validity.
AI.result: Experiments across five frontier models on 13 scenarios show all models achieve high structural validity but diverge sharply on strategic calibration. Systematic failure modes include single-advisor capture, conservative default under ambiguity, and historical amnesia. A structural integration-boldness tradeoff is identified: models engaging more deeply with conflicting perspectives produce less decisive action.
AI.conclusion: The findings delineate the current capability boundary of LLMs as organizational decision-makers and inform the design of future AI-assisted executive systems.

title: Toward Quantum-Enhanced ISAC: Active-RIS-Aided Integrated Sensing and Communication with Rydberg Atomic Receivers
summary: In this paper, we investigate an active-RIS (ARIS)-aided integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system with Rydberg Atomic REceiver (RARE). Leveraging the magnitude-only and real-domain observation structure of RARE, we first derive a unified ISAC model, along with a closed-form Cramer-Rao bound (CRB) for direction-of-arrival (DoA) estimation. Based on this formulation, we propose a joint design of the {base station (BS)} beamforming and ARIS reflection coefficients to minimize the CRB under RARE-specific signal-to-interference-noise-ratio (SINR) and ARIS power constraints. To tackle the resulting highly non-convex problem, we develop an alternating optimization (AO) framework that combines semidefinite relaxation (SDR) for beamforming and a majorization-minimization (MM)-based approach for ARIS design. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed RARE-aware framework significantly outperforms conventional RF-based designs and achieves performance close to the radar-only benchmark, highlighting the potential of RARE for quantum-enhanced ISAC with ARIS.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a joint beamforming and ARIS reflection coefficient design for an active-RIS-aided ISAC system using Rydberg Atomic Receivers, minimizing CRB for DoA estimation under SINR and power constraints, and shows superior performance over conventional RF-based designs.
AI.motivation: To leverage the unique magnitude-only and real-domain observation structure of Rydberg Atomic Receivers (RARE) for enhanced integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) performance, addressing the limitations of conventional RF-based receiver designs.
AI.method: The authors derive a unified ISAC model with closed-form CRB for DoA estimation, then propose an alternating optimization (AO) framework combining semidefinite relaxation (SDR) for BS beamforming and majorization-minimization (MM) for ARIS reflection coefficient design.
AI.result: The RARE-aware framework outperforms conventional RF-based designs and achieves performance close to the radar-only benchmark.
AI.conclusion: RARE shows strong potential for quantum-enhanced ISAC systems when combined with active-RIS, offering superior sensing performance while maintaining communication constraints.

title: AnnotateAnything: Automatic Annotation of 3D Assets for Robot Manipulation
summary: Simulation enables scalable robot data collection, but raw 3D assets provide only geometry, lacking the semantic, interactive, and physical knowledge needed to specify where and how robots should act. In this work, we present AnnotateAnything, a general automatic annotation framework that converts passive 3D assets into manipulation-ready assets with structured, diverse, and executable manipulation labels. AnnotateAnything is built around two complementary pipelines. First, a unified visual-language annotation pipeline using vision-language reasoning to infer object semantics, interaction constraints, and 3D-grounded cues, providing human-prior guidance for identifying meaningful interaction regions. Second, a fully automatic and massively parallel physics annotation pipeline grounds these priors in each asset's geometry and physical constraints through candidate generation, geometry optimization and trajectory generation. This pipeline produces diverse and executable action annotations, including grasp poses, dexterous contacts, articulation waypoints, insertion directions, hanging affordances, and navigation targets. Using the generated annotations, we further build an asynchronous parallel simulation data-collection system across diverse objects, tasks, and robot embodiments. Experiments demonstrate that AnnotateAnything achieves superior annotation efficiency, data-collection efficiency, and task success rates over existing annotation and data-generation pipelines, while also supporting downstream tasks such as affordance detection, robotic VQA, and visual instruction finetuning. We provide project materials on the project page and plan to release the full code, annotations, and benchmark to facilitate future research. Videos, code, demo assets, and annotations are provided in supplementary materials Project page: https://tourmaline-caramel-169490.netlify.app.

AI.tldr: AnnotateAnything is an automatic annotation framework that converts raw 3D assets into manipulation-ready assets with structured, executable manipulation labels using two complementary pipelines: visual-language reasoning for semantic/interaction cues and physics-based annotation for generating diverse action labels (grasps, contacts, waypoints, etc.), enabling efficient simulation-based robot data collection.
AI.motivation: Raw 3D assets lack semantic, interactive, and physical knowledge needed to specify where and how robots should act in simulation. The goal is to automatically annotate 3D assets with manipulation-ready labels to enable scalable robot data collection.
AI.method: Two complementary pipelines: (1) Unified visual-language annotation pipeline using vision-language reasoning to infer object semantics, interaction constraints, and 3D-grounded cues. (2) Fully automatic massively parallel physics annotation pipeline that grounds priors through candidate generation, geometry optimization, and trajectory generation, producing diverse action annotations (grasp poses, dexterous contacts, articulation waypoints, insertion directions, hanging affordances, navigation targets).
AI.result: Superior annotation efficiency, data-collection efficiency, and task success rates compared to existing pipelines. Supports downstream tasks including affordance detection, robotic VQA, and visual instruction finetuning.
AI.conclusion: AnnotateAnything effectively bridges the gap between passive 3D assets and manipulation-ready simulation environments, enabling scalable robot learning through automatic, structured annotation.

title: DriveJudge: Rethinking Autonomous Driving Evaluation with Vision-Language Models
summary: Autonomous driving has shifted towards end-to-end policy learning, where reliable, interpretable policy evaluation is a fundamental challenge as driving quality is highly context-dependent. Commonly used rule-based driving metrics like EPDMS are interpretable but lack context-awareness, while recent VLMbased evaluations are context-aware but limited by ambiguous VLM outputs and weak physical grounding. To evaluate driving in a manner that is both interpretable and context-aware, we introduce DriveJudge. DriveJudge is a driving evaluation agent that combines rule-grounded evaluation with Vision-Language Model (VLM) reasoning and selectively invokes physically-grounded deterministic rule functions after interpreting the environmental context. To train and evaluate DriveJudge, we curate a large-scale dataset of 33,577 challenging driving samples with human annotations on whether the driving behavior is reasonable in the given scenario. With this dataset, we address the underexplored problem of driving metric evaluation, and introduce two human-aligned benchmark tasks: Driving Quality Classification and Trajectory Preference Selection. DriveJudge outperforms EPDMS for driving quality classification by 21.23 AUC, and the recent VLM-based DriveCritic for trajectory preference selection by 6.5%, setting a new standard for interpretable and precise driving evaluation.

AI.tldr: DriveJudge combines rule-grounded evaluation with VLM reasoning for context-aware, interpretable driving assessment, outperforming existing methods on driving quality classification and trajectory preference tasks using a new 33K-sample dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing driving evaluation methods either lack context-awareness (rule-based metrics like EPDMS) or lack interpretability and physical grounding (VLM-based evaluations). The authors aim to create an evaluation agent that is both interpretable and context-aware.
AI.method: DriveJudge is a driving evaluation agent that integrates VLM reasoning with rule-grounded deterministic functions. It selectively invokes physically-grounded rule functions after interpreting the environmental context. A large-scale dataset of 33,577 challenging driving samples with human annotations was curated for training and evaluation.
AI.result: DriveJudge achieves 21.23 AUC improvement over EPDMS for driving quality classification and 6.5% improvement over DriveCritic for trajectory preference selection.
AI.conclusion: DriveJudge sets a new standard for interpretable and precise driving evaluation by combining the strengths of rule-based metrics and VLM reasoning.

title: Uncertainty Quantification of Engineering Structures by Polynomial Chaos Expansion and Multivariate Active Learning
summary: In many engineering applications, a single high-fidelity model produces multiple quantities of interest (QoIs) under the same input parameters, e.g. finite element models of complex physical systems. To alleviate the high computational cost of direct model evaluations, surrogate models are widely used to construct efficient approximations of model responses. Naturally, the accuracy of surrogates strongly depends on the quality of the experimental design (ED). However, a single ED may not provide an adequate representation for all outputs simultaneously, especially when different outputs exhibit varying sensitivities to the input variables. A straightforward solution is to perform separate sampling for each output, but this results in increased sampling complexity and computational cost. From a statistical perspective, such an approach also ignores potential correlations among all outputs and may compromise data consistency. To address this issue, an adaptive sequential sampling method for constructing polynomial chaos expansion surrogate models is generalized for vector valued QoIs. The method sequentially selects new samples from a candidate pool based on their local contribution to the output variance, while balancing distance-based exploration of the input space and exploitation of aggregated variance information across all outputs. Its performance is compared with non-sequential Latin Hypercube Sampling through several numerical examples from engineering problems. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed strategy improves both surrogate accuracy and stability, and provides a more reliable estimation of second-order statistics.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an adaptive sequential sampling method for constructing polynomial chaos expansion surrogates for vector-valued quantities of interest, balancing exploration and exploitation across multiple outputs, and demonstrates improved accuracy and stability over Latin Hypercube Sampling.
AI.motivation: A single experimental design often fails to adequately represent multiple outputs with varying sensitivities to inputs. Separate sampling per output increases cost and ignores correlations among outputs, compromising data consistency.
AI.method: An adaptive sequential sampling method for polynomial chaos expansion surrogates generalized for vector-valued QoIs. It selects new samples from a candidate pool based on local contribution to output variance, balancing distance-based exploration and exploitation of aggregated variance across all outputs.
AI.result: Numerical results from engineering problems show the proposed strategy improves surrogate accuracy and stability, and provides more reliable estimation of second-order statistics compared to non-sequential Latin Hypercube Sampling.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive sequential sampling method effectively addresses multi-output surrogate modeling challenges by jointly considering all QoIs, yielding better performance than standard non-sequential designs.

title: LLM-as-Judge in Education: A Curriculum-Grounded Marking Pipeline
summary: Generative AI and large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to question generation and automated assessment. However, deploying LLMs in preparation for high-stakes exams requires more than prompt engineering; it demands software pipelines that systematically ground model outputs in authorised curriculum artefacts and marking guidelines issued by education authorities. This paper presents a curriculum-grounded, configurable LLM-as-Judge pipeline for question-level marking, co-developed with an industrial partner, to support exam preparation for university admission. The pipeline identifies the relevant topics, subtopics, and cognitive demand of a question, and assembles verifiable and authorised context to support LLM judgement. Curriculum intent is operationalised through concrete syllabus artefacts, including prescribed verbs and outcomes, performance band descriptors, glossary definitions, and marking-guideline principles. A staged LLM workflow is employed to first generate question-specific rubrics, capturing structured expectations of performance, and then derive and evaluate marking criteria used to allocate marks to student responses. This design improves consistency, transparency, and alignment with official marking practices. Preliminary evaluation shows that the proposed LLM-as-Judge pipeline delivers marking outcomes comparable to human tutors, while yielding justifications that are more traceable to authorised curriculum artefacts and marking standards. The pipeline has also been integrated into an online study platform, where early deployment data provide initial insights into operational usage and manual overrides.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a curriculum-grounded, configurable LLM-as-Judge pipeline for automated question-level marking in high-stakes exam preparation, co-developed with an industrial partner. The pipeline identifies topics, subtopics, and cognitive demand of questions, assembles authorised curriculum context, and uses a staged LLM workflow to generate rubrics and evaluate marking criteria. Preliminary evaluation shows results comparable to human tutors with more traceable justifications.
AI.motivation: Deploying LLMs for high-stakes exam preparation requires more than prompt engineering; it demands software pipelines that systematically ground model outputs in authorised curriculum artefacts and marking guidelines issued by education authorities.
AI.method: A curriculum-grounded, configurable LLM-as-Judge pipeline with staged LLM workflow: (1) identifies topics, subtopics, and cognitive demand; (2) assembles verifiable authorised context from syllabus artefacts (prescribed verbs, outcomes, performance band descriptors, glossary, marking guidelines); (3) generates question-specific rubrics; (4) derives and evaluates marking criteria to allocate marks to student responses.
AI.result: Preliminary evaluation shows marking outcomes comparable to human tutors, with justifications more traceable to authorised curriculum artefacts and marking standards. The pipeline has been integrated into an online study platform with early deployment data providing insights into operational usage and manual overrides.
AI.conclusion: The proposed design improves consistency, transparency, and alignment with official marking practices for high-stakes exam preparation.

title: Joint Synchronization and Radar Parameter Estimation for Distributed OFDM-ISAC Systems
summary: We propose a novel approach to the synchronization paradigm in distributed ISAC (DISAC) systems in doubly-dispersive (DD) channel environments via a joint synchronization and radar parameter estimation framework. The proposed method exploits the structure of the system model, which can be linearized in order to apply a bivariate Gaussian belief propagation (GaBP) algorithm that jointly estimates the time offset (TO) and carrier frequency offset (CFO) of each base station (BS), as well as the delay and Doppler parameters of the DD channel in conventional orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) systems. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm, showing that the radar parameter estimates (i.e., range and velocity) and synchronization parameter estimates (i.e., TO and CFO) approach the Cramér Rao lower bound (CRLB) even at low-to-moderate signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) regimes.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a joint synchronization and radar parameter estimation framework for distributed ISAC systems in doubly-dispersive channels, using a bivariate Gaussian belief propagation (GaBP) algorithm that jointly estimates time offset, carrier frequency offset, delay, and Doppler parameters in OFDM systems.
AI.motivation: To address the synchronization challenges in distributed ISAC systems operating in doubly-dispersive channel environments, where channel variations in both time and frequency pose difficulties for accurate parameter estimation.
AI.method: The authors exploit the system model's structure to linearize it, then apply a bivariate Gaussian belief propagation (GaBP) algorithm to jointly estimate time offset (TO), carrier frequency offset (CFO), delay, and Doppler parameters in conventional OFDM systems.
AI.result: Simulation results show that the proposed algorithm achieves radar parameter estimates (range and velocity) and synchronization parameter estimates (TO and CFO) approaching the Cramér Rao lower bound (CRLB), even at low-to-moderate SNR regimes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed joint estimation framework is effective for distributed ISAC systems in doubly-dispersive channels, providing near-optimal performance across both radar and synchronization tasks.

title: Continual Online Personalization of Exoskeleton Control via Manifold-Aware Experience Replay
summary: Personalizing exoskeleton control remains a critical challenge for clinical users with gait disabilities. Online adaptation (OA) offers an effective solution by adapting in real time to subject variability, device fit, and diverse locomotor tasks. However, OA involves a continual stream of user state data, which can lead to catastrophic forgetting of previously learned locomotor contexts. Here, we develop a manifold-aware experience replay-based online personalization framework designed to maintain user-specific representations across diverse tasks during OA of exoskeleton control. By replaying previously experienced tasks from a replay buffer, we preserve the personalized exoskeleton assistance across all learned tasks. Furthermore, we capture a gait manifold that distinguishes between different locomotor tasks, removing the need for explicit task labeling when selecting target replay bins. We evaluated our framework on emulated hemiplegic gait, which largely deviates from able-bodied patterns, across multiple forgetting scenarios with speed and incline transitions. Our manifold-aware replay framework achieved 40% and 60% improvements in torque and gait phase tracking accuracy, respectively, compared to a baseline framework without replay, which exhibited catastrophic forgetting during task transitions. This demonstrates that our proposed framework personalizes exoskeleton control in real time across diverse locomotor contexts in daily ambulation of clinical populations.

AI.tldr: A manifold-aware experience replay framework prevents catastrophic forgetting in online exoskeleton control personalization, improving torque tracking by 40% and gait phase tracking by 60% over baseline.
AI.motivation: Online adaptation for personalized exoskeleton control suffers from catastrophic forgetting of previously learned locomotor contexts due to continual streaming of user state data.
AI.method: A manifold-aware experience replay framework that replays past tasks from a replay buffer to preserve personalized assistance, using a gait manifold to distinguish locomotor tasks without explicit task labeling.
AI.result: Achieved 40% improvement in torque tracking and 60% improvement in gait phase tracking accuracy compared to baseline without replay, on emulated hemiplegic gait across speed and incline transitions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables real-time personalization of exoskeleton control across diverse locomotor contexts, mitigating catastrophic forgetting for clinical populations during daily ambulation.

title: Rethinking Groups in Critic-Free RLVR
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a central paradigm for post-training large language models. Existing critic-free RL methods typically generate a group of rollouts for the same question to estimate value baselines for advantage computation. However, this design suffers from data inefficiency, group synchronization barriers, and inflexibility with structured rollouts. In this work, we revisit the role of the ``group'' and show that its underlying function is not merely to estimate baselines but to prevent false penalties on negative samples. Building on this insight, we propose negative token filtering, a simple and effective strategy that enables stable single-rollout training. We apply it to two batch-level advantage methods, achieving comparable performance on reasoning tasks and stronger performance on agentic tasks relative to group-based RL techniques.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes negative token filtering, a method that enables stable single-rollout training for RL-based post-training of LLMs, achieving comparable or better performance than group-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing critic-free RL methods use group rollouts for advantage estimation, but this suffers from data inefficiency, synchronization issues, and lack of flexibility. The authors revisit the role of "group" in these methods.
AI.method: The authors propose negative token filtering, a strategy that enables stable single-rollout training by preventing false penalties on negative samples. They apply it to two batch-level advantage methods.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves comparable performance on reasoning tasks and stronger performance on agentic tasks relative to group-based RL techniques.
AI.conclusion: Negative token filtering is an effective alternative to group-based estimation, addressing data inefficiency and synchronization barriers while maintaining or improving performance.

title: SEAGym: An Evaluation Environment for Self-Evolving LLM Agents
summary: Self-evolving LLM-based agents improve mainly by changing their agent harness: the structured execution layer around a base model, including prompts, memory, tools, middleware, runtime state, and the model-tool interaction loop. Existing evaluations often reduce this process to isolated task scores or a single sequential curve, obscuring whether an update produces reusable improvement, overfits recent tasks, increases cost, or harms older behavior. We introduce SEAGym, an evaluation environment for measuring agent harness updates across training, validation, test, replay, and cost records. SEAGym turns Harbor-compatible benchmarks into dynamic self-evolution task sources with train batches, frozen update-validation, held-out ID and OOD transfer views, replay diagnostics, and saved snapshot and metric records. Instantiating SEAGym on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and HLE, we compare ACE, TF-GRPO, and AHE under a shared epoch/batch protocol. The results show that these evaluation views provide complementary signals about the evolution process: frequent updates may fail to improve held-out performance, useful intermediate snapshots may collapse later, and source diversity and model backend can affect harness reliability.

AI.tldr: SEAGym is a new evaluation environment for measuring self-evolving LLM-based agent harness updates across multiple dimensions (training, validation, test, replay, cost), revealing that frequent updates can fail to improve held-out performance and useful intermediate snapshots may collapse later.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of self-evolving LLM agents reduce the process to isolated task scores or single sequential curves, obscuring whether updates produce reusable improvements, overfit recent tasks, increase costs, or harm older behaviors.
AI.method: SEAGym turns Harbor-compatible benchmarks into dynamic self-evolution task sources with train batches, frozen update-validation, held-out ID and OOD transfer views, replay diagnostics, and saved snapshot/metric records. It was instantiated on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and HLE, comparing ACE, TF-GRPO, and AHE under a shared epoch/batch protocol.
AI.result: Evaluation views provide complementary signals about the evolution process: frequent updates may fail to improve held-out performance, useful intermediate snapshots may collapse later, and source diversity and model backend can affect harness reliability.
AI.conclusion: SEAGym enables more comprehensive and diagnostic evaluation of agent harness updates, revealing nuanced trade-offs not captured by traditional single-metric evaluations.

title: BASIIS: Bistatic Angular Sampling and Interpolation for ISAC Setups
summary:
Integrated Sensing and Communications (ISAC) is a defining feature of 6G, extending cellular networks with radar-like sensing at limited additional overhead. In bistatic deployments, sensing requires coordinating the transmitter (TX) and receiver (RX) arrays to scan the Cartesian product of angle of departure and arrival, resulting in a four-dimensional sampling problem in the angular domain.
  This work establishes a complete angular sampling framework for bistatic ISAC, extending the DFT-based optimal-sampling methodology to the full azimuth and elevation domains of both arrays. We show that the bistatic geometry couples the TX and RX elevation angles, and represent this coupling through the ortho-baseline coarray, a virtual array that captures the joint elevation aperture of the array pair. From the coarray we derive a minimal sampling and interpolation scheme, near-lossless and realizable with any beamforming architecture. Monte Carlo simulations confirm the proposed minimal acquisition essentially equalizes the detection accuracy of dense oversampled imaging while acquiring 3 to 5 times fewer TX-RX direction pairs. This allows having bistatic operations with drastically reduced overhead on the radio resource usage of ISAC systems.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a complete angular sampling framework for bistatic ISAC, using the ortho-baseline coarray to derive a minimal sampling scheme that reduces TX-RX direction pairs by 3-5x while preserving detection accuracy.
AI.motivation: Bistatic ISAC sensing requires scanning 4D angular space (azimuth/elevation at both TX and RX), which is resource-intensive. The authors aim to reduce this overhead while maintaining sensing performance.
AI.method: Extends DFT-based optimal sampling to full azimuth/elevation domains; introduces the ortho-baseline coarray to represent TX-RX elevation coupling; derives minimal sampling and interpolation scheme from the coarray.
AI.result: Monte Carlo simulations show the minimal acquisition achieves detection accuracy comparable to dense oversampled imaging while using 3-5x fewer TX-RX direction pairs.
AI.conclusion: Bistatic ISAC operations can be performed with drastically reduced overhead on radio resource usage, realizable with any beamforming architecture.

title: Embodiment Shapes Rolling Behavior in a Multimodal Infant Model
comment: 7 pages, 7 figures. Accepted at the 2026 IEEE ICDL Conference. Cite as: L. Philipp, F. M. López, and J. Triesch, "Embodiment Shapes Rolling Behavior in a Multimodal Infant Model", in 2026 IEEE International Conference on Development and Learning (ICDL). IEEE, 2026, pp. 1-7
summary: Rolling over is one of the earliest milestones in infant motor development, reflecting the emergence of coordinated, whole-body sensorimotor control. Here, we conduct a computational study of infant rolling using MIMo, a virtual infant embodiment equipped with proprioception and vestibular sensation. MIMo learns supine-to-prone rolls with reinforcement learning. Interestingly, the learned behaviors capture developmental trends and coordination patterns consistent with those reported in real infants, including improved performance and faster execution with age. Our results explain how infant capabilities and constraints can give rise to realistic behaviors in artificial agents, with a particular emphasis on how motor development is shaped by the changing body morphology. This work highlights the role of embodied computational models as a powerful tool for studying sensorimotor development.

AI.tldr: This paper uses a virtual infant robot (MIMo) with reinforcement learning to simulate infant rolling over, showing that the learned behaviors match real infant development patterns including age-related improvements in performance and speed.
AI.motivation: To understand how infant motor development, specifically rolling over, emerges from sensorimotor coordination and changing body morphology, using computational modeling as a research tool.
AI.method: The authors use MIMo, a virtual infant embodiment with proprioception and vestibular sensation, trained via reinforcement learning to perform supine-to-prone rolls.
AI.result: The learned rolling behaviors capture developmental trends seen in real infants, such as improved performance and faster execution with age, and show realistic coordination patterns.
AI.conclusion: Embodied computational models are powerful tools for studying sensorimotor development, as they can reproduce realistic infant behaviors and reveal how changing body morphology shapes motor development.

title: Improving and Evaluating Hand-Object Interaction Detection
summary: Understanding hands and the objects they interact with, both directly and through tools, is a key step for tasks ranging from action perception to 3D reconstruction and robotics. Our paper provides several contributions to the Hand-Object Interaction (HOI) understanding literature: (1) HOI-DETR, a new framework that introduces hand-object and object-object interactions to the Co-DETR architecture to produce a state-of-the-art method; (2) a comprehensive HOI evaluation suite of 4 diverse datasets, including a video benchmark derived from the HD-EPIC dataset and fresh annotations that improve the Hands23 benchmark and (3) a trained checkpoint that significantly improves the state of the art across Hands23, HOIST, FineBio, and HD-EPIC, including mAP gains of over 20 percentage points on Hands23 and FineBio. Our ablations confirm the contributions of each model component.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HOI-DETR, a new framework enhancing Hand-Object Interaction understanding through hand-object and object-object interactions in Co-DETR architecture, achieving over 20 percentage point mAP gains across multiple benchmarks including Hands23, HOIST, FineBio, and HD-EPIC.
AI.motivation: Understanding hand-object interactions (including tool use) is crucial for action perception, 3D reconstruction, and robotics, motivating a more comprehensive and accurate HOI understanding framework.
AI.method: HOI-DETR framework integrates hand-object and object-object interactions into the Co-DETR architecture, supported by a new HOI evaluation suite including 4 datasets (with a video benchmark from HD-EPIC and improved Hands23 annotations).
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results across all four datasets, with over 20 percentage point mAP improvements on Hands23 and FineBio, plus significant gains on HOIST and HD-EPIC.
AI.conclusion: HOI-DETR substantially advances HOI understanding, and ablation studies confirm the contributions of each model component to the overall performance.

title: ProCUA-SFT Technical Report
summary: Training computer-use agents (CUAs) -- models that interact with graphical desktops through screenshots and keyboard/mouse actions -- requires large-scale, diverse trajectory data collected in full desktop environments. The largest public resource, AgentNet (22.5K human trajectories), leads to negative transfer when used for supervised fine-tuning (SFT): continuing training UI-TARS 7B on AgentNet causes OSWorld success rate to fall from 26.3% to 8-10%. We present ProCUA-SFT, a dataset of 3.1M step-level SFT samples distilled from 93K synthetic trajectories across 2,484 application combinations. The dataset is produced by a fully automated pipeline that (i) synthesizes grounded tasks on live desktops seeded with real-world content -- 912 spreadsheets from SpreadsheetBench, approximately 10K permissively-licensed presentations from Zenodo10K, and multi-application OSWorld configs -- and (ii) verifies each task's feasibility through binary precondition checking before rollout. A single VLM (Kimi-K2.5) serves as goal generator, precondition judge, and trajectory executor, eliminating planner-actor capability gaps. Each trajectory is expanded into step-prefix samples that exactly reproduce the context layout seen at inference time. Fine-tuning UI-TARS 7B on ProCUA-SFT for one epoch yields 45.0% on OSWorld -- an 18.7 percentage-point improvement over the base model and over 35% above AgentNet-trained counterparts. A subset of ProCUA was incorporated into the training data for the Nemotron 3 Nano Omni model, contributing to its computer-use capabilities.

AI.tldr: ProCUA-SFT is a 3.1M sample dataset distilled from 93K synthetic trajectories across 2,484 app combinations, created via a fully automated pipeline that generates grounded tasks on live desktops with real-world content. Fine-tuning UI-TARS 7B on it achieves 45.0% on OSWorld — an 18.7% improvement over the base model and 35% above models trained on the existing AgentNet dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing public trajectory data (AgentNet, 22.5K human trajectories) causes negative transfer in SFT for computer-use agents, actually decreasing performance when fine-tuning models like UI-TARS 7B (OSWorld success rate drops from 26.3% to 8-10%). A larger, higher-quality dataset is needed.
AI.method: ProCUA-SFT is produced by a fully automated pipeline that: (i) synthesizes grounded tasks on live desktops seeded with real-world content (912 spreadsheets, ~10K presentations, multi-application OSWorld configs), (ii) verifies task feasibility via binary precondition checking, and (iii) uses a single VLM (Kimi-K2.5) as goal generator, precondition judge, and trajectory executor. Each trajectory is expanded into step-prefix samples reproducing inference-time context layout.
AI.result: Fine-tuning UI-TARS 7B on ProCUA-SFT for one epoch achieves 45.0% on OSWorld — an 18.7 percentage-point improvement over the base model and over 35% above AgentNet-trained counterparts. A subset was incorporated into Nemotron 3 Nano Omni's training data.
AI.conclusion: ProCUA-SFT demonstrates that large-scale synthetic trajectory data with automated verification can substantially outperform human-collected data for training computer-use agents, resolving the negative transfer problem observed with existing datasets.

title: DeepInsight: A Unified Evaluation Infrastructure Across the Physical AI Stack
summary: Evaluating a Physical AI stack spans operators that differ by more than three orders of magnitude -- from a single foundation-model decoding step to thousands of physics ticks of whole-body control -- varying orthogonally in modality, reward semantics, and resource profile. No existing framework spans this range, so the stack is evaluated today by stitching together separate harnesses that share neither runtime nor scoring, preserving each segment's local validity but losing the shared identity needed to diagnose cross-layer regressions. We present DeepInsight, an evaluation infrastructure that serves this full spectrum on a single runtime. Rather than homogenize the regimes, it preserves their heterogeneity behind three narrow abstractions -- task, resource, and result -- each realized as one invariant shared by every subsystem: one episode driver, one resource-handle protocol implemented by every expensive backend (LLM inference and sandboxed runtimes alike), and one trace identity scheme under which every event is written. Deployed in production across all three layers of an embodied humanoid stack, this single set of invariants onboards new benchmarks largely by configuration. Where mature peer orchestrators exist -- at the foundation-model end -- it reproduces published references and peer-framework readings within their own spread, runs the same suites faster on a single node, and scales near-linearly across nodes. Its distinctive return is diagnostic: because every layer writes into one shared trace, a regression that begins in one layer and surfaces in another stays localizable on that trace -- a cross-layer payoff no federation of per-segment harnesses can reproduce.

AI.tldr: DeepInsight is a unified evaluation infrastructure that bridges three orders of magnitude in operators across Physical AI stacks (from LLM decoding steps to whole-body physics ticks) using three shared abstractions (task, resource, result), enabling cross-layer diagnosis that per-segment harnesses cannot achieve.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation frameworks cannot span the full range of operators in Physical AI stacks (differing by >3 orders of magnitude in scale, modality, reward semantics, and resource profile), forcing practitioners to stitch together separate harnesses that preserve local validity but lose the shared identity needed for diagnosing cross-layer regressions.
AI.method: DeepInsight preserves heterogeneity behind three narrow abstractions shared by every subsystem: (1) one episode driver (task), (2) one resource-handle protocol for all expensive backends (resource), and (3) one trace identity scheme for every event (result). It is deployed across all three layers of an embodied humanoid stack in production.
AI.result: Onboards new benchmarks largely by configuration; reproduces published references and peer-framework readings within their own spread; runs same suites faster on a single node; scales near-linearly across nodes; enables cross-layer regression diagnosis via a single shared trace that federated harnesses cannot reproduce.
AI.conclusion: DeepInsight provides a practical, scalable evaluation infrastructure for Physical AI stacks that maintains heterogeneous regimes while unifying them under minimal invariants, with the distinctive payoff being cross-layer diagnostic capability that no federation of per-segment harnesses can offer.

title: Deep CSI Feedback for FDD Massive MIMO Systems: A Curvelet Learning Approach
summary: Downlink channel state information (CSI) feedback plays a key role in frequency division duplex (FDD) massive multiple-input multiple-output (mMIMO) systems. The growth of antennas in ultra-massive MIMO increases the difficulty and overhead of CSI feedback, which poses significant challenges for conventional downlink CSI feedback mechanisms. To address the limitations of existing CSI feedback approaches, this paper proposes a novel curvelet learning based framework termed SwinCANet, comprising a frequency-domain information processing module and a denoising module. The frequency-domain information processing module employs curvelet transform to decompose CSI into low-frequency and high-frequency components. Subsequently, Swin Transformer and channel-wise attention block are utilized for extracting the low-frequency and high-frequency representations, respectively, thereby enhancing reconstruction quality. Notably, an additional Swin Transformer facilitates the fusion of multi-scale frequency components, enhancing capabilities across different angular resolutions and spatial directions. Furthermore, we develop a variant (De-SwinCANet), which employs a Sigmoid threshold function to effectively suppress noise coefficients, thereby mitigating various channel impairments and nonlinear distortions. Numerical simulation results demonstrate that the proposed methodology achieves superior performance compared to existing benchmarks while maintaining robust performance under challenging propagation conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes SwinCANet, a curvelet learning-based framework for downlink CSI feedback in FDD massive MIMO systems. It uses curvelet transform to decompose CSI into frequency components, processes them with Swin Transformer and channel-wise attention, and includes a denoising variant (De-SwinCANet) using Sigmoid threshold function. Results show superior performance over existing benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Conventional downlink CSI feedback mechanisms face significant challenges due to the growth of antennas in ultra-massive MIMO, which increases the difficulty and overhead of CSI feedback. Existing CSI feedback approaches have limitations in handling these challenges.
AI.method: SwinCANet framework with: (1) frequency-domain information processing module using curvelet transform to decompose CSI into low/high-frequency components; (2) Swin Transformer for low-frequency and channel-wise attention for high-frequency extraction; (3) additional Swin Transformer for multi-scale frequency component fusion; (4) De-SwinCANet variant with Sigmoid threshold function for noise suppression.
AI.result: The proposed methodology achieves superior performance compared to existing benchmarks and maintains robust performance under challenging propagation conditions.
AI.conclusion: The curvelet learning-based SwinCANet framework effectively addresses limitations of existing CSI feedback approaches, providing enhanced reconstruction quality and robustness for ultra-massive MIMO systems.

title: When Robots Sleep: Offline Skill Consolidation for Shared-Policy Robot Learning
summary: Robots that learn over long deployments must add new skills without losing the shared policy structure that makes earlier skills reusable. We study sequential robot skill learning, where previous trajectories and task losses may be unavailable, and the deployed policy must remain a single shared controller without task-specific heads, routing, or adapters. We identify skill-coupling collapse, a failure mode in which individual skill success remains non-trivial while reliability among related skills deteriorates. We propose Sleeping Robots, a wake-sleep framework that learns each new skill during wake and consolidates the shared policy offline during sleep using compact frozen skill memories: frozen critics with unordered state buffers for reinforcement learning and frozen actor snapshots with unordered observation buffers for imitation learning.
