This reliance limits their ability to acquire fine-grained manipulation skills beyond the demonstration distribution and prevents them from continuously improving through real-world interaction. To address these limitations, we propose WAM-RL, a reinforcement learning framework that enables joint optimization of the world model and the action model through online interaction with the environment. By allowing the two components to co-evolve, our approach enhances fine-grained control and adaptability. Specifically, a WA model consists of a world model and an actor. We design a tailored reinforcement learning method with hierarchical optimization to coordinate their improvement. On the methodological side, we systematically investigate the effects of applying reinforcement learning to the action model, as well as online training of the world model within an RL setting. Our experiments reveal a key insight: optimizing only the actor yields improvements on short-horizon tasks, but fails to provide significant gains on long-horizon tasks. In contrast, jointly optimizing both the world model and the actor is critical for achieving strong performance in long-horizon settings. Our work is the first to introduce reinforcement learning into the World-Action paradigm, and provides insights into how online optimization of both the action head and the world model impacts overall performance.

AI.tldr: WAM-RL introduces reinforcement learning to jointly optimize both the world model and action model in World-Action paradigms, enabling online improvement beyond expert demonstrations, with key finding that joint optimization is critical for long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing WA models rely on expert trajectories for training, which limits their ability to acquire fine-grained manipulation skills beyond demonstration distribution and prevents continuous improvement through real-world interaction.
AI.method: Proposes WAM-RL, a reinforcement learning framework with hierarchical optimization that jointly optimizes the world model and action model through online interaction, using tailored RL methods to coordinate their co-evolution.
AI.result: Optimizing only the actor improves short-horizon tasks but fails on long-horizon tasks; jointly optimizing both world model and actor is critical for strong long-horizon performance.
AI.conclusion: This is the first work to introduce RL into the World-Action paradigm, providing insights into how online optimization of both components impacts overall performance.

title: Contact-Based Fringe Projection Profilometry for High-Resolution 3-D Surface Measurement of Reflective and Transparent Objects
summary: This paper presents a contact-based 3-D surface measurement method based on a Digital Fringe Projection (DFP) system, belonging to the vision-based tactile sensing family pioneered by the commercially successful GelSight sensor. Such sensors have proven effective for robotic fingertip manipulation and contact sensing. However, because GelSight employs photometric stereo with RGB LEDs, it does not measure absolute depth directly but instead infers it by integrating estimated surface gradients, which can accumulate reconstruction errors; in addition, it becomes increasingly difficult to calibrate as the sensing area grows, and its depth accuracy is challenged on highly reflective or transparent objects. To overcome these drawbacks, we propose a fringe-projection-based contact measurement technique that performs triangulation-based 3-D reconstruction on a coated silicone contact surface, providing dense per-pixel surface geometry and full-field 3-D shape measurement over the contact region. By integrating high-accuracy digital fringe projection into the sensor, our approach simplifies calibration over larger areas and enhances depth precision for complex surfaces. Experimental results, including a direct comparison with a GelSight Mini sensor, a sphere-fitting accuracy evaluation, and an uncertainty analysis, confirm that the proposed method significantly improves the accuracy and stability of structured-light-based 3-D measurements, allowing reliable reconstruction of objects with diverse optical properties.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a fringe-projection-based contact sensing method (DFP) that improves upon GelSight's photometric stereo approach by using triangulation for direct 3-D reconstruction, offering better accuracy, simpler calibration, and robust performance on reflective/transparent objects.
AI.motivation: GelSight sensors suffer from accumulated reconstruction errors due to gradient integration, calibration difficulties on larger areas, and poor depth accuracy on reflective or transparent objects.
AI.method: A Digital Fringe Projection (DFP) system is integrated into a contact-based tactile sensor, performing triangulation-based 3-D reconstruction on a coated silicone contact surface instead of relying on photometric stereo.
AI.result: Compared with GelSight Mini, the proposed method shows improved accuracy and stability in sphere-fitting and uncertainty analysis, and successfully reconstructs objects with diverse optical properties.
AI.conclusion: Fringe-projection-based contact sensing significantly enhances depth precision and calibration simplicity over GelSight-style sensors, enabling reliable 3-D measurement for complex surfaces.

title: Operator Boosting Produces Pareto-Efficient PDE Surrogates
comment: 19 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Preprint submitted to Elsevier
summary: Neural operators are widely used as surrogate solution maps for partial differential equations (PDEs), but full-size models can be costly to store, deploy, and evaluate in many-query scientific workflows. This work introduces Operator Boosting, a stagewise residual-learning framework for constructing compact neural-operator surrogates directly, rather than training a large model and compressing it afterward. Starting from the empirical mean predictor in normalized output coordinates, the method trains a sequence of tiny same-family neural operators on residual fields and incorporates each correction through validation-selected shrinkage. We instantiate the framework with Fourier neural operators (FNOs), DeepONets, and convolutional neural operators (CNOs), and compare boosted tiny stacks against full-size monolithic baselines across one-, two-, and three-dimensional PDE benchmarks from PDEBench, APEBench, and The Well. Across 30 dataset-architecture pairs, 21 show positive mean accuracy gains and 17 have positive confidence intervals, while all boosted stacks reduce trainable parameter count by approximately 72-95%. Best-model comparisons show empirical Pareto improvements on 7 of 10 completed PDE benchmarks, including two-dimensional Navier-Stokes, shallow-water dynamics, Darcy flow, one-dimensional transport and reaction systems, and three-dimensional compressible Navier-Stokes. These results show that Operator Boosting often improves the empirical accuracy-parameter Pareto frontier of neural PDE surrogates, while also exposing PDE- and architecture-dependent regimes where residual boosting fails to offset compression.

AI.tldr: Operator Boosting is a stagewise residual-learning framework that builds compact neural operator surrogates for PDEs by sequentially training tiny models on residual fields, achieving 72-95% parameter reduction while often improving accuracy over full-size models across diverse benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Full-size neural operator models for PDE surrogates are costly to store, deploy, and evaluate in many-query scientific workflows, necessitating more compact alternatives that avoid the traditional "train large then compress" paradigm.
AI.method: A stagewise residual-learning framework that starts with an empirical mean predictor and sequentially trains tiny same-family neural operators (FNOs, DeepONets, CNOs) on residual fields, incorporating each correction via validation-selected shrinkage.
AI.result: Across 30 dataset-architecture pairs, 21 show positive mean accuracy gains, 17 have positive confidence intervals, boosted stacks reduce parameters by 72-95%, and empirical Pareto improvements on 7 of 10 PDE benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Operator Boosting often improves the accuracy-parameter Pareto frontier for neural PDE surrogates, though effectiveness depends on the specific PDE and architecture.

title: WallZero: Mastering the Game of WallGo with Strategic Analysis
comment: Accepted by the Computers and Games conference (CG 2026)
summary: WallGo is a recently introduced strategic board game popularized by the 2025 Netflix series The Devil's Plan. Although played on a small 7 x 7 board, its combination of stone movement and wall placement yields high game-tree complexity and intricate strategic interactions. Despite its growing popularity, WallGo remains underexplored. This paper presents WallZero, an AlphaZero-based agent for the two-player WallGo setting. We introduce tailored action and feature designs to improve playing performance significantly. In the evaluation, WallZero defeats two professional Go players who participated in this study, securing on average 1.98x more territory per game. Beyond its strength, we use WallZero to assess game fairness and identify key strategies for mastering WallGo. Interestingly, our results show that the opening used in the Netflix series yields a more balanced game. Our code is available at https://rlg.iis.sinica.edu.tw/papers/wallzero.

AI.tldr: This paper presents WallZero, an AlphaZero-based AI agent for the board game WallGo (from the Netflix series The Devil's Plan). WallZero defeats professional Go players by 1.98x more territory, and analysis shows the Netflix series' opening strategy yields more balanced gameplay.
AI.motivation: WallGo is a recently popularized strategic board game that remains underexplored despite its high game-tree complexity and intricate strategic interactions.
AI.method: The authors develop WallZero, an AlphaZero-based agent with tailored action and feature designs specifically adapted for WallGo's unique mechanics combining stone movement and wall placement.
AI.result: WallZero defeats two professional Go players, securing on average 1.98x more territory per game. Analysis reveals the opening used in the Netflix series yields more balanced gameplay.
AI.conclusion: WallZero demonstrates strong performance and provides insights into game fairness and key strategies, with code publicly available.

title: PearlVLA: Progressive Embodied Action-Plan Refinement in Latent Space
summary: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models face a trade-off between efficient action generation and explicit deliberation. Directly decoding actions from vision-language backbone representations enables low-latency control, whereas explicit reasoning through textual chains, pixel-level subgoals, or action search can improve planning but incurs substantial latency and computational cost. We propose PearlVLA, a VLA framework that moves deliberation into the latent space of a vision-language model (VLM). PearlVLA separates VLM meta-query representations into a fixed visual grounding branch and an iterative latent plan branch. At each refinement round, a plan-conditioned world query probes a lightweight frozen latent world model for an action-free future observation latent, which is fed back to guide plan refinement. A future-guided RefineNet then applies scheduled residual updates to progressively refine a coarse semantic draft into a fine-grained latent action plan. The refined plan after K rounds is then decoded in parallel into an action chunk for low-latency execution. We further introduce Causal Refinement-Grouped Process-Reward RL to optimize the latent refinement process with rewards from longer-horizon imagined futures induced by latent plan edits. Empirical evaluations on the LIBERO benchmark demonstrate that PearlVLA achieves state-of-the-art performance among existing methods.

AI.tldr: PearlVLA introduces latent-space deliberation for VLA models, separating visual grounding from iterative latent plan refinement guided by a frozen world model, achieving SOTA on LIBERO.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models face a trade-off: direct action decoding is fast but lacks deliberation, while explicit reasoning (text chains, subgoals, action search) improves planning but incurs high latency and computation. PearlVLA aims to bring deliberation into latent space to get the best of both worlds.
AI.method: (1) Separate VLM meta-query into visual grounding branch and iterative latent plan branch. (2) Use a plan-conditioned world query to probe a frozen latent world model for future observation latents. (3) Refine coarse semantic drafts into fine-grained latent action plans via a future-guided RefineNet with scheduled residual updates. (4) Introduce Causal Refinement-Grouped Process-Reward RL to optimize latent refinement using rewards from longer-horizon imagined futures.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on the LIBERO benchmark among existing methods, demonstrating effective latent-space deliberation without sacrificing efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Moving deliberation into latent space is a viable and effective approach for VLA models, enabling both planning quality and low-latency execution.

title: WeaveLA: Event Driven Cross-Subtask Latent Memory Weaving for Repetitive Robot Manipulation
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies have achieved remarkable single-step manipulation, yet they remain brittle precisely where each stage depends on what was just completed. The core issue is structural: short-window VLAs lack an explicit channel for rouxting information across sub-task boundaries, and existing memory-augmented variants either write at every frame, retrieve from demonstration-time stages, or fire at sub-goal events without performing an explicit sub-task-to-sub-task hand-off into the action expert. We identify the sub-goal completion event as the natural temporal unit for cross-subtask memory hand-off, and present WeaveLA (Weave Latent memory for Vision-Language-Action policies), a cross-subtask memory interface that, on top of a frozen VLA backbone, compresses each completed segment into latent tokens via query-driven attention pooling and routes them directly into the action-generation path of the next sub-task. This event-triggered, action-side design preserves the base policy's short-window interface while adding a lightweight cross-subtask channel. Through stratified evaluation on RoboMME with a $π_{0.5}$ backbone, WeaveLA's gains land exactly where the channel is needed: on the hardest repetition slice (SwingXtimes, $N{=}3$), success rises from $0\%$ to $47.8\%$, while single-execution episodes remain unchanged. Per-episode paired analysis confirms the gains are confined to tasks whose causal structure requires cross-subtask information.

AI.tldr: WeaveLA adds a lightweight cross-subtask memory channel to frozen VLA policies, compressing completed sub-task segments into latent tokens and routing them into the next sub-task's action path. On hard multi-step repetition tasks (SwingXtimes N=3), success jumps from 0% to 47.8% without degrading single-step performance.
AI.motivation: Short-window VLA policies fail on multi-step tasks because they lack an explicit channel for routing information across sub-task boundaries. Existing memory-augmented variants are inadequate—they write at every frame, retrieve from demonstration-time stages, or fire at sub-goal events without explicit hand-off to the action expert.
AI.method: WeaveLA operates on top of a frozen VLA backbone. It uses event-triggered (sub-goal completion) query-driven attention pooling to compress completed segments into latent tokens, then routes these tokens directly into the action-generation path of the next sub-task. This preserves the short-window interface while adding a lightweight cross-subtask channel.
AI.result: On RoboMME with a π₀.₅ backbone, WeaveLA improves the hardest repetition slice (SwingXtimes, N=3) from 0% to 47.8% success rate, while single-execution episodes remain unchanged. Per-episode paired analysis confirms gains are confined to tasks requiring cross-subtask information.
AI.conclusion: The sub-goal completion event is the natural temporal unit for cross-subtask memory hand-off. WeaveLA's event-triggered, action-side design effectively addresses the structural brittleness of VLA policies in multi-step manipulation without harming single-step performance.

title: ResAware: Cross-Environment Website Fingerprinting via Resource-Privileged Distillation
summary: While Website Fingerprinting (WF) attacks achieve high accuracy in controlled laboratory settings, they often degrade substantially in real-world environments due to spatio-temporal drift, browser heterogeneity, proxy obfuscation and etc. This limitation stems from their sole reliance on low-level traffic features that are noisy and highly sensitive to environmental perturbations. To address this problem, we propose \textbf{ResAware}, a cross-environment resource-aware distillation framework under a \textit{training-rich/inference-poor} asymmetric setting. Specifically, ResAware trains a teacher model on resource-level features, and then distills the resulting privileged knowledge into a student model through heterogeneous knowledge distillation. At deployment time, the student model performs inference using only encrypted traffic, incurring zero additional cost. We evaluate ResAware on a large-scale dataset collected over five months from six globally distributed vantage points, comprising more than $160{,}000$ paired samples. The results show that ResAware significantly enhances the cross-environment robustness of diverse WF baselines. Under a 150-day temporal drift, for example, ResAware improves the F1-score of Var-CNN from $72.77\%$ to $81.49\%$ and the open-world $TPR@1\%FPR$ from $22.40\%$ to $27.20\%$. Our results demonstrate that resource-level supervision improves WF robustness without expanding online observation capabilities.

AI.tldr: ResAware is a resource-aware distillation framework that improves Website Fingerprinting robustness across environments by training a teacher on resource-level features and distilling knowledge to a traffic-only student model.
AI.motivation: Existing WF attacks degrade in real-world environments due to spatio-temporal drift, browser heterogeneity, and proxy obfuscation, as they rely solely on noisy, low-level traffic features susceptible to environmental perturbations.
AI.method: ResAware adopts a training-rich/inference-poor asymmetric setting: a teacher model is trained on privileged resource-level features, and heterogeneous knowledge distillation transfers this knowledge to a student model that uses only encrypted traffic at inference time, incurring zero additional cost.
AI.result: Evaluated on a large-scale five-month dataset from six global vantage points (160,000+ paired samples), ResAware significantly improves cross-environment robustness. Under 150-day temporal drift, Var-CNN F1-score improves from 72.77% to 81.49%, and open-world TPR@1%FPR from 22.40% to 27.20%.
AI.conclusion: Resource-level supervision enhances WF robustness without expanding online observation capabilities, effectively addressing real-world WF degradation.

title: A homotopy-type-theoretic generalization of neurosymbolic inference
summary: A wide range of neurosymbolic (NeSy) systems compute one functional: a belief-weighted sum of a logical quantity over a space of $σ$-structures, of which weighted model counting, fuzzy logic, and probabilistic logic are special cases. This account is built on sets, and a set deliberately forgets two things that are important for NeSy: when two $σ$-structures are the same up to a symmetry of the theory, and how many distinct proofs witness a query. Replacing the underlying sets by types, in the sense of homotopy type theory, preserves this information, and turns this functional into a belief-weighted homotopy cardinality, a notion of size that counts each object in inverse proportion to its symmetries. We develop the framework from scratch for NeSy systems, prove a conservativity theorem that recovers the classical functional when symmetries are trivial, and show that the symmetry our framework exposes is exactly the one behind reasoning shortcuts. The payoff is concrete: the shortcut-aware concept posterior that recent methods reach by ensembling or expressive density estimation is the only symmetry-invariant point of the confusion-set simplex, computable in closed form by averaging a single model over the symmetry group. On MNIST reasoning-shortcut benchmarks this single-model wrapper is better calibrated than a diversity-trained ensemble, while leaving label accuracy and identifiable concepts untouched. Code is freely available at https://github.com/bio-ontology-research-group/hott-nesy.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a homotopy-type-theoretic framework for neurosymbolic systems that preserves symmetry and proof-count information lost in standard set-based approaches, leading to a single-model solution for reasoning shortcuts that previously required ensembles.
AI.motivation: Standard neurosymbolic systems based on sets forget two critical pieces of information: (1) when two σ-structures are symmetric under the theory, and (2) how many distinct proofs witness a query. This loss leads to reasoning shortcuts.
AI.method: Replace underlying sets with types from homotopy type theory, reformulating the belief-weighted sum as a belief-weighted homotopy cardinality that counts objects inversely to their symmetries. A conservativity theorem recovers the classical functional when symmetries are trivial.
AI.result: The shortcut-aware concept posterior (previously requiring ensembling or expressive density estimation) becomes the unique symmetry-invariant point of the confusion-set simplex, computable in closed form by averaging a single model over the symmetry group.
AI.conclusion: On MNIST reasoning-shortcut benchmarks, this single-model wrapper achieves better calibration than diversity-trained ensembles while maintaining label accuracy and identifiable concepts. Code is publicly available.

title: SPARK: Low Latency Single-Camera 3D Pose Estimation for Autonomous Racing using Keypoints
summary: In autonomous racing, fast detection of other participants' movements is required to plan safe, collision-free trajectories with non-cooperative opponents. LiDAR detection is inherently slower and harder to deploy on edge devices than vision methods, causing delayed detections that limit object tracking performance during high-dynamic maneuvering. Utilizing monocular 3D detection enables an easy-to-deploy, low-latency detection of other participants on the racetrack. We present SPARK, a single-camera pose-estimation algorithm for autonomous racing using keypoint detection. It achieves long-range detection with high accuracy, exceeding the performance of state-of-the-art monocular camera detection algorithms while maintaining lower latency. By employing well-optimized YOLO models and leveraging the fixed geometry in the autonomous racing domain, the algorithm also exhibits low latency and resource usage. We evaluate the performance of our approach on real-world autonomous racing data and compare it to state-of-the-art LiDAR and camera detection algorithms. The source code is available at: https://github.com/TUMFTM/SPARK-camera-det

AI.tldr: SPARK is a monocular 3D pose-estimation algorithm for autonomous racing that uses keypoint detection with YOLO models, achieving high accuracy and low latency for long-range detection.
AI.motivation: LiDAR detection is slow and hard to deploy on edge devices; faster detection of opponents is needed for safe, collision-free planning in autonomous racing.
AI.method: Single-camera pose-estimation using keypoint detection with well-optimized YOLO models, leveraging fixed racing geometry for efficiency.
AI.result: Exceeds state-of-the-art monocular detection algorithms with higher accuracy and lower latency; performs well on real-world racing data.
AI.conclusion: Monocular 3D detection is a viable, low-latency alternative to LiDAR for autonomous racing; SPARK is open-source.

title: StereoFactory: A Unified Merging Framework for Robust Stereo Matching
summary: Stereo matching has advanced through foundation models trained on large-scale datasets, yet this paradigm suffers from a scalability bottleneck: incorporating new data requires costly joint retraining. Model merging offers a scalable post-hoc alternative by integrating knowledge from specialized models after source checkpoints are available. However, existing merging methods typically retain all available models or rely on greedy inclusion, which can preserve harmful task-vector interference. We propose StereoFactory, a coarse-to-fine evolutionary framework for adaptive model merging. Stage~1 employs a genetic algorithm to search the combinatorial space of model subsets, determining which models should participate. Stage~2 addresses module-level knowledge specialization (different functional modules exhibit distinct preferences for knowledge sources) through CMA-ES optimization of architecture-adaptive routing over the selected task vectors, with optional module-level scaling. Experiments across two architectures and four benchmarks demonstrate that StereoFactory consistently achieves the best four-benchmark average under the same checkpoint pool, reducing the average error from 3.80 to 3.30 on NMRF and from 2.88 to 2.19 on FoundationStereo relative to the strongest controlled baseline. The post-hoc search requires only 2.7--3.7\% of the corresponding joint-retraining wall-clock time. Analysis reveals that knowledge contributions are inherently module-specific, and selected subsets can transfer across architectures with minimal degradation. Code will be publicly released upon acceptance at: https://github.com/XiandaGuo/StereoFactory.

AI.tldr: StereoFactory is a coarse-to-fine evolutionary framework for adaptive model merging in stereo matching that searches optimal model subsets via genetic algorithms (Stage 1) and applies CMA-ES optimization for module-level routing (Stage 2), achieving superior benchmark performance with only 2.7-3.7% of joint-retraining time.
AI.motivation: Stereo matching foundation models suffer from a scalability bottleneck where incorporating new data requires costly joint retraining. Existing model merging methods either retain all models or use greedy inclusion, failing to avoid harmful task-vector interference.
AI.method: A two-stage evolutionary framework: Stage 1 uses a genetic algorithm to search the combinatorial space of model subsets; Stage 2 applies CMA-ES to optimize architecture-adaptive routing over selected task vectors with optional module-level scaling.
AI.result: StereoFactory achieves best four-benchmark averages across two architectures, reducing average error from 3.80 to 3.30 on NMRF and 2.88 to 2.19 on FoundationStereo, using only 2.7-3.7% of joint-retraining time.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge contributions are inherently module-specific, selected subsets can transfer across architectures with minimal degradation, and the evolutionary merging approach offers a scalable, efficient alternative to joint retraining.

title: CheckMIABench: Firm Foundations For Membership Inference Attacks on Language Models
summary: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) are a canonical way to assess a machine learning model's privacy properties. Although several attempts have been made to evaluate MIAs on language models, the extant literature has suffered numerous difficulties in constructing clean evaluations to test new techniques. In particular, subtle distribution shifts between member and non-member sets can undermine the statistical validity of MIAs; recent work has underscored this by showing that "blind" methods with no access to the underlying model can perform far better than published methods on the same benchmarks. This paper constructs a benchmark for principled evaluation of MIAs against LLMs, by leveraging the insight that training data before and after a fixed point during training are drawn from the same distribution. Therefore, all open-source models with intermediate checkpoints and public training data can be converted into MIA testbeds. We apply our framework to a half-dozen published attacks on the Pythia and OLMo family of models, from 70M to 7B parameters. To facilitate further privacy research, we open-source a modular library for designing and implementing attacks in this setting: https://github.com/safr-ai-lab/pandora_llm.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs a principled benchmark for evaluating membership inference attacks (MIAs) on LLMs by using intermediate checkpoints and public training data, overcoming distribution shift issues. It evaluates several attacks on Pythia and OLMo models (70M-7B) and releases an open-source library.
AI.motivation: Existing MIA evaluations on language models suffer from distribution shifts between member and non-member sets, undermining statistical validity. "Blind" methods without model access can outperform published techniques on the same benchmarks, highlighting the need for cleaner, more principled evaluation frameworks.
AI.method: The authors leverage the insight that training data before and after a fixed training checkpoint are drawn from the same distribution. Using open-source models with intermediate checkpoints and public training data, they convert these into MIA testbeds, applying the framework to published attacks on Pythia and OLMo models ranging from 70M to 7B parameters.
AI.result: The framework is applied to a half-dozen published attacks on the Pythia and OLMo family of models, from 70M to 7B parameters.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a benchmark for principled MIA evaluation against LLMs and releases an open-source modular library (Pandora LLM) for designing and implementing attacks, facilitating future privacy research.

title: FlowRAG: Synergizing Explicit Reasoning via Frequency-Aware Multi-Granularity Graph Flow
summary: Graph-based retrieval-augmented generation (GraphRAG) is effective for knowledge-intensive and multi-hop query tasks; however, many existing methods primarily seed entity-based graphs and rely on implicit semantic relevance propagation. This often (i) under-retrieves when user queries are abstract and semantically sparse at the entity level, and (ii) suffers from brittle multi-hop reasoning, where noisy activations can derail entity-to-entity transitions and corrupt the inferred relation chain, yielding unreliable conclusions. To this end, we propose \texttt{FlowRAG}, a semantic-aware retrieval framework that improves both semantic recall and explicit reasoning. Specifically, \texttt{FlowRAG} constructs a quad-level heterogeneous graph over passages, summaries, sentences, and entities, where summary nodes serve as a coarse semantic hub. At retrieval time, a dual-granularity activation module combines summary--query alignment with sentence-level matching to activate relevant entities under paraphrase and abstraction robustly. We then introduce a frequency-aware weighted flow module that routes relevance through entity--passage links weighted by within-passage term frequency, pruning noisy connections and extracting high-confidence reasoning paths as an explicit logic skeleton for generation. Extensive experiments show that \texttt{FlowRAG} obtains state-of-the-art performance on complex reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: FlowRAG improves GraphRAG by constructing a quad-level heterogeneous graph (passages, summaries, sentences, entities) with summary nodes as semantic hubs, using dual-granularity activation for robust query matching and frequency-aware weighted flow for explicit, noise-pruned reasoning paths, achieving SOTA on complex reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing GraphRAG methods rely on entity-based graphs with implicit semantic relevance propagation, leading to under-retrieval for abstract queries and brittle multi-hop reasoning due to noisy activations.
AI.method: (1) Quad-level heterogeneous graph over passages, summaries, sentences, and entities; (2) Dual-granularity activation combining summary-query alignment and sentence-level matching; (3) Frequency-aware weighted flow module routing relevance via term-frequency-weighted entity-passage links to prune noise and extract explicit reasoning paths.
AI.result: FlowRAG achieves state-of-the-art performance on complex reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed semantic-aware retrieval framework effectively addresses semantic recall and explicit reasoning limitations in GraphRAG, providing a robust solution for knowledge-intensive multi-hop queries.

title: ThinkingVLA: Interleaved Vision and Language Reasoning for Robotic Manipulation
summary: Most Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models map observations directly to actions without explicit reasoning, limiting their capacity for reasoning-intensive long-horizon tasks. To address this, existing approaches adopt Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to enable subgoal decomposition and spatial anticipation. However, those methods lack a unified architecture for effective cross-modal reasoning and fail to explicitly include inverse reasoning ability based on the target state. We argue that manipulation planning naturally decomposes into prediction, anticipating the next visual state, and inverse dynamics, inferring the actions to reach it. Bridging both requires a unified autoregressive architecture that interleaves textual and visual reasoning in a single generation process. We propose \textbf{ThinkingVLA}, a generative model that realizes this decomposition within a unified Mixture-of-Transformers architecture. ThinkingVLA consists of a forward CoT that identifies the immediate subgoal and guides the visual forecasting; the predicted image then serves as the target state, grounding an inverse CoT that reasons about spatial relationships and action intent based on the predicted image; and the final action is generated conditioned on this full reasoning context. Extensive experiments on simulation and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that ThinkingVLA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, with particularly large gains on long-horizon manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: ThinkingVLA is a unified Mixture-of-Transformers architecture that combines forward Chain-of-Thought (subgoal prediction and visual forecasting) with inverse Chain-of-Thought (spatial reasoning and action intent) to improve reasoning-intensive long-horizon manipulation tasks, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models lack explicit reasoning for long-horizon tasks, and current CoT approaches lack unified cross-modal reasoning and explicit inverse reasoning based on target states.
AI.method: ThinkingVLA uses a Mixture-of-Transformers architecture with (1) forward CoT for subgoal identification and visual forecasting, (2) inverse CoT for spatial reasoning and action intent grounded on predicted visual states, and (3) final action generation conditioned on the full reasoning context.
AI.result: ThinkingVLA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on simulation and real-world benchmarks, especially on long-horizon manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Unifying forward prediction and inverse dynamics reasoning within a single autoregressive generation process significantly improves VLA performance on complex, long-horizon tasks.

title: Theoretical Grounding of Out-Of-Distribution Detection With Reinforcement Learning Optimizer
summary: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection in dynamic open-world environments requires a model to continually adapt to evolving data distributions while generalizing to covariate-shifted inputs and rejecting semantic-shifted OOD examples. Most existing OOD detection methods optimize only the current-step objective and do not explicitly account for how post-deployment environment changes affect future OOD behavior. In this paper, we establish a theoretical grounding for dynamic OOD detection using a reinforcement learning (RL)-guided optimizer that explicitly favors updates that reduce the semantic OOD false positive rate over time. We develop a novel augmented optimizer that uses an RL-guided correction term on top of standard gradient descent (GD) and show its improvement over both future-domain generalization and semantic-OOD rejection. We analyze temporal error decomposition in terms of model-change and environment-change generalization errors and develop a new theoretical framework for comparing the generalization errors under both GD and RL-guided optimizers.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an RL-guided optimizer for dynamic OOD detection that reduces semantic OOD false positive rates over time, outperforming standard gradient descent.
AI.motivation: Existing OOD detection methods only optimize for the current step and fail to account for how post-deployment environment changes affect future OOD behavior in dynamic open-world settings.
AI.method: The authors develop an RL-guided correction term on top of standard gradient descent, creating an augmented optimizer that explicitly favors updates reducing semantic OOD false positive rates over time.
AI.result: The RL-guided optimizer shows improvement in both future-domain generalization and semantic-OOD rejection compared to standard gradient descent.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a theoretical framework for temporal error decomposition and generalization error comparison between GD and RL-guided optimizers, establishing theoretical grounding for dynamic OOD detection.

title: Perron--Frobenius Operator Matching for Generative Modeling
summary: We introduce Perron--Frobenius Operator Matching (PFOM), a generative framework that matches density evolution via the integral PF operator, subsuming flow, diffusion, and jump models. We prove that among Bregman divergences, only Kullback--Leibler divergence preserves equality between density-level and sample-conditioned objectives, yielding a practical loss equivalent to Koopman path matching. We further develop Nesterov-accelerated training and sampling that stabilize discretization and accelerate convergence. %On Gaussian mixtures and two-moons, PFOM achieves faster KL/$W_2$/MMD decrease and improved wall-clock efficiency with empirical validation. PFOM unifies operator-theoretic identification with modern generative modeling and opens paths to adaptive dictionaries and high-dimensional applications.

AI.tldr: PFOM is a generative framework unifying flow, diffusion, and jump models via Perron-Frobenius operator matching, with theoretical justification for KL divergence and Nesterov acceleration for training/sampling.
AI.motivation: To unify disparate generative modeling approaches (flow, diffusion, jump) under a single operator-theoretic framework and address training instability and convergence issues.
AI.method: Uses Perron-Frobenius operator to match density evolution; proves KL divergence is the only Bregman divergence preserving equality between density-level and sample-conditioned objectives; applies Nesterov acceleration to training and sampling.
AI.result: Faster KL/W2/MMD convergence and improved wall-clock efficiency on Gaussian mixtures and two-moons; unifies operator-theoretic identification with generative modeling.
AI.conclusion: PFOM provides a principled unified framework for generative models, with potential for adaptive dictionaries and high-dimensional applications.

title: StepGuard: Guarding Web Navigation via Single-Step Calibration
summary: Web navigation requires agents to follow natural language goals, interact with web pages, and produce accurate answers. While recent advances leverage vision-language models and reinforcement learning, existing methods still suffer from single-step fragility due to reward misalignment and error propagation. To tackle the reward entanglement, we design Dynamic Dual-Policy Optimization (DDPO), which dynamically switches between a navigation-first mode for exploration and an answer-first mode for question-answering to mitigate reward conflict. To calibrate the single-step error, we propose Confidence-Guided Adaptive Navigation Reflection (CANR), a mechanism that estimates per-step confidence, triggers reflection only when necessary, and uses contrastive rewards to encourage self-correction to calibrate the single-step inaccuracy. With the above as the main components, we finally develop our StepGuard, a new framework of Guarding Web Navigation via Single-Step Calibration. Experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly improves navigation and answer accuracy, setting new state-of-the-art performance on standard web navigation benchmarks.

AI.tldr: StepGuard introduces Dynamic Dual-Policy Optimization (DDPO) and Confidence-Guided Adaptive Navigation Reflection (CANR) to improve web navigation by tackling reward misalignment and single-step error propagation, achieving SOTA results on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing web navigation methods suffer from reward misalignment and error propagation due to single-step fragility, leading to poor navigation and answer accuracy.
AI.method: (1) DDPO: dynamically switches between navigation-first mode (exploration) and answer-first mode (QA) to mitigate reward conflict. (2) CANR: estimates per-step confidence, triggers reflection only when necessary, and uses contrastive rewards for self-correction. Together they form the StepGuard framework.
AI.result: StepGuard significantly improves navigation and answer accuracy, achieving new SOTA on standard web navigation benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Single-step calibration via dynamic policy optimization and adaptive confidence-guided reflection effectively addresses reward entanglement and error propagation in web navigation.

title: LAGO Policy: Latency-Aware Asynchronous Diffusion Policies with Goal-Directed Collision-Free Planning for Smooth Manipulation
summary: Diffusion-based visuomotor policies deployed with asynchronous inference often exhibit inter-chunk discontinuities and lack explicit mechanisms for obstacle-aware execution, leading to jerky motions and collisions that hinder reliable manipulation in real-world scenes. To address these issues, we propose LAGO Policy, a unified asynchronous action-generation framework that integrates trajectory optimization with diffusion policy for smooth and safe execution. LAGO Policy improves inter-chunk consistency via latency-aware classifier-free guidance conditioning on future actions. It further enables goal-directed collision-free trajectory planning by predicting a task-relevant interaction goal from demonstrations. Finally, spatial-temporal trajectory optimization refines the actions to be executed for low-jerk and feasible motion. Extensive real-world experiments demonstrate that LAGO Policy achieves smooth collision-free execution with high task success across challenging manipulation tasks. Project Website: https://lago-policy.github.io/

AI.tldr: LAGO Policy is a unified asynchronous action-generation framework that combines trajectory optimization with diffusion policy to achieve smooth, collision-free robot manipulation by improving inter-chunk consistency, predicting interaction goals, and refining trajectories for low-jerk motion.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based visuomotor policies suffer from inter-chunk discontinuities and lack obstacle-aware execution, causing jerky motions and collisions that hinder reliable real-world manipulation.
AI.method: (1) Latency-aware classifier-free guidance conditioning on future actions for inter-chunk consistency; (2) Goal prediction from demonstrations for collision-free trajectory planning; (3) Spatial-temporal trajectory optimization for low-jerk feasible motion.
AI.result: Achieves smooth collision-free execution with high task success across challenging real-world manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: LAGO Policy effectively addresses discontinuities and obstacle avoidance in diffusion-based policies, enabling reliable and safe asynchronous robot manipulation.

title: GeneralVLA-2: Geometry-Aware Reconstruction and Governed Memory for Robot Planning
summary: Generalist vision-language-action systems need object-centric 3D evidence and reusable manipulation experience to plan reliable robot trajectories. GeneralVLA provides a hierarchical interface for converting language and RGB-D observations into 3D end-effector paths, but two bottlenecks remain. First, monocular SAM3D-style object reconstruction can hallucinate pose and unseen geometry, while manipulation benefits from stable object shape when calibrated multi-view observations are available. Second, the original KnowledgeBank mainly retrieves semantically similar snippets and appends new knowledge, which makes it difficult to control memory quality, conflicts, confidence, and geometric relevance. To address the first challenge, we introduce GeoFuse-MV3D, a geometry-prior-guided MV-SAM3D reconstruction branch that verifies external geometry cues with input-view masks, applies soft visual-hull support, performs axis-wise refinement, and fuses only geometry while preserving appearance. To address the second challenge, we upgrade KnowledgeBank into a governed long-term memory system with explicit quality, confidence, lifecycle, verifier, and conflict metadata, together with precision-oriented retrieval. Finally, we evaluate the reconstruction branch on GSO-30 and the memory module on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and SWE-Bench Verified; GeoFuse-MV3D improves over the MV-SAM3D baseline by reducing CD and LPIPS by 2.20% and 2.02% while increasing PSNR and SSIM by 2.36% and 1.03%, and KnowledgeBank improves over ReasoningBank by 4.53% on Terminal-Bench SR and 3.73% on SWE-Bench resolve rate, while reducing AS by 4.95% and 5.65%, respectively. Code: https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/GeneralVLA-2. Website: https://aigeeksgroup.github.io/GeneralVLA-2.

AI.tldr: GeneralVLA-2 improves a vision-language-action system with a geometry-prior-guided multi-view 3D reconstruction (GeoFuse-MV3D) that reduces hallucination in object shape, and upgrades KnowledgeBank with governed long-term memory metadata and precision-oriented retrieval, achieving consistent improvements across reconstruction and task-planning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Two bottlenecks in the original GeneralVLA: (1) monocular SAM3D-based object reconstruction hallucinates pose and unseen geometry, whereas manipulation needs stable object shape from calibrated multi-view inputs; (2) the original KnowledgeBank lacks memory quality control, conflict resolution, confidence scoring, and geometric relevance, making it hard to manage long-term experience.
AI.method: Proposes GeoFuse-MV3D: a geometry-prior-guided MV-SAM3D reconstruction branch that verifies external geometry cues with input-view masks, applies soft visual-hull support, performs axis-wise refinement, and fuses only geometry while preserving appearance. Upgrades KnowledgeBank into a governed long-term memory system with explicit quality, confidence, lifecycle, verifier, and conflict metadata, plus precision-oriented retrieval.
AI.result: GeoFuse-MV3D reduces CD by 2.20% and LPIPS by 2.02%, increases PSNR by 2.36% and SSIM by 1.03% over MV-SAM3D on GSO-30. KnowledgeBank improves Terminal-Bench SR by 4.53% and SWE-Bench resolve rate by 3.73%, while reducing AS by 4.95% and 5.65%, respectively, over ReasoningBank.
AI.conclusion: Both proposed components—GeoFuse-MV3D for geometry-aware 3D reconstruction and governed KnowledgeBank for reliable long-term memory—effectively address the original system's bottlenecks, yielding consistent gains in reconstruction quality and task-planning performance.

title: ReRAM-aware Model Finetuning addressing I-V Non-linearity and Retention Errors
comment: 11 pages, 12 figures, 2 tables, with appendix (5 pages, 9 figures)
summary: Traditional CPU, GPU, and NPU architectures are increasingly limited by the von Neumann bottleneck. While In-Memory Computing (IMC) using ReRAM crossbar arrays offers a high-density, energy-efficient alternative, its practical deployment is constrained through their non-idealities. Existing hardware-aware training frameworks often require training from scratch, which is computationally prohibitive for modern large-scale models. In this work, we propose a finetuning-based hardware-aware training algorithm that enables robust DNN deployment on ReRAM with minimal training overhead. Our approach mitigates I-V non-linearity by applying a range-shrunk sinh transformation and incorporates retention errors directly into a regularization loss during the finetuning process. We evaluate our framework across models and tasks such as image classification and question-answering (QA). Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves similar accuracy on large-scale models like ResNet18 and DeiT-Tiny as the base model. In-case of ImageNet for MobileNetV3 families the technique has only less than 2% accuracy degradation. Further, applying the technique on the SQuAD v2 dataset results in only 1 point degradation of F-1 score.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a finetuning-based hardware-aware training method for deploying DNNs on ReRAM-based In-Memory Computing systems, addressing non-idealities like I-V non-linearity and retention errors with minimal computational overhead, achieving near-baseline accuracy on image classification (ResNet18, DeiT-Tiny, MobileNetV3) and QA (SQuAD v2) tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional CPU/GPU/NPU architectures suffer from the von Neumann bottleneck. ReRAM-based IMC offers a promising alternative but suffers from non-idealities that hinder practical deployment. Existing hardware-aware training requires training from scratch, which is computationally prohibitive for large-scale models.
AI.method: A finetuning-based hardware-aware training algorithm that: (1) mitigates I-V non-linearity via a range-shrunk sinh transformation, and (2) incorporates retention errors into a regularization loss during finetuning, enabling robust deployment with minimal training overhead.
AI.result: Achieves similar accuracy to base models on ResNet18 and DeiT-Tiny; less than 2% accuracy degradation on MobileNetV3 families (ImageNet); only 1 point F-1 score degradation on SQuAD v2 dataset.
AI.conclusion: The proposed finetuning-based approach effectively enables robust DNN deployment on ReRAM with minimal overhead, making hardware-aware training practical for large-scale models.

title: Structural Preservation and the Logical Expressiveness of Graph Neural Networks
summary:
Bridges between graph neural networks (GNNs) and logical formalisms have been established by fixing architectural choices, such as the types of aggregation, combination, and activation functions. These choices define restricted classes of GNNs for which tight correspondences with logical formalisms can be obtained, by showing that logical formulae can be translated into equivalent GNNs and, conversely, that GNNs can be translated into equivalent formulae.
  In this paper we take a semantic perspective by establishing the logical expressiveness of classes of GNN classifiers that are preserved under structural properties: embeddings (extensions), injective homomorphisms, and homomorphisms. We show that, for each such property, there exists a fragment of graded modal logic characterising the class of GNNs. In particular, preservation under embeddings, injective homomorphisms, and homomorphisms corresponds to existential graded modal logic, its existential-positive fragment, and existential-positive modal logic, respectively. These results characterise the expressiveness of broad classes of GNNs independently of specific architectural choices, but we also show that each of these classes admits a GNN architecture of the same expressiveness.
  Technically, our approach uses a new well-quasi-order result for trees of bounded height, yielding finite representations of unravelling-invariant classes.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes logical correspondences for broad classes of GNNs based on semantic preservation properties (embeddings, injective homomorphisms, homomorphisms), showing they correspond to fragments of graded modal logic, with each class admitting a GNN architecture of equivalent expressiveness, using a new well-quasi-order result for bounded-height trees.
AI.motivation: Existing work on GNN-logic correspondences relies on specific architectural choices (aggregation, combination, activation functions), which define restricted GNN classes. This paper aims to characterize the logical expressiveness of broader GNN classes independent of such architectural details, using semantic preservation properties instead.
AI.method: The authors take a semantic approach by considering classes of GNN classifiers preserved under three structural properties: embeddings, injective homomorphisms, and homomorphisms. They show each property corresponds to a specific fragment of graded modal logic. A new well-quasi-order result for bounded-height trees is employed to obtain finite representations of unravelling-invariant classes.
AI.result: Preservation under embeddings corresponds to existential graded modal logic; preservation under injective homomorphisms corresponds to its existential-positive fragment; preservation under homomorphisms corresponds to existential-positive modal logic. Each class also admits a GNN architecture of equivalent expressiveness.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a semantic characterization of GNN expressiveness that is independent of specific architectural choices, bridging GNNs and logical formalisms at a more fundamental level.

title: Uncertainty Quantification for Flow-Based Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-language-action models (VLAs) combine vision-language backbones with expressive generative action heads trained via flow matching on large-scale robotic datasets. Despite their strong empirical performance in robotic manipulation, VLAs lack mechanisms to quantify confidence in their predictions and to detect when their actions may be unreliable. This presents a critical limitation for real-world deployment in non-stationary environments, where models inevitably encounter scenarios outside their pretraining distribution and may fail without warning. To address this, we derive an efficient method for quantifying epistemic uncertainty in flow-matching models by leveraging velocity-field disagreement (VFD) across a small ensemble. We successfully use this uncertainty estimate for failure detection during deployment and active fine-tuning of flow-based VLAs. To this end, we propose SAVE, a framework for uncertainty-guided active multitask fine-tuning that reduces the number of costly expert demonstrations required to adapt VLAs to new tasks. Through extensive experiments on the LIBERO benchmark, we demonstrate that VFD yields better-calibrated uncertainty estimates predictive of downstream performance, that VFD achieves strong performance in detecting failures, and that uncertainty-guided data acquisition with SAVE requires at least 22% fewer samples than baselines. In summary, our work shows that quantifying epistemic uncertainty in flow-based VLAs improves both failure awareness and adaptation. Project website: tum-lsy.github.io/uq_vla/.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces VFD (velocity-field disagreement) to quantify epistemic uncertainty in flow-matching vision-language-action models, and proposes SAVE, an uncertainty-guided active fine-tuning framework that reduces expert demonstrations needed for adapting VLAs to new tasks by at least 22%.
AI.motivation: Current VLAs lack mechanisms to quantify prediction confidence and detect unreliable actions, which is critical for real-world deployment in non-stationary environments where distribution shift occurs.
AI.method: Derives VFD (velocity-field disagreement) across a small ensemble to quantify epistemic uncertainty in flow-matching models, and proposes SAVE framework for uncertainty-guided active multitask fine-tuning to reduce required expert demonstrations.
AI.result: VFD yields better-calibrated uncertainty estimates predictive of downstream performance, achieves strong failure detection, and SAVE requires at least 22% fewer samples than baselines on LIBERO benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Quantifying epistemic uncertainty in flow-based VLAs improves both failure awareness and adaptation efficiency.

title: SPHINX: First Explain, Then Explore
summary: Generating adversarial driving scenarios is critical for evaluating and improving autonomous vehicle decision-making systems in simulation. Recent approaches, such as ChatScene and LLM-Attacker, rely primarily on the prior knowledge of Large Language Models and Vision-Language Models to generate driving scenarios procedurally. We argue that adversarial scenes should be generated based on the failure diagnosis (e.g., indecisiveness, multi-frame inconsistency) of the driving policy to specifically address the policy's weaknesses instead of relying on prior assumptions. In this paper, we propose SPHINX, a closed-loop framework for adversarial scenario synthesis guided by a simple principle: first explain, then explore. Beyond blindly exploring the scenario space, SPHINX leverages explainable artificial intelligence methods to analyze the policy, identifying key visual concepts and their influence on policy outputs, and the uncertainty of the decisions. Given the interpretable evidence extracted from the policy's own decision process, we use a vision language model to rationalize and criticize failure modes of the current policy. These critics are then used to generate targeted adversarial scenarios for policy retraining and improvement. We demonstrate that SPHINX can highlight an interpretable account of policy failures while other adversarial scene generation cannot. Across the evaluated benchmarks and test suites, SPHINX can be applied to diverse state-of-the-art autonomous vehicle architectures and yields consistent robustness improvements over existing scenario-generation methods.

AI.tldr: SPHINX is a closed-loop framework for generating adversarial driving scenarios by first explaining policy failures through XAI methods, then exploring targeted scenarios to improve autonomous vehicle decision-making.
AI.motivation: Existing adversarial scenario generation methods (e.g., ChatScene, LLM-Attacker) rely on prior knowledge from LLMs/VLMs rather than diagnosing actual failure modes of the driving policy (e.g., indecisiveness, multi-frame inconsistency). The authors argue scenarios should stem from the policy's specific weaknesses.
AI.method: SPHINX follows a "first explain, then explore" paradigm: (1) uses explainable AI to identify key visual concepts and their influence on policy outputs, along with decision uncertainty; (2) leverages a VLM to rationalize and criticize failure modes based on this interpretable evidence; (3) generates targeted adversarial scenarios for policy retraining.
AI.result: SPHINX provides interpretable accounts of policy failures (unlike other methods), applies to diverse state-of-the-art autonomous vehicle architectures, and yields consistent robustness improvements over existing scenario-generation methods across benchmarks and test suites.
AI.conclusion: Generating adversarial scenarios guided by policy failure diagnosis (explain-then-explore) is more effective than relying on prior assumptions, leading to better interpretability and robustness improvements for autonomous driving systems.

title: Multi-Adapter PPO: A Cross-Attention Enhanced Wavelength Selection Framework for LIBS Quantitative Analysis
summary: Laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy (LIBS) quantitative analysis faces critical challenges in wavelength selection due to high-dimensional spectral data and the fundamental trade-off between prediction accuracy and feature efficiency. This paper presents a novel Multi-Adapter PPO framework that transforms wavelength selection into a reinforcement learning problem, leveraging cross-attention mechanisms and multiple specialized adapters to capture complex spectral relationships. Our approach outperforms traditional Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) by an average of 28.4\% in comprehensive score and 45.2\% in prediction accuracy across steel and coal datasets. The proposed method demonstrates superior performance in balancing prediction accuracy with feature efficiency, achieving state-of-the-art results in LIBS quantitative analysis while maintaining interpretability and computational efficiency. We released our code and dataset here: https://github.com/Hflying/MAPPO

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Multi-Adapter PPO framework for wavelength selection in LIBS quantitative analysis, transforming the problem into a reinforcement learning task. It outperforms traditional PSO by 28.4% in comprehensive score and 45.2% in prediction accuracy across steel and coal datasets.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of high-dimensional spectral data and the trade-off between prediction accuracy and feature efficiency in LIBS quantitative analysis wavelength selection.
AI.method: A Multi-Adapter PPO framework that uses reinforcement learning with cross-attention mechanisms and multiple specialized adapters to capture complex spectral relationships for wavelength selection.
AI.result: Outperforms traditional PSO by 28.4% in comprehensive score and 45.2% in prediction accuracy on steel and coal datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art results in LIBS quantitative analysis by balancing prediction accuracy with feature efficiency while maintaining interpretability and computational efficiency.

title: MathVis-Fine: Aligning Visual Supervision with Necessity via Progressive Dependency-Guided Training for Multimodal Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning has extended from purely linguistic domains to multimodal scenarios; however, existing approaches often treat visual inputs as homogeneous or auxiliary signals, failing to capture the intricate and sample-specific dependencies between text and images in mathematical problem-solving. This gives rise to two core issues: first, the supervisory signals for visual content are generalized and coarse-grained, lacking adaptation to the actual necessity of visual information in each sample; second, training feedback becomes inaccurate when visual rewards are uniformly applied without distinguishing the complementary relationships among inputs. These limitations hinder models from achieving precise multimodal reasoning. In this work, we propose a framework for modeling fine-grained visual dependencies in mathematical reasoning. We first construct the MathVis-Fine dataset, augmenting fine-grained visual annotations with visual dependency ratings. Building upon this dataset, we introduce a two-stage progressive visual enhancement training paradigm that balances answer correctness rewards and visual grounding rewards according to the intrinsic visual dependency level of each sample, thereby mitigating reward bias and improving supervision accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the MathVis-Fine framework effectively enhances visual perception progressively based on visual dependency, offering a more precise training framework for multimodal mathematical reasoning. We will release the dataset upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes MathVis-Fine, a framework that models fine-grained visual dependencies in multimodal mathematical reasoning, featuring a dataset with visual dependency ratings and a two-stage progressive training paradigm balancing answer correctness and visual grounding rewards.
AI.motivation: Existing CoT reasoning approaches treat visual inputs homogeneously or as auxiliary signals, failing to capture sample-specific text-image dependencies. This leads to coarse-grained visual supervision and inaccurate training feedback when rewards are uniformly applied without distinguishing complementary relationships.
AI.method: (1) Constructs the MathVis-Fine dataset with fine-grained visual annotations and dependency ratings. (2) Introduces a two-stage progressive visual enhancement training paradigm that balances answer correctness rewards and visual grounding rewards based on each sample's intrinsic visual dependency level, mitigating reward bias.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the framework effectively enhances visual perception progressively based on visual dependency, offering a more precise training framework for multimodal mathematical reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The MathVis-Fine framework provides improved supervision accuracy by adapting rewards to visual dependency levels, addressing limitations of homogeneous visual treatment in multimodal mathematical reasoning.

title: A Hybrid Optimization Framework for Grasp Synthesis under Partial Observations
summary: We propose a hybrid grasp synthesis framework that combines a learning-based Energy-Based Model (EBM) with an analytical Iterative Closest Point (ICP) method to generate robust grasps from partially observed point clouds. The learned energy function acts as a prior within a Stein Variational Gradient Descent (SVGD) framework, guiding iterative refinement of grasp configurations. Evaluated on 67 objects with 5,360 grasp attempts, our method achieves an average success rate of 60.9\%, outperforming AnyGrasp (31.1\%) and Grasp Pose Detection (48.4\%) and AS-ICP (56.6\%). These results highlight the strong generalization ability of our approach and demonstrate how combining data-driven learning with geometric optimization addresses the limitations of either strategy in isolation.

AI.tldr: A hybrid grasp synthesis framework combining learned EBM with analytical ICP, achieving 60.9% success rate on 5,360 grasp attempts across 67 objects, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of purely data-driven or purely geometric approaches in robotic grasp synthesis by combining both strategies for robust performance.
AI.method: Combines a learned Energy-Based Model (EBM) as a prior with analytical Iterative Closest Point (ICP) within a Stein Variational Gradient Descent (SVGD) framework for iterative grasp refinement from partial point clouds.
AI.result: 60.9% average success rate on 67 objects with 5,360 grasp attempts, outperforming AnyGrasp (31.1%), Grasp Pose Detection (48.4%), and AS-ICP (56.6%).
AI.conclusion: Combining data-driven learning with geometric optimization yields strong generalization and addresses the limitations of either strategy alone.

title: OmniDrive: An LLM-Choreographed Multi-Agent World Model with Unified Latent Co-Compression for Multi-View Driving Video Generation
summary: Generative world models for autonomous driving face two unresolved tensions: heterogeneous control injection, where free-form language, HD-maps, trajectories, and camera poses reside in incompatible representational spaces, and post-hoc cross-view fusion, where per-camera latents fail to encode global 3-D geometry. We trace both to a single root cause: the absence of a shared symbolic interlingua aligning language, geometry, and pixels at the latent-token level. We present DRIVE-CHOREO, an LLM-choreographed multi-agent world model that recasts controllable multi-view video generation as latent choreography. Three Qwen2.5-VL agents - a Director parsing user intent into a structured WorldScript, a Cartographer grounding it into spatially-anchored layout tokens, and an Auditor feeding cross-view critiques back as auxiliary supervision - jointly author a single position-aware token sequence. This sequence is co-compressed with the multi-view video via a view-time permutation that enforces inter-camera geometry within the convolutional receptive field of a 3-D VAE. On nuScenes, DRIVE-CHOREO sets new state-of-the-art multi-view consistency and BEV mAP (21.6) with competitive FVD (45.7); a detector trained purely on our synthetic data gains +2.4 NDS on the real validation split, validating downstream utility.

AI.tldr: DRIVE-CHOREO is an LLM-choreographed multi-agent world model for autonomous driving that uses three Qwen2.5-VL agents to parse language into structured tokens, ground them spatially, and provide cross-view critiques, achieving state-of-the-art multi-view consistency and downstream driving task performance.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses two key tensions in generative world models for autonomous driving: (1) heterogeneous control injection, where different input modalities (language, HD-maps, trajectories, camera poses) reside in incompatible representational spaces, and (2) post-hoc cross-view fusion, where per-camera latents fail to encode global 3D geometry.
AI.method: DRIVE-CHOREO uses three Qwen2.5-VL agents: a Director (parses user intent into structured WorldScript), a Cartographer (grounds WorldScript into spatially-anchored layout tokens), and an Auditor (feeds cross-view critiques as auxiliary supervision). These jointly produce a position-aware token sequence, co-compressed with multi-view video via a view-time permutation that enforces inter-camera geometry within the 3D VAE's convolutional receptive field.
AI.result: On nuScenes, DRIVE-CHOREO achieves multi-view consistency SOTA, BEV mAP 21.6, and competitive FVD 45.7. A detector trained purely on synthetic data gains +2.4 NDS on the real validation split.
AI.conclusion: The shared symbolic interlingua (WorldScript) at the latent-token level resolves both heterogeneous control and cross-view fusion issues, demonstrating strong downstream utility for autonomous driving perception tasks.

title: Online LLM Selection via Constrained Bandits with Time-Varying Demand
comment: 11 pages, 3 figures with multiple subfigures, 1 table, submitted for possible journal publication
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in edge-cloud inference systems to handle diverse user tasks with heterogeneous accuracy, latency, and cost profiles. Selecting the appropriate LLM for each incoming task is critical for ensuring service quality and efficient resource utilization. However, model heterogeneity, stochastic and unknown performance characteristics, and time-varying task demands make static selection strategies inadequate. Real-world deployments often impose hard resource budgets such as monetary expenditure limits, along with soft service-level requirements such as latency guarantees. These constraints introduce additional challenges for online decision-making. We formulate this problem as a constrained stochastic bandit learning task, where the learner sequentially selects models under both packing-type (hard) and covering-type (soft) constraints, while adapting to time-varying task demand. The learner operates without access to the underlying reward, cost, or latency distributions and must rely on partial feedback. We develop a novel online learning algorithm that leverages confidence-bound estimates and demand predictions to balance reward maximization with long-term constraint satisfaction. We provide theoretical guarantees showing sublinear regret and sublinear covering constraint violations compared to an offline benchmark with full information. Experimental results on synthetic workloads demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of our approach in dynamic, resource-constrained environments.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an online learning algorithm using constrained stochastic bandit formulation to select LLMs under hard (budget) and soft (latency) constraints in edge-cloud inference systems, achieving sublinear regret and constraint violations.
AI.motivation: LLM deployment in edge-cloud systems faces challenges from model heterogeneity, unknown performance characteristics, time-varying task demands, and hard/soft resource constraints, making static selection strategies inadequate.
AI.method: Formulates LLM selection as a constrained stochastic bandit learning problem under packing-type (hard) and covering-type (soft) constraints, using confidence-bound estimates and demand predictions for online decision-making.
AI.result: Provides theoretical guarantees of sublinear regret and sublinear covering constraint violations compared to an offline full-information benchmark, with experimental validation on synthetic workloads.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithm is effective and robust for dynamic, resource-constrained LLM selection in edge-cloud environments.

title: Learn to Quantify Social Interaction with Constraints for Pedestrian Walking
summary: Long-term human path forecasting in crowds is critical for autonomous moving platforms (like autonomous driving cars and social robots) to avoid collision and make high-quality planning. Although the current research take into account social interactions for prediction, they don't reveal the exact kinds of social interactions happened among people and how the social interactions affect the decision-making process of pedestrians, which further limits its robustness. Social interactions in pedestrian walking are intuitively massive and hard to label and quantify. In this paper, we explore creatively to quantify and interpret how pedestrians interact with others by proposing Learn to Cluster. Our clustering social interactions is probabilistic latent variable generative, learning directly from sequential trajectory observations, scalable to arbitrary number of pedestrians. Learn to cluster is label-free and can be naturally integrated into the training process of the prediction model. The latent variables will then serve as 'labels' to categorize social interactions. Extensive experiments over several trajectory prediction benchmarks demonstrate that our method is able to learn the patterns of social interactions and effectively integrate the patterns to pedestrian trajectory prediction.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes "Learn to Cluster," a label-free probabilistic generative model that learns to quantify and categorize social interaction patterns from pedestrian trajectories, integrating these patterns to improve long-term path forecasting in crowds.
AI.motivation: Current pedestrian trajectory prediction methods consider social interactions but fail to reveal the exact kinds of social interactions or how they affect pedestrian decision-making, limiting robustness.
AI.method: A probabilistic latent variable generative model called "Learn to Cluster" that learns directly from sequential trajectory observations, is scalable to any number of pedestrians, requires no labels, and integrates naturally into prediction model training via latent variables that categorize social interactions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on trajectory prediction benchmarks show the method successfully learns social interaction patterns and effectively integrates them into pedestrian trajectory prediction.
AI.conclusion: The proposed label-free clustering approach can quantify and interpret social interactions among pedestrians, improving the robustness and quality of long-term path forecasting for autonomous platforms.

title: EAGG: Embodiment-Aligned Grasp Generation via Geometry-Aware Graph Conditioning
comment: 16 pages, 8 figures. Code is available at https://github.com/wanhaoniu/EAGG
summary: Cross-end-effector grasp generation seeks a unified model that generalizes across objects and across embodiments ranging from parallel grippers to dexterous end effectors. Existing grasp generators are typically designed for a fixed embodiment or encode embodiment identity with a static descriptor, which weakens transfer when topology, actuation coupling, and contact geometry differ substantially. We present EAGG, an embodiment-aligned grasp generator that represents each embodiment with a topology-aware end-effector graph and an embodiment-specific low-dimensional end-effector control space. A frozen end-effector-cognition backbone converts the current articulated state into geometry-aware tokens that act as a reusable morphology prior, and iterative geometry injection refreshes these tokens throughout sampling so that conditioning remains synchronized with the evolving end-effector geometry. On the MultiGripperGrasp benchmark, EAGG reaches 56.17% average success across six training end effectors, remaining within 1.10 percentage points of specialized training while preserving transfer to finetuning and zero-shot end effectors. Iterative geometry injection further reduces the pooled median contact distance from 0.239 cm to 0.189 cm. These results show that cross-end-effector grasp generation is strengthened by aligning embodiment structure inside a shared generator rather than suppressing embodiment differences. Code is available at https://github.com/wanhaoniu/EAGG.

AI.tldr: EAGG is a unified grasp generator that uses topology-aware end-effector graphs and iterative geometry injection to generalize across different robotic end effectors (from parallel grippers to dexterous hands), achieving 56.17% success rate on MultiGripperGrasp benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing grasp generators are designed for fixed embodiments or use static descriptors that fail to transfer when topology, actuation coupling, and contact geometry differ substantially across end effectors.
AI.method: EAGG represents each embodiment with a topology-aware end-effector graph and a low-dimensional control space, uses a frozen end-effector-cognition backbone to convert articulated state into geometry-aware tokens as reusable morphology prior, and employs iterative geometry injection to keep conditioning synchronized with evolving end-effector geometry during sampling.
AI.result: Achieves 56.17% average success across six training end effectors (within 1.10% of specialized training), reduces pooled median contact distance from 0.239 cm to 0.189 cm, and transfers to finetuning/zero-shot end effectors.
AI.conclusion: Cross-end-effector grasp generation is strengthened by aligning embodiment structure inside a shared generator rather than suppressing embodiment differences.

title: Reinforcing Dual-Path Reasoning in Spatial Vision Language Models
summary: Spatial VLMs have made substantial progress in geometric perception, yet complex spatial reasoning requiring multi-step inference over depth, distance, and scene relations remains challenging. Moreover, different spatial queries call for fundamentally different strategies: some are best addressed through purely linguistic, step-by-step deduction, while others require explicit 3D grounding before quantitative inference. We present Dual-Path Spatial Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning for Spatial VLMs (SR-REAL), a unified framework that equips a spatial VLM with two complementary reasoning paths: Language-Only Reasoning (LOR), which performs step-by-step linguistic deduction, and Detect-Then-Reason (DTR), which detects 3D geometric cues (e.g., centers or bounding boxes) via region tokens before explicit geometric inference. SR-REAL begins with a cold-start supervised fine-tuning stage that constructs LOR and DTR chain-of-thought supervision and exposes a region-to-3D interface, followed by RL that optimizes the policy model with accuracy and format rewards; for DTR, a discrete center-based detection reward further refines geometric alignment.
