AI.tldr: SEGTME-UNI2 is a unified framework integrating H&E-stained histology image analysis via UNI2-UPERHOVER (a dual-head segmentation model with pseudo-label curriculum learning), a structured TME feature extraction pipeline, and a fine-tuned BioNeMo GPT model for generating clinical narratives. The framework and associated datasets are publicly released.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of integrated, scalable solutions that simultaneously perform cell segmentation, feature extraction, and interpretable clinical reporting from routine H&E-stained histology for tumour microenvironment characterisation, especially given the scarcity of pixel-level annotations at scale.
AI.method: 1) UNI2-UPERHOVER: dual-head segmentation model combining UNI2-H foundation model (ViT-Giant) with two UperNet decoders (semantic + gradient regression). 2) Three-stage progressive pseudo-label curriculum (PanNuke → entropy-filtered TCGA-UT → full multi-scale TCGA-UT) without weight transfer. 3) Structured TME feature extraction (20+ metrics). 4) BioNeMo GPT fine-tuned to generate clinical narratives from JSON-encoded features.
AI.result: Preliminary validation on held-out PanNuke and TCGA-UT partitions demonstrates framework feasibility and internal consistency. Public release of pseudo-labelled TCGA-UT dataset and UNI2-UPERHOVER checkpoint.
AI.conclusion: SEGTME-UNI2 provides a scalable, interpretable solution for TME profiling from routine histology, with public resources to support large-scale spatial biology research.

title: Physics-Constrained Neural Networks for Improved Short-Term Weather Forecasting: A Case Study over the South Pacific
comment: Presented at ICLR 2026 Workshop AI and PDE
summary: This study introduces enhancements to physics-constrained neural networks (PCNNs) that improve the accuracy and stability of hybrid short-term weather forecasting models. Building on the WeatherGFT architecture, three innovations are proposed. First, an upgraded numerical solver, combining a fifth-order weighted essentially non-oscillatory scheme (WENO-5), a beta-plane approximation, and subgrid-scale viscosity, permits a fourfold increase in the integration time step to 1200 s while reducing the daily mean squared error by up to 26%. Second, a unified autoregressive hybrid block replaces the original chain of 24 specialised modules, eliminating overfitting to specific lead times. Third, the physical core is integrated with two state-of-the-art neural backbones, resulting in PI-PredFormer and PI-IAM4VP. Evaluation on the WeatherBench South Pacific subset from 2000 to 2004 shows that these hybrids reduce root mean squared error at 1-12 h lead times by 8-22% compared to purely neural counterparts, while better preserving physical consistency. These results demonstrate that incremental refinement of hybrid components offers a practical route toward more accurate and efficient short-range weather forecasting.

AI.tldr: This paper enhances physics-constrained neural networks (PCNNs) for hybrid short-term weather forecasting by introducing three innovations: (1) an upgraded numerical solver enabling 4x larger time steps (1200s) and up to 26% lower daily MSE, (2) a unified autoregressive hybrid block replacing 24 specialized modules, and (3) integration with two neural backbones (PI-PredFormer & PI-IAM4VP). Evaluated on WeatherBench data, these hybrids reduce RMSE by 8-22% versus purely neural models while maintaining physical consistency.
AI.motivation: To improve the accuracy, stability, and efficiency of hybrid physics-constrained neural network models for short-term weather forecasting by addressing limitations in existing architectures like WeatherGFT.
AI.method: Three innovations: (1) Upgraded numerical solver with WENO-5 scheme, beta-plane approximation, and subgrid-scale viscosity; (2) Unified autoregressive hybrid block replacing 24 specialized modules; (3) Integration with PI-PredFormer and PI-IAM4VP neural backbones.
AI.result: Fourfold increase in integration time step (to 1200s), up to 26% reduction in daily MSE, and 8-22% RMSE reduction at 1-12h lead times compared to purely neural models on WeatherBench South Pacific subset (2000-2004).
AI.conclusion: Incremental refinement of hybrid components provides a practical pathway toward more accurate and efficient short-range weather forecasting.

title: Your AI Travel Agent Would Book You a Bullfight: An Agentic Benchmark for Implicit Animal Welfare in Frontier AI Models
summary: AI agents are moving from advisors to actors, booking travel, planning menus, and running procurement on behalf of users. Existing benchmarks for AI and animal welfare evaluate model text responses to question-answer prompts, leaving open whether the welfare reasoning surfaced in those responses transfers to agentic deployment where the model must take actions with tools. We introduce TAC (Travel Agent Compassion), the first agentic benchmark measuring whether AI agents avoid options involving animal exploitation when acting on behalf of users. TAC presents an AI agent with twelve hand-authored travel booking scenarios across six categories of animal exploitation, augmented to forty-eight samples to control for price, rating, and position confounds. We evaluate seven frontier models from four labs. Every model scores below the chance level of sixty-four percent, with the best performer (Claude Opus 4.7) at fifty-three percent. A single welfare-aware sentence in the system prompt yields gains of forty-seven to sixty-three percentage points in Claude and GPT-5.5, twenty-six points in GPT-5.2, and under twelve points in DeepSeek and Gemini. An auxiliary Inspect Scout audit of 288 base-condition transcripts from the top two performers, using Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite as judge, flags zero transcripts for evaluation awareness, suggesting the below-chance rates do not stem from the models recognising the evaluation. We discuss implications for category-level variation across cultural domains, the limits of text-response welfare benchmarks, and the EU General-Purpose AI Code of Practice systemic risk framework.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces TAC, the first agentic benchmark testing whether AI agents avoid animal exploitation when taking actions (not just answering questions). All seven frontier models scored below chance (64%), with best at 53%. Adding a welfare-aware prompt sentence significantly improved performance for some models but not others.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks evaluate AI welfare reasoning only through question-answer text responses, but AI agents are increasingly acting autonomously (booking travel, planning menus, procurement). It's unclear if welfare reasoning transfers from text responses to tool-based agentic deployment.
AI.method: TAC benchmark: 12 hand-authored travel booking scenarios across 6 categories of animal exploitation, augmented to 48 samples controlling for price/rating/position confounds. Evaluated 7 frontier models from 4 labs. Also conducted an Inspect Scout audit with Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite as judge to check for evaluation awareness.
AI.result: All models scored below chance (64%); best was Claude Opus 4.7 at 53%. Welfare-aware system prompt improved Claude and GPT-5.5 by 47-63 percentage points, GPT-5.2 by 26 points, DeepSeek and Gemini by under 12 points. Audit found zero transcripts flagged for evaluation awareness.
AI.conclusion: Current AI agents fail to avoid animal exploitation in agentic settings despite potential text-response competence. Welfare reasoning doesn't automatically transfer to tool use. Results vary across cultural domains, text-response benchmarks are insufficient, and findings have implications for EU AI Code of Practice systemic risk framework.

title: Vision-language models for chest radiography do not always need the image
summary: Medical vision-language models report strong chest radiograph accuracy, and this is increasingly read as evidence that they use the image. That inference is unsafe: a model exploiting finding-name priors scores like one that reads the scan, and no standard benchmark separates them. We introduce a causal audit that intervenes on the image, occluding the relevant region, occluding an irrelevant one, and swapping in another patient's same-label scan, and combines three behavioral metrics to test whether a correct answer depends on the image. Across nine systems, a text-only model with no image access reaches within 5.7 accuracy points of the best multimodal one, and a 119-billion-parameter multimodal model is statistically indistinguishable from a 7-billion text-only baseline. The audit splits the cohort into three models that ignore the image, one that is unstable, and five that use it selectively, for a subset of findings; the categories hold across a second dataset, resolution, and prompt phrasing. Against board-certified radiologists, a text-only model is statistically indistinguishable from a radiologist's accuracy while grounding at zero, whereas the image-using models ground at radiologist-comparable rates. Reported confidence flags ungrounded answers only when a model uses the image. Grounding audits, not accuracy, should gate clinical deployment.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a causal audit method to determine whether medical vision-language models actually use chest X-ray images or rely on text-only priors. Testing nine systems, they find that text-only models perform nearly as well as multimodal ones, with most models either ignoring or only selectively using image information. The authors argue that grounding audits, not accuracy, should determine clinical readiness.
AI.motivation: Current accuracy benchmarks cannot distinguish whether a model genuinely reads the chest radiograph or exploits finding-name priors (statistical associations in text), making standard evaluations unsafe for validating image understanding in clinical AI.
AI.method: The authors design a causal audit that intervenes on images by occluding relevant vs. irrelevant regions and swapping in another patient's same-label scan, then combines three behavioral metrics to test if correct answers depend on image content.
AI.result: (1) A text-only model reaches within 5.7 accuracy points of the best multimodal model; (2) A 119B-parameter multimodal model is statistically indistinguishable from a 7B text-only baseline; (3) The audit splits models into those ignoring image, unstable, or selectively using it; (4) Text-only models match radiologist accuracy but ground at zero, while image-using models ground at radiologist-comparable rates.
AI.conclusion: Grounding audits (testing whether predictions depend on actual image evidence) should gate clinical deployment rather than accuracy benchmarks, because accuracy alone masks whether models truly use visual information.

title: TuneAhead: Predicting Fine-tuning Performance Before Full Training Begins
summary: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) is compute-intensive and error-prone: model performance depends sensitively on data quality and hyperparameter choices, and naïve runs can even degrade model performance. This raises a practical question:can we predict fine-tuning performance before committing to a full training run? We present TUNEAHEAD, a lightweight framework for pre-hoc prediction of fine-tuning performance. TUNEAHEAD encodes each candidate run as a meta-feature vector that combines static dataset descriptors with dynamic probe features from a short standardized probe. A predictor maps these features to performance estimates, while SHAP-based attributions provide interpretable diagnostics that reveal which specific features drive the prediction. Across 1,300+ fine-tuning runs on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, TUNEAHEAD consistently outperforms strong baselines such as Early-Stop Extrapolation and ProxyLM. On a held-out test set of 370 runs, TUNEAHEAD achieves an RMSE of 1.47 percentage points and places 95.1% of predictions within +3/-3 percentage points of the true score. These accurate continuous predictions support practical go/no-go screening policies that can reduce unnecessary full fine-tuning while retaining most promising runs.

AI.tldr: TUNEAHEAD is a lightweight framework that predicts LLM fine-tuning performance before full training runs by encoding meta-features from dataset descriptors and probe runs, achieving 1.47 pp RMSE and 95.1% predictions within ±3 pp on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning LLMs is compute-intensive and error-prone, with performance heavily dependent on data quality and hyperparameters; naïve runs can even degrade performance. The key practical question is whether we can predict fine-tuning performance before committing to a full training run.
AI.method: TUNEAHEAD encodes each candidate run as a meta-feature vector combining static dataset descriptors with dynamic probe features from a short standardized probe. A predictor maps these features to performance estimates, with SHAP-based attributions for interpretable diagnostics.
AI.result: On 1,300+ fine-tuning runs with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, TUNEAHEAD outperforms Early-Stop Extrapolation and ProxyLM. On a held-out test set of 370 runs, it achieves RMSE of 1.47 percentage points and places 95.1% of predictions within ±3 pp of the true score.
AI.conclusion: Accurate continuous predictions from TUNEAHEAD support practical go/no-go screening policies that can reduce unnecessary full fine-tuning while retaining most promising runs.

title: Structured Adversarial Camouflage via Voronoi Diagrams
summary:
Pixel-wise adversarial patches are computationally heavy and often visually detectable, limiting utility in security-critical systems. We present adversarial Voronoi camouflage that optimizes only seed-point locations under fixed, printable palettes using a soft assignment, producing structured, splinter camouflage-like patterns without additional regularization. Evaluated on person detection with COCO-style AP@[.5:.95], naive placement (Inria -> COCO) performs comparably bad, while garment-level application via segmentation mask (3DPeople) results in a significant AP drop. The attack transfers to out-of-domain backgrounds and across detector families (YOLOv9/10/11/12), indicating robustness in black-box settings. Repainting with different palettes largely nullifies the effect, and single-color tweaks show limited tolerance (<=0.17), highlighting a structure-palette coupling. The parameter-efficient, palette-constrained design improves visual plausibility while degrading real-time detector performance. Physical validation and color calibration are left for future work.
  Code: https://github.com/JensBayer/Voronoi
  This paper was originally presented at the International Conference on Military Communication and Information Systems (ICMCIS), organized by the Information Systems Technology (IST) Scientific and Technical Committee, IST-224-RSY - the ICMCIS, held in Bath, United Kingdom, 12-13 May 2026.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes adversarial Voronoi camouflage that optimizes seed-point locations under fixed, printable palettes via soft assignment, generating structured patterns that degrade person detector performance (e.g., YOLOv9-12) while improving visual plausibility over traditional adversarial patches.
AI.motivation: Existing pixel-wise adversarial patches are computationally expensive and visually conspicuous, limiting their practicality in security and military contexts.
AI.method: Optimizes only seed-point locations of Voronoi tessellations under fixed, printable color palettes using a soft assignment function, producing structured splinter-like camouflage patterns without extra regularization terms.
AI.result: Garment-level application via segmentation masks (3DPeople) causes significant AP drop; attack transfers across backgrounds and detector families (YOLOv9-12); repainting with different palettes largely nullifies the effect; single-color tweaks show limited tolerance (≤0.17).
AI.conclusion: The parameter-efficient, palette-constrained Voronoi design improves visual plausibility while degrading detector performance, though physical validation and color calibration remain as future work.

title: Handling Feature Heterogeneity with Learnable Graph Patches
summary: In recent years, the rapid development of foundation models and graph pre-training technologies has spurred increasing interest in constructing a universal pre-trained graph model or Graph Foundation Model (GFM). However, a significant challenge is that existing models are unable to address feature heterogeneity in graph data without textual information, which hinders the transferability of graph models across different datasets. To bridge this gap, we propose the concept of learnable graph patches, which we regard as the smallest semantic units of any graph data. We decompose the graph into learnable graph patches by unfolding the node features and constructing corresponding patch structures separately. We then design a framework that mines transferable information from graph data across domains. Specifically, after extracting graph patches, we propose a patch encoder to extract knowledge from each unit and a patch aggregator to learn how the units are combined into a whole. Due to its domain-agnostic nature, the model can be applied to downstream data across different domains. Furthermore, we analyze the connection between our method and existing graph models, as well as the transferability of the node embeddings it generates. Empirically, our method not only achieves the capability to use multi-domain graphs for pre-training, but also shows enhanced performance across various downstream datasets and tasks. Moreover, we observe consistent improvement in downstream performance as the volume of pre-training data increases.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces learnable graph patches as the smallest semantic units of graph data to address feature heterogeneity across domains. The proposed framework extracts domain-agnostic patches via a patch encoder and aggregator, enabling pre-training on multi-domain graphs and improving downstream transfer performance.
AI.motivation: Existing graph foundation models cannot handle feature heterogeneity in graph data without textual information, limiting their transferability across different datasets and domains.
AI.method: The authors propose learnable graph patches (smallest semantic units), decompose graphs by unfolding node features and constructing patch structures, then use a patch encoder to extract knowledge from each unit and a patch aggregator to combine units into a whole graph representation.
AI.result: The method achieves multi-domain graph pre-training capability and shows enhanced performance across various downstream datasets and tasks. Performance consistently improves with increased pre-training data volume.
AI.conclusion: Learnable graph patches provide a domain-agnostic representation that enables effective cross-domain transfer for graph foundation models, with scalability benefits from larger pre-training data.

title: WEQA: Wearable hEalth Question Answering with Query-Adaptive Agentic Reasoning
summary: Language models are remarkably capable at medical question answering, in some cases surpassing the accuracy of general physicians. However, answering questions about wearable health data remains challenging and understudied, as these ubiquitous sensors produce continuous, high-dimensional, and longitudinal data, which is non-trivial to align with text-centric distributions in LLM pretraining. The diversity of sensor modalities and user intents cannot be effectively handled by a fixed reasoning workflow or a single pretrained foundation model. To address these challenges, we propose WEQA, a query-adaptive agent framework that unifies LLM reasoning with specialized wearable analytical and modeling tools. An LLM controller is employed to synthesize execution plans and dynamically route each query to the appropriate combination of sensor analysis and pretrained models, and perform grounded response auditing with external knowledge. We also curate a benchmark spanning four open wearable datasets comprising analytic and predictive tasks in three different health domains. Experiments show that our framework is 24% more accurate than LLM and agentic baselines, and a blinded study with 12 medical experts and 8 users shows substantial gains in usefulness and clinical soundness.

AI.tldr: WEQA is a query-adaptive agent framework that combines LLM reasoning with specialized wearable analytics tools to answer health questions from wearable sensor data, achieving 24% higher accuracy than baselines and receiving positive evaluations from medical experts and users.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs struggle to answer questions about wearable health data because these sensors produce continuous, high-dimensional, longitudinal data that doesn't align well with text-centric LLM pretraining distributions, and the diversity of sensor modalities and user intents cannot be handled by fixed reasoning workflows or single foundation models.
AI.method: WEQA uses an LLM controller to synthesize execution plans and dynamically route each query to appropriate combinations of sensor analysis and pretrained models, with grounded response auditing using external knowledge.
AI.result: The framework is 24% more accurate than LLM and agentic baselines. A blinded study with 12 medical experts and 8 users demonstrated substantial gains in usefulness and clinical soundness.
AI.conclusion: The query-adaptive agent framework effectively addresses the challenges of wearable health data question answering by unifying LLM reasoning with specialized tools, outperforming existing approaches in both accuracy and clinical relevance.

title: Heterogeneous SAR-optical fusion for near-real-time land use and land cover mapping under cloud contamination: A novel framework and global benchmark dataset
summary: Optical remote sensing imagery is frequently degraded by cloud and cloud-shadow contamination, which limits its reliability for near-real-time land use and land cover (LULC) mapping. Although synthetic aperture radar (SAR) can provide cloud-penetrating structural information, existing SAR-optical fusion methods often assume reliable optical observations and insufficiently address the semantic uncertainty introduced by cloud contamination. To address this issue, we propose CloudLULC-Net, an end-to-end heterogeneous SAR-optical fusion framework that directly predicts LULC maps from cloud-contaminated Sentinel-2 imagery and temporally adjacent Sentinel-1 SAR observations. The proposed network incorporates optical reliability modulation to suppress unreliable optical responses, heterogeneous information adaptive aggregation to model high-order spatial-channel interactions between optical and SAR representations, and a unified semantic mapping transformer to organize fused features in a LULC-oriented latent space. A semantic anchor-guided optimization strategy is further introduced to improve the consistency of intermediate semantic representations. To support this task, we construct CloudLULC-Set, a large-scale benchmark dataset containing 40,223 curated SAR-optical-label triplets with pixel-level LULC annotations across diverse geographic regions and cloud conditions. Experimental results show that CloudLULC-Net achieves an OA of 86.60%, an F1-score of 83.29%, and an mIoU of 73.51%, outperforming representative heterogeneous reconstruction-first and end-to-end SAR-optical mapping methods. Comparisons with existing global LULC products and analyses under different cloud-cover levels further demonstrate the robustness and practical value of CloudLULC-Net for target-date LULC mapping in cloud-prone regions.The project is publicly available at: https://github.com/RSIIPAC/CloudLULC

AI.tldr: CloudLULC-Net is an end-to-end SAR-optical fusion framework that directly maps LULC from cloud-contaminated Sentinel-2 imagery and Sentinel-1 SAR data. It includes optical reliability modulation, heterogeneous information adaptive aggregation, a unified semantic mapping transformer, and a semantic anchor-guided optimization strategy. A large benchmark dataset (CloudLULC-Set) with 40,223 triplets is constructed. The method achieves 86.60% OA, 83.29% F1-score, and 73.51% mIoU, outperforming existing methods under cloud conditions.
AI.motivation: Optical remote sensing imagery is frequently degraded by cloud and cloud-shadow contamination, which limits its reliability for near-real-time LULC mapping. Existing SAR-optical fusion methods often assume reliable optical observations and insufficiently address the semantic uncertainty introduced by cloud contamination.
AI.method: CloudLULC-Net incorporates: (1) optical reliability modulation to suppress unreliable optical responses, (2) heterogeneous information adaptive aggregation for high-order spatial-channel interactions, (3) a unified semantic mapping transformer for LULC-oriented feature organization, and (4) a semantic anchor-guided optimization strategy for consistent intermediate representations. A large-scale benchmark dataset CloudLULC-Set (40,223 triplets) was also constructed.
AI.result: CloudLULC-Net achieves 86.60% OA, 83.29% F1-score, and 73.51% mIoU, outperforming representative heterogeneous reconstruction-first and end-to-end SAR-optical mapping methods. Robustness is demonstrated across different cloud-cover levels and in comparison with existing global LULC products.
AI.conclusion: CloudLULC-Net provides a robust and practical solution for target-date LULC mapping in cloud-prone regions by effectively fusing SAR and cloud-contaminated optical imagery, addressing semantic uncertainty through reliability modulation and adaptive aggregation.

title: ASTEROID: A Spatiotemporal Information Transformer for Forecasting Multi-Step Time Series of Molecular Dynamics
summary: Molecular dynamics (MD) simulation is computationally demanding, particularly for large-scale systems requiring long-term analysis. Accurate forecast of the outcomes of a MD simulation is not only an attractive scientific challenge but also has substantial practical value. In this work, we developed a data-driven framework, termed ASTEROID (Advanced Spatiotemporal TransformER fOr Inferring Dynamics), that can directly predict multi-step atomic coordinates, avoiding conventional iterative integration. For this purpose, our ASTEROID reformulates MD trajectories as high-dimensional spatiotemporal sequences and integrates the Spatiotemporal Information (STI) Transformation equation into a Transformer architecture. The core innovation of ASTEROID lies in its ability to model multiscale spatiotemporal dependencies. In particular, for spatial dependencies, a local-global self-attention mechanism captures both short- and long-range interactions. For temporal dependencies, an encoder-decoder structure integrates global context with autoregressive forecasting. ASTEROID was evaluated on several quantum-mechanics derived molecular datasets. Our results indicate that ASTEROID achieved not only a higher level of accuracy in multi-step prediction than existing methods on various benchmarks, but also significantly reduced computational cost of conventional MD simulation. Moreover, the model supports iterative multi-step forecasting over an extended time scale. This work establishes a robust and generalizable data-driven paradigm for accelerating MD simulations.

AI.tldr: ASTEROID is a Transformer-based framework that predicts multi-step atomic coordinates in molecular dynamics simulations by modeling spatiotemporal dependencies, achieving higher accuracy and lower computational cost compared to conventional MD methods.
AI.motivation: MD simulations are computationally expensive for large-scale, long-term systems; accurate prediction of simulation outcomes would save significant computational resources while maintaining scientific value.
AI.method: ASTEROID reformulates MD trajectories as high-dimensional spatiotemporal sequences, integrates Spatiotemporal Information (STI) Transformation into a Transformer architecture, uses local-global self-attention for spatial dependencies, and an encoder-decoder with autoregressive forecasting for temporal dependencies.
AI.result: ASTEROID achieved higher accuracy in multi-step prediction than existing methods on quantum-mechanics molecular benchmarks, significantly reduced computational cost compared to conventional MD, and supports iterative multi-step forecasting over extended time scales.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a robust and generalizable data-driven paradigm for accelerating MD simulations, demonstrating that direct coordinate prediction via Transformer architectures can effectively replace iterative numerical integration.

title: Learning Cardiac Electrophysiology Digital Twins Through Agentic Discovery of Hybrid Structure
summary: Building personalized cardiac electrophysiology (EP) digital twins requires identifying the appropriate model structure for each patient, not merely fitting parameters. Traditional methods rely on experts to manually prescribe hybrid physics-neural architectures, which requires deep domain expertise and does not transfer across patients. Recent works have applied large language models (LLMs) to generate or act as hybrid models. However, despite their promising generalization capacity, these LLM-based methods lack the structural priors needed for stable cardiac simulations. Hence, we propose LEADS, a framework that formulates cardiac EP domain knowledge as a structured action space and utilizes an LLM agent to discover hybrid models. The agent follows an iterative reasoning-and-action loop to select, combine, and refine hybrid models, whilst gradient descent handles parameter fitting. The proposed LEADS designs every candidate model towards physically grounded, interpretable, and numerically stable, while allowing open-ended architectural discovery. We validate LEADS on synthetic data with three ground-truth reaction models and on real cardiac EP data, demonstrating that it outperforms both human-designed hybrid models and other LLM-based hybrid modeling.

AI.tldr: LEADS uses an LLM agent operating in a structured action space to automatically discover personalized, physically grounded hybrid models for cardiac electrophysiology digital twins, outperforming human-designed and other LLM-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Traditional methods require experts to manually design hybrid physics-neural architectures per patient, which is labor-intensive and non-transferable. Existing LLM-based methods lack structural priors needed for stable cardiac simulations.
AI.method: Formulates cardiac EP domain knowledge as a structured action space, where an LLM agent iteratively reasons and acts to select, combine, and refine hybrid models, while gradient descent handles parameter fitting.
AI.result: Outperforms both human-designed hybrid models and other LLM-based hybrid modeling on synthetic data (three ground-truth reaction models) and real cardiac EP data.
AI.conclusion: LEADS effectively balances domain-specific structural priors with LLM-driven architectural discovery for personalized cardiac EP digital twins.

title: GSPan: A Continuous Gaussian Primitive Representation for Arbitrary-Scale Pansharpening
summary: Pansharpening aims to generate high-resolution multispectral (HRMS) images by fusing low-resolution multispectral (LRMS) and panchromatic (PAN) observations. Most existing deep learning methods treat pansharpening as fixed-grid prediction, which limits scale adaptation. To address this, we propose GSPan, a framework that introduces 2D Gaussian Splatting (GS) into pansharpening. Instead of directly predicting pixels, GSPan represents band-wise residual details as continuous and learnable 2D Gaussian primitives. We design a Dual-Stream Hierarchical Interaction (DSHI) architecture with a Spatial-Spectral Interactive Attention (SSIA) module to estimate these primitives from complementary PAN and MS observations. The predicted primitives are rendered as a residual detail field and injected into the upsampled MS image. This continuous representation allows GSPan to render fused images on arbitrary target sampling grids without scale-specific retraining. It further enables a Scale-Decoupled Asymmetric Inference (SDAI) strategy, which estimates primitives at a reduced resolution and renders the fused image at the target resolution for efficient large-scene pansharpening. Experiments on QuickBird, GaoFen-2, WorldView-3, and WorldView-3-4K datasets show that GSPan delivers state-of-the-art fusion performance. Moreover, SDAI markedly accelerates inference, achieving a favorable trade-off between computational efficiency and fusion quality. Our results demonstrate the potential of continuous Gaussian residual representations as a flexible and scale-decoupled alternative to fixed-grid prediction.

AI.tldr: GSPan introduces 2D Gaussian Splatting into pansharpening to represent residual details as continuous primitives, enabling scale-adaptive fusion without retraining and achieving SOTA results across multiple satellite datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning pansharpening methods treat the task as fixed-grid prediction, limiting their ability to adapt to different output scales without retraining.
AI.method: GSPan uses a Dual-Stream Hierarchical Interaction (DSHI) architecture with Spatial-Spectral Interactive Attention (SSIA) to estimate 2D Gaussian primitives from PAN and MS inputs. These primitives are rendered as continuous residual detail fields and injected into upsampled MS images. A Scale-Decoupled Asymmetric Inference (SDAI) strategy further enables efficient large-scene processing.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art fusion performance on QuickBird, GaoFen-2, WorldView-3, and WorldView-3-4K datasets. SDAI significantly accelerates inference while maintaining good fusion quality.
AI.conclusion: Continuous Gaussian residual representations offer a flexible, scale-decoupled alternative to fixed-grid prediction, overcoming the scale adaptation limitation in pansharpening.

title: EnvRL: Learn from Environment Dynamics in Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for training Large Language Models (LLMs) as agents. However, conventional RL methods for long-horizon agentic tasks often struggle with sparse outcome rewards. Intuitively, this overlooks the rich environment dynamics information contained in rollout interaction trajectories. We argue that the interaction experience inherently serves as an implicit supervision signal, reveals the underlying transition mechanisms of the environment, and enables the agent to construct a more accurate internal model of the environment.. Therefore, in this work, we investigate how to leverage this additional signal to improve policy learning. Specifically, we propose EnvRL, a framework that incorporates environment dynamics learning into agentic RL via two auxiliary objectives: state prediction and inverse dynamics. By jointly optimizing with the primary RL objective, we encourage the agent to internalize environment dynamics from its own interaction experience. Extensive experiments on two long-horizon agentic benchmarks demonstrate that EnvRL achieves significant improvements on success-rates over RL-only baselines, e.g., when trained with GRPO, lifting Qwen-2.5-1.5B-Instruct from 72.8% to 77.4% on ALFWorld, and from 56.8% to 67.0% on WebShop.

AI.tldr: EnvRL enhances RL-based LLM training by incorporating environment dynamics learning (state prediction + inverse dynamics) as auxiliary objectives, significantly improving success rates on ALFWorld and WebShop tasks.
AI.motivation: Conventional RL methods for long-horizon agentic tasks struggle with sparse outcome rewards, ignoring rich environment dynamics information embedded in rollout interaction trajectories.
AI.method: EnvRL integrates two auxiliary objectives—state prediction and inverse dynamics—alongside the primary RL objective to help the agent internalize environment dynamics from its own interaction experience.
AI.result: On ALFWorld, GRPO+EnvRL improved Qwen-2.5-1.5B-Instruct from 72.8% to 77.4%. On WebShop, from 56.8% to 67.0%.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging interaction trajectories as implicit supervision for environment dynamics learning substantially boosts policy learning in long-horizon agentic RL tasks.

title: DRFLOW: A Deep Research Benchmark for Personalized Workflow Prediction
summary: Deep research (DR) systems are increasingly used for complex information-seeking tasks, but existing works mainly focus on generating reports and summaries. In contrast, many enterprise tasks instead require an agent to identify concrete workflows which is a sequence of action-steps. For example, rather than summarizing budgeting policies, an agent should be able to determine the steps needed to answer a question such as: "How do I request new headcount given a fixed budget?". Therefore, we introduce DRFLOW, a benchmark for evaluating personalized workflows predicted by agents from heterogeneous sources. Each task requires the agent to identify relevant evidence from scattered sources, then use that evidence to predict the correct action-step sequence for the user's task. DRFLOW contains 100 tasks across five domains, with 1,246 reference workflow steps grounded in more than 3,900 sources. We define seven diagnostic metrics covering factual grounding, step recovery, structural ordering, condition resolution, and personalization. We further present DRFLOW-Agent (DRFA), a workflow-oriented reference agent to predict personalized workflow. We show that although DRFA improves over strong baseline agents (upto 10.02% average F1 score), there is substantial room for improvement remains across these workflow metrics, indicating that predicting complete and correct personalized workflows remains a challenging frontier for deep research.

AI.tldr: DRFLOW is a benchmark for evaluating how well AI agents can predict personalized workflows (sequences of action-steps) from diverse sources, rather than just generating summaries. It includes 100 tasks across 5 domains with 1,246 reference steps from 3,900+ sources, and introduces 7 diagnostic metrics. Their proposed DRFLOW-Agent improves over baselines but still has significant room for improvement.
AI.motivation: Existing deep research systems focus on generating reports and summaries, but enterprise tasks often require identifying concrete workflows (sequences of action-steps). For example, instead of summarizing budgeting policies, an agent should determine steps like "How do I request new headcount given a fixed budget?"
AI.method: The authors introduce DRFLOW, a benchmark with 100 tasks across 5 domains, 1,246 reference workflow steps grounded in 3,900+ sources. They define 7 diagnostic metrics covering factual grounding, step recovery, structural ordering, condition resolution, and personalization. They also present DRFLOW-Agent (DRFA), a workflow-oriented reference agent.
AI.result: DRFLOW-Agent improves over strong baseline agents by up to 10.02% average F1 score, but substantial room for improvement remains across all workflow metrics.
AI.conclusion: Predicting complete and correct personalized workflows remains a challenging frontier for deep research, despite the improvements offered by DRFLOW-Agent.

title: ActWorld: From Explorable to Interactive World Model via Action-Aware Memory
summary: Interactive world models aim to simulate environment dynamics under real-time user actions. However, their action vocabulary is largely confined to navigation: most actions correspond to motion (e.g., walk, turn, look around), while interaction with objects in the scene (e.g., pick up plates, open doors, or trigger physical responses) is either absent, restricted to game domains, or relegated to prompt-to-full-video scenarios. The resulting worlds are visually explorable but not truly actionable. In this work, we present ActWorld, an interactive world model that extends prior navigation-centric generators to support mid-rollout object interaction within a chunk-autoregressive framework. We argue that the navigation-interaction gap stems from two bottlenecks. First, a data bottleneck: the lack of human-object interaction data with accurate, dense labels. Second, a memory bottleneck: recency-biased history compression in existing world models discards the event-transition frames that causally determine subsequent object states, leading to an action-forgetting pathology. On the data side, we construct a 100K interaction video dataset, each annotated with per-chunk captions via chain-of-thought reasoning. On the model side, we introduce a hierarchical action-aware memory design that routes history compression by interaction importance, complemented by a persistent memory bank that maintains event-update and object-identity tokens across long rollouts. Experiments show that ActWorld supports both flexible navigation and rich object interaction within a single model, substantially improving interaction fidelity over navigation-only baselines without sacrificing viewpoint control. Project page is available at https://interactwm.github.io/ActWorld.

AI.tldr: ActWorld extends navigation-centric world models to support real-time object interaction (e.g., picking up plates, opening doors) via a chunk-autoregressive framework with a hierarchical action-aware memory design and a 100K interaction video dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing interactive world models are mostly limited to navigation actions (walk, turn), lacking object interaction capabilities due to (1) a data bottleneck — scarcity of human-object interaction data with dense labels, and (2) a memory bottleneck — recency-biased history compression that discards causally important event-transition frames.
AI.method: ActWorld uses a chunk-autoregressive framework with a hierarchical action-aware memory design that routes history compression by interaction importance, plus a persistent memory bank maintaining event-update and object-identity tokens across long rollouts. A 100K interaction video dataset with per-chunk chain-of-thought captions is also constructed.
AI.result: ActWorld supports both flexible navigation and rich object interaction within a single model, substantially improving interaction fidelity over navigation-only baselines without sacrificing viewpoint control.
AI.conclusion: Bridging the navigation-interaction gap in world models requires addressing both data and memory bottlenecks; ActWorld demonstrates that hierarchical, interaction-aware memory and targeted dataset construction enable actionable, visually explorable environments.

title: Delta-Based Target Reformulation for Short-Term Electricity Load Forecasting Using LSTM and Transformer Models
summary:
Accurate short-term electricity load forecasting is critical for the reliable and economic operation of modern power systems, under non-stationarity arising from weather variability, calendar effects, and evolving consumption patterns. While deep learning models such as LSTMs and Transformers show promising performance, most existing studies focus on direct absolute load prediction without explicitly addressing target non-stationarity.
  Motivated by classical time-series differencing techniques in ARIMA models, this paper investigates a delta-based target reformulation for short-term electricity load forecasting using deep learning. Instead of directly predicting absolute load values, the proposed formulation trains models to predict the change in load between consecutive time steps, with final forecasts reconstructed using the last observed load. This aims to stabilize the learning target and reduce forecasting difficulty.
  Using multi-year, hourly real-world electricity load data from India, augmented with meteorological variables from the NASA POWER project and calendar features, this study evaluates LSTM and Transformer models under both formulations, benchmarking them against LightGBM. Experiments are conducted for hour-ahead and day-ahead horizons, assessing performance via Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE).
  Results show that delta-based reformulation consistently improves forecasting accuracy for hour-ahead prediction across all evaluated models, yielding MAPE reductions of over 50% compared to absolute formulations. For day-ahead forecasting, delta targets specifically benefit deep sequence models (LSTM and Transformer), while LightGBM remains competitive under the absolute formulation. These findings indicate that while delta reformulation is a powerful inductive bias for neural networks, its efficacy is model- and horizon-dependent.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a delta-based (change prediction) reformulation for short-term electricity load forecasting using deep learning, showing over 50% MAPE reduction for hour-ahead predictions, with effectiveness varying by model and horizon.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning models for load forecasting focus on direct absolute load prediction without addressing target non-stationarity caused by weather, calendar effects, and changing consumption patterns.
AI.method: Models predict the change in load (delta) between consecutive time steps instead of absolute load values, with final forecasts reconstructed using the last observed load. LSTM and Transformer models are evaluated under both delta and absolute formulations, benchmarked against LightGBM.
AI.result: Delta-based reformulation consistently improves hour-ahead prediction across all models with >50% MAPE reduction. For day-ahead, delta targets benefit LSTM and Transformer but not LightGBM, which remains competitive under absolute formulation.
AI.conclusion: Delta reformulation is a powerful inductive bias for neural networks in load forecasting, but its efficacy is model- and horizon-dependent.

title: The Stanford EDGAR Filings Dataset: Reconstructing U.S. Corporate and Financial Disclosures into Layout-Faithful and Token-Efficient Pretraining Data
comment: Preprint. Includes appendix, tables, and figures
summary: As high-quality public web corpora become increasingly exhausted, clean long-context documents have become a scarce and expensive source of training data for large language models (LLMs). Existing long-context corpora are often proprietary and costly to acquire, synthetically generated, or concentrated in narrow domains such as programming. We introduce the Stanford EDGAR Filings Dataset (SEFD), an open reconstruction of SEC filings into layout-faithful MultiMarkdown for financial language modeling and evaluation. SEFD makes audited financial statements, risk disclosures, ownership reports, accounting notes, and market-moving event filings usable as long-context pretraining data and as a basis for financial reasoning, forecasting, compliance, and document understanding. The resulting corpus is token-efficient, model-ready, and has less than 0.1% overlap with Common Crawl-derived corpora. We release SEFD-v1, a 152B-token initial public snapshot, and provide corpus-level analyses of a larger 18.5M-filing archive estimated at 550B tokens. We further introduce two SEFD-derived benchmarks: EDGAR-Forecast, which evaluates filing-grounded numerical forecasting after model knowledge cutoffs, and EDGAR-OCR, which evaluates transcription of complex financial tables.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Stanford EDGAR Filings Dataset (SEFD), a 152B-token open-source corpus of SEC filings in MultiMarkdown format for financial LLM training and evaluation, with less than 0.1% overlap with Common Crawl, plus two new benchmarks (EDGAR-Forecast and EDGAR-OCR).
AI.motivation: High-quality long-context training data (especially clean financial documents) is becoming scarce and expensive; existing options are proprietary, synthetic, or domain-limited (e.g., programming).
AI.method: Reconstructs SEC EDGAR filings into layout-faithful MultiMarkdown format, creating a large-scale (152B-token snapshot, 550B-token archive) open corpus with minimal Common Crawl overlap.
AI.result: SEFD-v1 is 152B tokens with <0.1% Common Crawl overlap; the full archive is ~550B tokens across 18.5M filings.
AI.conclusion: SEFD provides a valuable, token-efficient, model-ready long-context pretraining resource for financial NLP, along with two evaluation benchmarks for forecasting and OCR tasks.

title: BrainWorld: A Structural-Prior-Conditioned Generative Model for Whole-Brain 4D fMRI Dynamics
summary: Whole-brain 4D fMRI generation is valuable for modeling functional brain dynamics, yet existing fMRI foundation models mainly target representation learning and downstream prediction rather than conditional predictive generation. We introduce BrainWorld, a structural-prior-conditioned generative model for whole-brain 4D fMRI dynamics. BrainWorld uses sMRI as subject-level anatomical context to guide future fMRI generation, integrating structural information into the denoising process rather than treating it as a parallel modality. Evaluated on 22 datasets spanning diverse cohorts and brain states, BrainWorld generates stable 4D fMRI trajectories up to 400 frames, improves downstream performance through generated-example augmentation, and learns transferable multimodal representations that outperform baselines. Together, these results establish BrainWorld as a condition-aware generative framework for long-horizon brain dynamics modeling and multimodal representation learning.

AI.tldr: BrainWorld is a generative model that uses structural MRI as context to generate whole-brain 4D fMRI dynamics, achieving stable long-horizon generation (up to 400 frames) and improving downstream tasks across 22 datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing fMRI foundation models focus on representation learning and prediction tasks, but lack the capability for conditional predictive generation of whole-brain 4D fMRI dynamics.
AI.method: BrainWorld integrates structural MRI (sMRI) as subject-level anatomical context into the denoising process of a generative model, conditioning future fMRI generation on structural priors rather than treating sMRI as a separate modality.
AI.result: Evaluated on 22 datasets, BrainWorld generates stable 4D fMRI trajectories up to 400 frames, improves downstream performance via generated-example augmentation, and learns transferable multimodal representations that outperform baselines.
AI.conclusion: BrainWorld establishes a condition-aware generative framework for long-horizon brain dynamics modeling and multimodal representation learning.

title: Confusion-Aware Transfer Teacher Curriculum Learning Framework: Disentangling Scoring and Pacing Effects
comment: Accepted at International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) GlobalSouthML Workshop (2026)
summary: Curriculum learning couples two design choices, how samples are scored by difficulty and how harder samples are paced into training, making it difficult to attribute observed gains to either component. We disentangle these factors with two evaluation protocols: stage-wise test subsets that validate scoring functions independently of curriculum training, and a baseline that applies the same pacing schedule to randomly ordered data. Within the Transfer Teacher framework (TTF), we use these protocols to evaluate a confusion-aware difficulty score that considers both correct-class confidence and the probability distribution over incorrect classes. On CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18 and VGG-16, the proposed score produces model-interpretable difficulty rankings that align with human intuition. However, at full data, neither curriculum nor anti-curriculum ordering improves accuracy over standard training, indicating that improving the scoring function alone is insufficient to overcome the known failure modes of curriculum learning in TTF. In contrast, We find that confusion-aware curriculum ordering result in consistent data-efficiency benefits, outperforming random ordering by up to 8.7% points at the 20% data regime, suggesting the potential of TTF as a data-efficient training method.

AI.tldr: This paper disentangles scoring and pacing in curriculum learning, finding that confusion-aware difficulty scores help in data-efficient regimes (up to 8.7% improvement at 20% data) but not in full-data training, suggesting TTF's potential for data efficiency.
AI.motivation: Curriculum learning couples sample scoring and pacing, making it hard to attribute observed gains to either component. The authors aim to disentangle these factors.
AI.method: Two evaluation protocols: (1) stage-wise test subsets to validate scoring functions independently, (2) a baseline applying the same pacing schedule to randomly ordered data. They propose a confusion-aware difficulty score considering both correct-class confidence and distribution over incorrect classes.
AI.result: The confusion-aware score produces interpretable rankings aligned with human intuition. At full data, no improvement over standard training. At 20% data, up to 8.7% improvement over random ordering.
AI.conclusion: Improving scoring alone is insufficient to overcome curriculum learning's failure modes in TTF at full data, but shows promise for data-efficient training.

title: Fixed-Point Reasoners: Stable and Adaptive Deep Looped Transformers
summary: Looped architectures provide an inductive bias toward learning step-by-step procedures for tasks that require compositional reasoning. The number of effective layers reached by looping determines the quality of the solution these models find. Like deep architectures, looped architectures are prone to a signal propagation problem induced by depth as the halting decision is postponed. In this paper, we address this signal propagation issue using pre-norm layers and residual scaling. Building on these architectural modifications, we propose FPRM, a Transformer-based Fixed-Point Reasoning Model that uses fixed-point convergence as an end-to-end halting mechanism in a looped architecture. We show that fixed-point halting allows FPRM to adapt its compute to task difficulty. FPRM is effective on common reasoning benchmarks, namely Sudoku, Maze, state-tracking, and ARC-AGI.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes FPRM, a Transformer with looped architecture using fixed-point convergence for halting, which adapts computation to task difficulty and performs well on reasoning benchmarks like Sudoku, Maze, state-tracking, and ARC-AGI.
AI.motivation: Looped architectures suffer from signal propagation problems due to depth when halting is delayed. The paper aims to address this issue to enable effective compositional reasoning.
AI.method: Uses pre-norm layers and residual scaling to fix signal propagation, then proposes FPRM—a Transformer-based Fixed-Point Reasoning Model with fixed-point convergence as an end-to-end halting mechanism in a looped architecture.
AI.result: FPRM adapts compute to task difficulty and is effective on Sudoku, Maze, state-tracking, and ARC-AGI benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Fixed-point halting in looped architectures enables compute-adaptive reasoning, and architectural fixes (pre-norm, residual scaling) solve depth-related signal propagation issues.

title: LiveStarPro: Proactive Streaming Video Understanding with Hierarchical Memory for Long-Horizon Streams
summary: Despite the remarkable progress of Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs), current online architectures still struggle to simultaneously process continuous video streams, decide autonomously when to respond, and preserve long-horizon contextual memory. These obstacles undermine real-time responsiveness and cause severe forgetting throughout prolonged interactions. In this work, we introduce LiveStarPro, a live streaming assistant that is designed for proactive video understanding over long-horizon streams. The design of LiveStarPro rests on three complementary components. The first component is Streaming Verification Decoding (SVeD), an inference framework that identifies the appropriate response timing through single-pass perplexity verification, thereby eliminating the dependency on explicit silence tokens. The second component is Streaming Causal Attention Masks (SCAM), a training strategy that enforces incremental video-language alignment over variable-length streams. The third component is Tree-Structured Hierarchical Memory (TSHM), a recursive memory architecture that organizes evicted historical information into event chains and consequently enables efficient retrieval from effectively unbounded video streams. To facilitate a comprehensive evaluation under realistic online conditions, we further present OmniStarPro, a large-scale benchmark that spans 15 diverse real-world scenarios and that extends to hour-scale streams for the assessment of long-term recall. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LiveStarPro consistently surpasses existing methods, attaining a 28.9% improvement in semantic correctness and an 18.2% reduction in timing error, while its streaming key-value cache further yields a 1.58x inference speedup over the same model without caching. The model and the code are publicly available at https://github.com/sotayang/LiveStarPro.

AI.tldr: LiveStarPro is a live streaming Video-LLM that improves real-time proactive video understanding through three key innovations: Streaming Verification Decoding (SVeD) for automatic response timing, Streaming Causal Attention Masks (SCAM) for incremental video-language alignment, and Tree-Structured Hierarchical Memory (TSHM) for long-horizon recall. It achieves 28.9% better semantic correctness and 18.2% lower timing error, with a 1.58x inference speedup.
AI.motivation: Current Video-LLMs struggle with continuous video streams: they cannot autonomously decide when to respond, suffer from severe forgetting over long interactions, and lack real-time responsiveness.
AI.method: Three components: (1) SVeD — single-pass perplexity verification for response timing without silence tokens; (2) SCAM — causal attention masks for incremental video-language alignment; (3) TSHM — tree-structured hierarchical memory for organizing evicted information into event chains for efficient retrieval.
AI.result: 28.9% improvement in semantic correctness, 18.2% reduction in timing error, 1.58x inference speedup via streaming key-value cache.
AI.conclusion: LiveStarPro consistently surpasses existing methods in live streaming video understanding, with strong real-time responsiveness and long-horizon memory. Code and model are open-sourced.

title: A fairness-aware extension of Stochastic Multicriteria Acceptability Analysis for ranking
summary: Fairness has become a central concern in ranking problems involving individuals or social groups, particularly under the Responsible Artificial Intelligence agenda. In Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis, Stochastic Multicriteria Acceptability Analysis (SMAA) provides a robust framework for handling uncertainty and incomplete preference information, but it does not explicitly address fairness in the resulting rankings. This paper proposes SMAA-Fair, a fairness-aware extension of SMAA for ranking problems. The approach reweights the simulated rankings generated by SMAA according to their level of group fairness, so that fairer rankings contribute more strongly to the acceptability indices and central weights vector. The framework is independent of the aggregation model and can incorporate different fairness metrics. In this study, Statistical Parity, normalized discounted Kullback--Leibler divergence (rKL) and normalized discounted cumulative Kullback--Leibler divergence (nDKL) are adopted. Rankings are derived from the fairness-adjusted acceptability matrix using expected ranking and maximum acceptability ranking. We also derive the central weight according to the degree of fairness in the obtained rankings. Numerical experiments with synthetic and real data show that SMAA-Fair improves the representation of protected groups among favourable ranking positions, while preserving robustness to preference uncertainty.

AI.tldr: SMAA-Fair extends Stochastic Multicriteria Acceptability Analysis to explicitly incorporate group fairness by reweighting simulated rankings based on fairness metrics, improving protected group representation while maintaining robustness to preference uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Ranking problems often overlook fairness, and existing SMAA methods do not explicitly address group fairness despite handling uncertainty well. The authors aim to bridge this gap.
AI.method: The authors propose SMAA-Fair, which reweights simulated rankings from SMAA according to group fairness levels using metrics like Statistical Parity, rKL, and nDKL. Rankings are derived from fairness-adjusted acceptability matrices via expected or maximum acceptability ranking.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on synthetic and real data demonstrate that SMAA-Fair improves representation of protected groups in top ranking positions while preserving robustness to preference uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: SMAA-Fair effectively integrates fairness into stochastic ranking analysis without sacrificing robustness, offering a flexible framework compatible with various fairness metrics and aggregation models.

title: EvolveNav: Proactive Preflection and Self-Evolving Memory for Zero-Shot Object Goal Navigation
summary: Zero-Shot Object-Goal Navigation (ZS-OGN) requires embodied agents to explore and locate target objects without any prior training. To this end, recent methods leverage foundation models. But they typically rely on static priors and lack adaptation, which leads to repeated errors and costly trial and error. In this paper, we propose a self-evolving ZS-OGN framework that enables continuous test-time improvement. Specifically, we build an agentic rule memory by extracting actionable knowledge from past trajectories. Then, we propose a retrieval strategy based on upper confidence bound, selecting effective rules by balancing semantic relevance and historical success. In addition, we introduce a memory-guided preflection module that forecasts potential outcomes before action, reducing inefficient exploration. Extensive experiments show that our method outperforms existing zero-shot baselines, achieving a 10.1\% improvement in success rate with fewer unnecessary steps.

AI.tldr: A self-evolving ZS-OGN framework that improves test-time performance via adaptive rule memory, upper confidence bound retrieval, and memory-guided preflection, achieving 10.1% better success rate.
AI.motivation: Existing ZS-OGN methods rely on static priors without adaptation, causing repeated errors and inefficient trial-and-error during test time.
AI.method: Builds an agentic rule memory from past trajectories; retrieves rules via upper confidence bound balancing semantic relevance and historical success; uses memory-guided preflection to forecast outcomes before action.
AI.result: Outperforms existing zero-shot baselines with 10.1% improvement in success rate and fewer unnecessary steps.
AI.conclusion: Self-evolving test-time adaptation is an effective paradigm for zero-shot object-goal navigation without retraining.

title: MaineCoon: Pursuing A Real-Time Audio-Visual Social World Model
summary: As an increasing majority of global video content is consumed on social platforms for interactive social purposes, video generation models built for social worlds are important but largely overlooked by previous studies. In this work, we define the position of social world models and build a prototype model as the first step towards this goal. While previous world models successfully simulate physical environments or gaming world exploration, they remain fundamentally detached from human-centric social dynamics. To bridge this gap as the first step to social world models, we present MaineCoon, the first real-time audio-visual autoregressive model that has 22B parameters and is capable of real-time streaming generation and sub-second interaction, with a record-breaking frame rate of up to 47.5 FPS, on a single GPU. To the best of our knowledge, MaineCoon is also the first real-time audio-visual generation model specifically optimized for social-interactive applications. To enable efficient and stable training, we introduce several novel techniques into MaineCoon, including self-resampling, cross-modal representation alignment, domain-aware preference optimization, and reinforced online-policy distillation (ROPD). We also design the first agentic streaming inference framework that supports thousand-second-scale or even longer generation while mitigating drift with agentic cache management and prompt planing. These innovations significantly accelerate training while optimizing real-time inference performance. We believe this work not only sets a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance benchmark for high-quality, low-latency, and long-horizon audio-visual autoregressive models, but also points out the paradigm shift desired for next-generation AI-native social platforms.

AI.tldr: MaineCoon is the first real-time audio-visual autoregressive model (22B parameters, up to 47.5 FPS on a single GPU) designed for social-interactive video generation, introducing novel training techniques and an agentic streaming inference framework to enable sub-second interaction for social world applications.
AI.motivation: Previous world models focus on physical environments or gaming, ignoring human-centric social dynamics. As most video content is consumed on social platforms for interactive purposes, a video generation model built specifically for social worlds is needed but has been overlooked.
AI.method: MaineCoon is a 22B-parameter real-time audio-visual autoregressive model. Novel techniques include self-resampling, cross-modal representation alignment, domain-aware preference optimization, and reinforced online-policy distillation (ROPD). It also uses an agentic streaming inference framework with cache management and prompt planning for long-horizon generation.
AI.result: Achieves record-breaking 47.5 FPS on a single GPU, enabling real-time streaming generation and sub-second interaction. Sets a new SOTA benchmark for high-quality, low-latency, long-horizon audio-visual autoregressive models.
AI.conclusion: This work not only sets a new performance benchmark but also points toward a paradigm shift for next-generation AI-native social platforms, serving as the first step toward social world models.

title: Blind Recovery of Latent Domains via Unsupervised Symmetry Discovery
summary: Primary motivation in blind inverse problems is to recover signals of interest from corrupted observations without knowing the obfuscating mechanism. Blind deconvolution is a prominent approach when the corruption is convolutional, but it is not applicable when general linear transformations obfuscate the domain structure. In this work, we propose an unsupervised framework for recovering latent domains and signals by discovering symmetries of the data distribution. Our framework models observations as linear measurements of signals sampled from a latent random field, and optimizes a shallow group-convolutional network by imposing stationarity and locality regularization at the model output. The model learns a latent symmetry action and an appropriate filter, thereby mapping unstructured observations to a symmetry-based representation that reveals latent signals. Experiments on stochastic processes, Ising models, shuffled and bit-scrambled images, and neural recordings show that the method recovers latent domains and signals from unstructured observations, suggesting symmetry discovery as a new direction for unsupervised structure learning and blind inverse problems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an unsupervised framework that uses symmetry discovery to recover latent domains and signals from corrupted observations in blind inverse problems, where the corruption mechanism is unknown and not necessarily convolutional.
AI.motivation: To solve blind inverse problems where observations are corrupted by unknown general linear transformations (not just convolutional), which existing blind deconvolution methods cannot handle.
AI.method: An unsupervised framework that models observations as linear measurements from a latent random field, optimized via a shallow group-convolutional network with stationarity and locality regularization to discover symmetry actions and appropriate filters.
AI.result: The method successfully recovers latent domains and signals from unstructured observations across stochastic processes, Ising models, shuffled/bit-scrambled images, and neural recordings.
AI.conclusion: Symmetry discovery offers a new direction for unsupervised structure learning and solving blind inverse problems beyond traditional deconvolution approaches.

title: Million-scale multimodal pollen microscopy with expert-guided foundation models
comment: 31 pages, 5 main figures, supplementary information included. Submitted to Scientific Reports
summary: Automated pollen identification from microscopy remains a bottleneck in aerobiology, palaeoecology and biodiversity monitoring, because scalable systems must generalise across specimen preparation, scanner settings and geographic origins while retaining palynological interpretability. To address this gap, we present a million-scale multimodal pollen microscopy resource, Pollen AI Atlas, assembled from pure-species whole-slide bright-field images spanning four geographic origins, four scanner settings and 46 taxon labels across 31 botanical families. Seeded by one manually selected exemplar per source slide, token-level mining and filtering produced 1,511,390 released grain detections with 99.6\% proposal precision in expert-curated test regions. Each detection was paired with machine-generated grain-level morphological captions from five open-weight vision-language models, guided by expert-verified palynological anchors, yielding structured descriptions of aperture systems, wall ornamentation, shape and size. Among the evaluated models, Gemma4 provided the most controlled primary caption set, combining tight length control, no leakage and the strongest text-retrieval performance. Baseline benchmarks with frozen visual features reached 88.16\% top-1 accuracy, while cross-regional retrieval showed that caption-derived text embeddings remained robust when image similarity degraded (mAP@20 0.811 versus 0.262). Released data, annotations, captions, splits, code, and weights provide a benchmark for pollen recognition, cross-regional domain adaptation and domain-specific multimodal microscopy learning.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Pollen AI Atlas, a million-scale multimodal pollen microscopy dataset with 1.5M grain detections, paired machine-generated morphological captions, and benchmarks showing 88.16% top-1 accuracy with strong cross-regional robustness, addressing generalization gaps in automated pollen identification.
AI.motivation: Automated pollen identification from microscopy lacks scalable systems that generalize across varying specimen preparation, scanner settings, and geographic origins while maintaining palynological interpretability, creating a bottleneck in aerobiology, palaeoecology, and biodiversity monitoring.
AI.method: The authors built a million-scale multimodal pollen resource (Pollen AI Atlas) from pure-species whole-slide bright-field images across 4 geographic origins, 4 scanner settings, and 46 taxon labels. They used token-level mining and filtering seeded by one manually selected exemplar per slide, producing 1.5M grain detections with 99.6% proposal precision. Each detection was paired with machine-generated captions from five open-weight vision-language models guided by expert-verified palynological anchors.
AI.result: Baseline benchmarks with frozen visual features achieved 88.16% top-1 accuracy. Cross-regional retrieval showed caption-derived text embeddings remained robust when image similarity degraded (mAP@20 0.811 vs 0.262). Gemma4 provided the best primary caption set with tight length control, no leakage, and strongest text-retrieval performance.
AI.conclusion: The released data, annotations, captions, splits, code, and weights provide a benchmark for pollen recognition, cross-regional domain adaptation, and domain-specific multimodal microscopy learning, addressing key generalization and interpretability challenges in automated palynology.

title: Continual Self-Improvement with Lightweight Experiential Latent Memories
summary: Large language models achieve strong reasoning performance by scaling inference-time compute, yet remain fundamentally stateless, discarding the rich, self-produced reasoning traces generated during this process. We investigate whether models can instead learn online from this experience, converting transient computation (reasoning traces) into persistent reusable knowledge, and without external supervision or access to future data. We show that In-Context Learning (ICL) over raw reasoning traces fails to generalize, reflecting a fundamental limitation of token-level reuse: individual traces lack the abstraction needed for transfer, even after refinement (e.g. self-reflection). In contrast, drawing inspiration from recent works on unsupervised reinforcement learning, we find that lightweight per-instance training with self-generated test-time signals (majority voting) as rewards yields substantial gains, often surpassing full-dataset offline training, motivating a shift from raw traces to learned latent representations. Building on this insight, we propose an online method that distills inference-time compute spent on encountered problems into compact modular latent memories capturing the underlying reasoning structure. These memories are stored and retrieved for future inputs, enabling continual improvement while avoiding catastrophic forgetting through modular design. Importantly, our method is highly efficient, parametrized as extremely lightweight soft prompt memories (~0.001% of model parameters) and trained with only a few gradient steps, yet achieving performance competitive with full parametric updates and offline training. Across challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks, our approach significantly outperforms zero-shot and raw data ICL baselines, while transferring effectively across datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an online method that distills self-generated reasoning traces from LLMs into compact, lightweight soft-prompt memories via unsupervised learning, enabling continual improvement without external supervision, and outperforming zero-shot and raw ICL baselines on math reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs are stateless and discard rich self-produced reasoning traces from inference-time computation; the authors ask whether models can learn online from this experience to convert transient reasoning into persistent, reusable knowledge without external supervision or future data access.
AI.method: The method uses per-instance training with self-generated test-time signals (majority voting) as rewards, inspired by unsupervised RL. It distills inference-time compute into compact modular soft-prompt memories (~0.001% of model parameters) via only a few gradient steps, enabling storage and retrieval for future inputs while avoiding catastrophic forgetting through modular design.
AI.result: The approach surpasses full-dataset offline training in some settings, significantly outperforms zero-shot and raw ICL baselines, and transfers effectively across challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The findings motivate a shift from raw reasoning traces to learned latent representations, showing that lightweight online distillation of self-generated experience is a highly efficient and effective paradigm for continual improvement in LLMs.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Atlas-Based 3D Asset Segmentation for Interactive Content Workflows
summary: Segmenting 3D assets into meaningful regions remains challenging, especially when segmentation criteria are application-dependent and require user control. We present a human-in-the-loop pipeline for generating a segmented 2D parameterized atlas from a 3D model for interactive media, game, and XR content workflows. Our method first selects a compact set of rendered views using a greedy set cover strategy over sampled surface points, and then supports interactive segmentation of these views with SAM~2 and Label Studio. The resulting masks are back-projected onto the model's UV parameterization to produce a unified segmented atlas that supports downstream production tasks such as segment-wise material assignment, style transfer, and semantic labeling. We assess the pipeline through a demonstration-based technical evaluation on eight cultural heritage objects. The results show that the approach can generate usable segmented atlases across diverse geometries while revealing recurring sources of manual correction, particularly fine structures, cavities, and weak appearance boundaries.

AI.tldr: A human-in-the-loop pipeline that segments 3D models into meaningful regions by selecting optimal rendered views, using SAM 2 for interactive segmentation, and back-projecting masks onto a UV atlas for downstream production tasks.
AI.motivation: Segmenting 3D assets into meaningful regions is challenging because segmentation criteria are application-dependent and require user control, which existing automatic methods lack.
AI.method: 1) Greedy set cover strategy over sampled surface points to select compact rendered views. 2) Interactive segmentation of these views using SAM 2 and Label Studio. 3) Back-projection of masks onto UV parameterization to produce a unified segmented atlas.
AI.result: Demonstrated on eight cultural heritage objects; generates usable segmented atlases across diverse geometries, but reveals recurring manual correction needs for fine structures, cavities, and weak appearance boundaries.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline effectively supports segment-wise material assignment, style transfer, and semantic labeling for interactive media, game, and XR workflows, though certain geometry/appearance features still require user intervention.

title: QueryMarket: Cost-Aware Online Active Learning in Data Markets
comment: 10 pages, 8 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
summary: Data acquisition is a major bottleneck for learning in real-time streams: analysts must decide on the fly which labels to purchase while respecting a rolling budget. However, existing online active learning rarely unifies pricing, information gain, and rolling budget constraints under concept drift. We introduce QueryMarket, a market-inspired framework that queries each incoming data point based on its estimated utility to the model and its price. Within this framework, we propose OVBAL (online variance-based active learning), which integrates data pricing with information-driven selection by estimating each sample's marginal utility via a D-optimality criterion with exponential forgetting and executing cost-aware purchases under rolling budget constraints. OVBAL yields a simple, fully online decision rule that adapts to nonstationary streams and heterogeneous label costs. Experiments on synthetic data and a real-world solar power generation forecasting task show that OVBAL is particularly effective under seller-centric pricing and yields a more favorable long-run error-cost trade-off in the real-world task under both pricing schemes.
